text: You cannot touch the pulse of this sick [Italian Theatre] without questioning those who have shown that they know with dignity and conscience to exercise this troubled art of ours, that will never rest, that will never touch a goal, because as soon as one has reached, another arises different from conquering. So, for us, our life will always be a poor thing even if to the other splendid pairs. A life, that if it is made of so many lives and gives the joy and torment to live them all, tomorrow then it is nothing.

I was fourteen years old when I introduced myself to the secretariat of the Academy of the Teatro dei Filodrammatici, to be admitted to the diction and acting courses of our school in Milan. But I had to go back, disappointed and sad, after a kind but firm denial having not yet reached the age prescribed by the regulations for admission (sixteen years). The following year, as if I had already reached that age, with a firmer face, I still knocked on that door; and, in the face of such a good and passionate will, here is the first tear from regulations, dates, principles, customs. [...]

[The Academy] was like a second and truer home of mine [...] Ofelia Mazzoni was lending with an enchantment, I would say authoritarian, her teaching; [...] The joy of my first conquest was her judgment [...]. Here then in the second year, replace her, Enrico Reinach: good, fatherly, noble. He could find in me the two veins: the dramatic vein and the comedy vein; therefore, I was among his students the only one to whom each essay was entrusted two scenes, one dramatic and one comic.

Among the memories of the Academy I cannot leave the greatest: the venture of having had among the spectators, to one of the essays, Eleonora Duse. I didn't even have the good of seeing her face. [...] I had the sense of his physical presence only when Enrico Reinach, at the end of the essay, told me radiantly that he had received from her the warmest congratulations for his pupil.

His two - year teaching [by Henry Reinach] had grasped our knowledge more closely, devoted on my part and friendly also to my relatives on his part. Imagine, then, my surprise when I learned that my parents had not been informed of my entry into art. A flame of anger went up to my forehead; it seemed to me a betrayal. But he, with his old experience as a comedian, caressing my cheek with his beautiful hand jeweled to the little finger: "Dear, the theater, it is better to see him from the armchair." He already knew me engaged, and he was more grateful to see me in the care of a house than in the uncertainty and instability of a stage. [...] The family tightened more and more the brakes, prohibition to participate in any play [...] I was offered then also the possibility to enter the Italian cinematography, [...] but I didn't dare even mention it to my parents, although I felt attracted to this new and prestigious form of art. I looked forward to becoming an adult to declare my aspiration. But the moment came also before [...] with the interest [...] by Sabatino Lopez, who then presided at the performances of the Teatro del Popolo in Milan. He convinced my parents with his authority that being a stable theatre based in the same city, I could act as an actress and not stray from home.

Ettore Paladini, director of the Company, [...] looked at us with good eyes and peers [...], and hard but illuminated in the face [...] by a light of intelligent goodness. My soul tightened to its discipline. Discipline of soldier, just, in love with his work. [...] Virgilio Talli I immediately understood him: exuberant in spirit for his actors, [...] exuberant in love for the theater. I wasn't afraid of his screaming and his fury. [...] And he was an incomparable Master for me. The care of detail, although very minute, was never in him at the expense of the general idea of work.

Added by Marco Praga as one of the few mature actresses for a place of responsibility and prominence, I was predestined to tie my youth [...] trained by the two major masters of the prose scene in Italy, [...] to the most beautiful and sublime undertaking that a very young and selfless spirit had to give to our country and to us actors with its high and human participation. While Duse, around the world, blessed this undertaking, Luigi Pirandello, interested in Marco Praga's criticism, wrote to Talli for me. Following Talli's reply, here is a poster from Pirandello in Milan with the precise task of writing me first actress. Our first understanding was like this: without seeing or speaking to us.

I arrived in Rome with my mother and immediately ran to the Odescalchi Theatre to take my place. The Company was already reunited for some time; I [...] came last. A bald man with a small pointed beard, in the darkness of the stage, the pale and furrowed face, had leaped up with a portentous agility, in contrast to his appearance, as soon as someone said my name. And a warm and vigorous hand shook me over and over again, with that friendliness that is proper only to men who know how to give and do good. [...] Curiosity, interest, envy, struggles had been on since the beginning; struggles, I mean, by those who have always been opposed to every noble and disinterested art venture in the world of theatre. The wait for my presentation, exacerbated by the delay, was very lively. As with any major enterprise in the early days, the first settlement was difficult. [...] It was a triumph. A triumph for Bontempelli, for Luigi Pirandello, for the Theatre and for me. I cried for consolation and bliss. Never again, afterward, did I have such a full and pure joy.

Alone, helped by my father's watchful and constant love, I took the reins of the first Company Marta Abba in 1929, all of young people led and directed by me.  [...] I followed to carry around my major interpretations of the Pirandelli repertoire. [...] wise men and experts of the theatre [...] catalogued my repertoire as a brain and only artistic (i.e. anti-commercial). And the voice spread to the public. Cerebral Marta Abba is great only in piradellian art. [...] And so the year after a second Company of my formation, with a repertoire that was increasingly increasing of new interpretations, among which I like to remember above all that of Madame Legros, Penelope of Maugham, The Good Fairy of Molnar, Our companion of Antoine, and Anna Kapina taken from the novel of Tolstoi and the Cricket of the hearth taken from the novel of Dickens.

Returning to our homeland after many triumphs, the bitterest disappointment awaited us: we no longer knew where to go. For the debts incurred due to the reconstruction of the Odescalchi Theatre that had to be paid by the State and for the delays in payment, the Theatre had to abandon itself to the concessionary of Prince Odescalchi. [...] The war began from the day that the will and passion of eleven young people founded a stable theatre in Rome. In fact, the Odescalchi Theatre was also called "Theatre of the Eleven," and they put Luigi Pirandello at the head of it; the struggle was covered and insidious by a society that hijacked the entire monopoly of theatre in Italy: theatres, repertoire, Compagnie. In the face of this war I [...] remained to fight alongside Luigi Pirandello, then always exposed to all the vendetta of the opponents, feeling that the cause for which he fought was holy, and all in favor of us actors for the dignity of our profession and for the honor of the Italian Theatre. From the contracts that the companies did with the Compagnia del Teatro d'Arte of Rome, directed by Luigi Pirandello, it was demanded that next to the name of the Master figure as a diction of writing, unique and only, mine; [...] A Company that in all the nations of Europe had been welcomed with all the honors of the greatest theaters, was condemned to shoot almost only in the province [...] Only hope of salvation the South America. It had been promised to the American contractor that our Company would be alone in that year, 1926, for Argentina.

[In Paris] The trials [of the Pirandellian Man, the beast and the virtue] had to begin after a few days, only comforted me that they would last at least a month. But I should not forget that the character was everything to be recreated in another language and with other accents and other ways. I put myself, suffering, and with long stops in bed, to the study of the part. I felt the effects that I had on my only viewer, who was a French lady from Compagnia; but it was necessary to face the rehearsals in the theater. The outcome was higher than all my expectations.

While I was waiting for rehearsals, it was also necessary to think about dressing this character. [...] I had neither time nor way to address Italian tailors from Paris. [...] And here I am with my good script in the hand of a Parisian tailor. When this read the work I saw him standing before me between mortified and despondent. What figure could he make us dress a character that the author himself described as clumsily disguised as virtue? He didn't see how his work could be necessary. I explained to him that it was very necessary. The more difficult it may be, the less it was to make one of the usual elegant lounge dresses. It was necessary to create new forms with a new to appropriate style. [...] And so it happened. To the representation also the dresses as an integral part of the creation of the characters got the most successful, so much so that the newspaper "Excelsior" dedicated an entire page to comment on this new interpretative complement. The shape of my flat-tied hat with rigid pudica is read on the eyes, the sinuous tail of the hermetic black dress of the second act and many other found had obtained the approval of all the critics and the flower of the audience with come to the general test.

The performances followed for three months, and the most refined performance for taste and quality was undoubtedly ours, the Italian one, during that period of the best Parisian theater season. I could easily have taken advantage of this Parisian success [...], but I made my trunks in haste and fury. It was an almost physical need to go back to Italy. After this really difficult victory obtained abroad, I did not ask anyone for recognition of it, I only wanted to have a compensation asking to be received by our Duce. [...] I would have told him that, since I was a child, I was trained at that school of courage and courage and faith, of which Benito Mussolini set an example in our streets and squares in Milan [...] I was received in that solemn hall of Piazza Venezia. [...] He was an exquisite guest: he was interested in the events of the French theater, he also asked me about small things, like the pay I felt in Paris, and wanted to know when I would start reciting in Italy. [...] I preferred to turn all my attention and care to the formation of a beautiful Company for the coming comic year. And resumed from Naples [...] I then left for Palermo [...] [and] for some extraordinary performances in Sardinia. [...] But I longed for Rome, and once again I was rejected by those same people who were always opposed to me. [...] But all these oppositions I wanted to overcome, even for the desire that enlivened me to approach more and more the warm breath of the masses. I was rewarded.

How much better party could have been drawn from me, from my art, from my figure, from my voice, from all my stage play, was seen in my second film, Teresa Confalonieri, which had a resounding success throughout Italy and which was consecrated by the first prize at the Venice International Film Festival. [...] The miracle took place on a night of magnificent full moon, after a week of raining rains; and it was like a dream for me, that the success if I had dreamed could not be greater and more beautiful.

What I've said so far for the theater I could start to say for cinematography too. Up to a certain point seeing me constantly kept out of every experiment attempt of my figure on the screen, I myself asked myself: "But maybe in the movie I won't be okay? It's okay, it's okay, it's okay, it's okay, it's okay, it's okay, it's okay, it's okay, it's okay, it's okay, it's okay, it's okay, it's okay, it's okay, it's okay, it's okay, it's okay, it's okay, it's okay, it's okay, it's okay, it's okay, it's okay, it's okay, it's okay. I knew that in all other countries after the advent of the film, most actresses and actors in the theatre of prose had been called to interpret the new subjects. [...] the doubt about my movie possibilities, gradually began to take root in me so much, that only as an essay or trial to myself, I allowed myself to accept a side part in a film of German origin of which we wanted to make the Italian reproduction. That's when someone else's distrust had been reduced to me.

The most authoritative critics noticed which purchase had made Italian cinematography with me, that is with an artist who wanted nothing and nothing to resemble the heroines of films from overseas, with gestures, ways, and portamento and personal characters of his own; such as to give a typical imprint to Italian production. To whom did the Italian critics say these things? Who listened to them? [...] Martha Abba after that success and after the consecration of criticism was never called by anyone.

This is, in summary and in summary, the story of my artistic career so far. The conclusion that logically and on the basis of the facts is to be drawn from this is unfortunately not to be welcomed. Not so much for me that [...] at the bottom I was always able to impose myself [...] as for the conditions of dramatic art in Italy.

I've been living with my secret for about ten years, and I think if I had given away all the food I chewed and then vomited, I could have saved at least one colony of malnourished children. [...] What am I saying? I think I saved the malnourished children from this drug that gives a shit about smiling, pretending not to be the same as the others.

I didn't drink enough and I ate solid foods so how could I think I could put it back, they're physical laws! [...] Any bulimic in the world must know the right amount of liquid according to what it chooses to swallow and for the more refined also what to drink... sometimes the water is not enough, better the milk.

I'm swollen, the skin pulls, the face is deformed. [...] I have a body that varies in weight day after day, excess liquids shake the water already existing in the thighs especially, that's where you see most. I'm all face and thighs. The face of those like me is recognized by traits. The part of the jaw widens, the periocular zone becomes as high as the cheekbones, a sign that the liver is blaspheming to make me stop. I have huge glands under my neck, they deface the profile, making it lose its freshness, I'm already old. The thighs instead, take on a colorful post exposure to the sun of the 14 without protection, tingle and move from soil ill-willed. I talk to them, I try to motivate them [...] but they don't answer. They have Niagara Falls under my thighs, and they seem to be deaf as well. There are no symptoms for certain visions.

[The daughter] works on the interior, I just trust. [...] He fixed things, took space, lit the darkness, warmed the area that now spits out only what must not stay inside. [...] Piano, pianoissimo, reshapes the traits, with the feet stop the black holes and does not give up the grip. It's so strong. For nine months, he won't stop building me. [...] works on the destroyed soul, restructures the foundations and what I ask, because they serve just materials this time. What I eat stays there and turns into healthy pieces of me.

I look pregnant in the seventh month and I'm not sorry at all, it takes me into a cathartic state that requires me to continue playing the happy pregnant part. I cleanse myself of guilt and dream that all that food equal to a whole shelf of an old food is my first son and that waiting for me outside the door there is all the love of the world. I sit and rise feeling the pressure of my belly, pretending to feel someone inside and for a moment gives me the fullness that I just can't feel alone.

I'm going back to the house and I'm crying. Yeah, I feel really lost this time. I didn't want it, it happened that I didn't even notice it, and I don't think that's a good sign. I open the water in the bathroom and vomit crying. The sour liquid that comes out of my stomach mixes with the sea from the eyes and just like him is infinite. It's all flowing. The water of the earth, the water of the stomach, the water from mine, the eyes... the water from my wounds now without blood. Lose liquids, unfortunately. I always empty but never fill; yet to me it seems to lose love even from the eyes. I think I'll be alone forever. You and me.

I have everything I need to feel loved in my car, and even if it only lasts a few hours, when I feel all this adrenaline, I feel like I'm having a great day. [...] nothing went wrong, even this time the cashier didn't notice or if it did whatever, so much I can change again and again. I'm free to shoot all the supermarkets in the area, I already have a map of those to jump for this month and where to review next month. [...] Everything I bought on the bed, I go to the kitchen and put the water to boil for safety, I open a can of tuna as I begin to bite the ice cream cookie that I ate as a child.

He keeps calling me a guy, he's fixed himself up, and he's decided to stand up to everything that my misconception combines [...]. I'm distracted, I just need the thought of having it, and I absolutely don't want to own it. I don't want him to think about me, I don't want him to feel what he says he feels, but instead he insists on wanting to love me without realizing that I will always choose two fingers in my throat to be with anyone.

I have a nice smile lately, it doesn't taste acidic and doesn't create bitter taste in the mouth and elsewhere. I'm going to work on the radio. Ever since I've been here, I feel more solid, the vehicle can hide from me and it doesn't force me towards what makes me uncomfortable. To be more precise, the package is delineated.

At eleven o'clock in the morning, sitting on my parents' bedside dad, as I was wandering through the channels of the biggest television in the house, she arrived. I couldn't believe that something like this could draw so much attention to everything I had inside, it was basically just the scene of a movie that despite telling me who I really was, the title I never discovered and never looked for.

In secret the belly still grumbles but the music of us kickboxing athletes overlooks it. The day of the yellow belt examination comes, the first to be precise, I am proud to have reached this goal. I walk into the locker room, find a piece of floor where I put my things and prepare myself together with all the other athletes. Exciting atmosphere.

I have to have a face today. The job is for you. In these cases I guess I'm Meryl Streep, as you will try to seem languorous, kind, deep, confused but resolved. [...] I come to the smiling and relaxed work, I get red if someone gives me a compliment; I speak with a thread of voice as in those films of author where the best to say the jokes is what least makes me understand what he is saying. I'm sitting in my dressing room and waiting.

Today I start fasting so I can go back out, see my friends and dress up with other pants from the ones I wear. Having a closet over the years has been quite complex, I couldn't find a comfortable dress that would cover this huge discomfort that I have towards being in the world. For at least five years I've been walking around with these low waist pants, of technical fabric, a little paws that make me feel "normal" because at the bottom that's what I want. In order not to miss this feeling I wash them in the evening and wait for them to be at least damp in the morning to wear them again. I'm bulimic about my pants, too.

I'm very depressed, my home is two hours ago, The chef's proof smiles food and happiness but I don't like anything anymore. Everything is so shabby and gray when love ends. This, love. "You must accept it, it is so, you cannot change it!" This phrase I have said a thousand times addressed to anyone, even to food, or rather to vomiting, or even better to my practice. Only to myself did I never repeat it.

I don't know how many cigarettes I smoke a day anymore. My bathroom is a museum of vanities: I collect beauty products. I have at least six different types of shampoos, or maybe eight. Of conditioner I only use the Khiel's, but I have two different types... And also a cream that you don't have to rinse that costs $30, gel more and less wet effect, and gel more and less fixating. Scented oils for hair, creams for the body, cream legs after-shave, cream for the hands with glycerin, creams for the face, for the night and super moisturizing, powerful eye drops and natural eye drops, almond scrub, bubble bath with goat's milk, masks with chamomile and rosemary, and vitamins, all timerelease naturally, at least five lipsticks tending to the red firefighter, foundation Lobby Brown, foundation Mac, foundation Screen Face, four different types of mascara Maybelline... I am afraid of the bathroom. The mirror. I'm stuck staring at myself, and I can't recognize myself.

My survival became a matter of pride. I swore to myself that I would once again prove to the world what I was made of, what kind of fiber my body and soul formed. I increased my visits with my psychologist who has been following me for 16 years now and to whom I owe so much, among other things also the writing of this book. She convinced me to do it. Thank you Anna Rita

I never had the claim to be anyone's paladin, let alone the example, but one thing I feel like telling those who will read this book: being a woman like me has never been comfortable, but it was almost always a choice. I have had few beacons in my life, I have always sailed on sight and it was not always easy to proceed against everything and against everyone. But believe me, every time my total absence of conformism annoyed someone, I knew I was in the right direction.

Unfortunately, when the lights really turned on, mine didn't appear. This is just one of a long series of missed promises. At the award ceremony of the Golden Globe, I was ill. Best actress for the film Zoo, written and directed by Cristina Comencini. I was 12 years old and had just won the Golden Globe as best actress and my parents hadn't shown up for the award.

I chose to lose my virginity on the afternoon of April, I was fourteen years old. I liked a type of 18, Gianluca, who already lived alone. It was full of money. The intercoms and I said, "I ran away from home, can I sleep with you?" Gianluca agreed. A friend of mine had given me that day a white rabbit that I had baptized Syd (like Syd Barrett), and I was brought by Gianluca on the scooter inside a cage.

My mother could make me hate every meal, I still remember perfectly the icy feeling of the fork stuck in my tonsils and I forced to swallow chicory, boiled brain, liver. He said they were good for me, but at night I couldn't resist and vomit everything on the heavenly tiles of the only bathroom in the house. My dad hated eating, too. He's always been skinny. Anna, as a teenager, became anorexic, and bulimic flower. I mean, we all had a complicated relationship with food and ourselves. No wonder, eating disorders are always connected to some kind of discomfort.

Federico was my first true love and with him I reached orgasm, which had never happened to me and which consecrated him as the first real love. I was 16. [...] Federico remained at my side at every moment during the shooting of Placido's film. He was proud of me. When the film came out, he went to see him alone and told me that at the end of the screening he was moved and stood up to beat his hands and that the rest of the audience in the room was forced to imitate him. A few months later, my father called me to make a film with him in America. Trauma was the first time my dad and I worked together and I had a fucking fear of letting him down. It was terrible to have to separate myself from Frederick for months.

All of her was witchcraft, violence and enchantment. I remember few moments of tenderness between us, but those I remember create such fascination in me, as if my mother were a mythical and ethereal character, elusive. Like when he took a mirror: it was night, and he told me to pay attention, he was going to do a magic. In the mirror he made the moon reflect and the moon reflected on his face, making my mother herself look like the moon.

Even my parents can't be called a model, what maybe it would have been for me and my sisters to turn us into real people. They have always been very physically absent, yet such a bulky presence... Even when they were still together Dad was almost never there, he was always around shooting his movies and, if there was, he was locked in the room writing or he went straight to the hotel to concentrate better.

Angelica and I were inseparable and with her I put a notch on a long series of first times, The first time we bought cigarettes we lied to the tobacconist by giving us a pack of the lightest, explaining that our mother smoked those. Then we went to the Vanni bar, an elegant café frequented by actors and television celebrities, and locked ourselves in the bathroom. With calm and commitment we finished the package, smoking one after the other. Eventually, we both threw up.

I remember the time, attention and feelings dedicated to the family. And then also the serenity of being finally engaged in normal activities, the picnics in the park, the pediatric visits, the pilates, the birthday parties of my children's classmates, the dinners on Sunday, some dj sets around the world, hotel rooms all the same, jokes of scripts so useless that I could not memorize them, dozens of actors crossed and immediately forgotten, some written and never realized scripts, a radio broadcast where I put the music I thought, dozens of books read and then removed, travel with the family on beaches, lakes, snowy mountains, some lunch out with Angelica... In short, those were the years of the flat calm to which I was not aware and therefore perhaps I do not remember them. The bad things of those years instead I do remember them, but I don't want to write about them, I don't want to put fingers in sores that are finally healing.

Hannah's anorexia [Ceroli] got worse, it was imaginable. When he organized the feast for his eighteen years, both I and Flower [Silver] went. There were a lot of guys, all dressed up. I was fifteen years old and I still remember Anna's lean arms shaking like wicker sticks as she danced fashionable songs of the time. The vision of those thin arms, almost transparent, like the wings of a little bird that wobbles under the sun, still faces my heart. She must have weighed 38 kilos. I felt guilty of her mental illness because I had abandoned her a year earlier, when I had moved to my father, to escape my mother's beatings, her senseless aggression against me and against my slender little body.

Even though my mom always told me not to be a victim, I felt like a victim to all intents and purposes. I always felt this sense of injustice, the same as I felt when Flower [Silver] showed me her pink bedroom. It was a fact, Mother had never raised her hands on Anna [Ceroli], Flower had never been abandoned by Dad, and now she also had the cat. Anna also suffered for her father's departure. She loved my father because her father had always screwed her. Even today I feel sorry for Anna, without peace, neglected and abandoned by all the men of her life.

It began a long phase of wandering during which I was acting as a shuttle between Mom and Dad, until one of them threw me out because I answered him badly or refused to carry out an order, or simply because I was not the favorite daughter of either one or the other, but only the reflection of a distant love that had devoured them to the bone.

I've been sharing my life with these thoughts for years now. At first they told me it was depression, and I tried to treat it with medicine. I still take four different pills before I go to bed today. Sleeping, in fact, is not the right word, it would be better to try to sleep. I've had insomnia since I was a child. I remember once when I was under the influence of some terrible drugs that had been prescribed to me, a journalist told me: "What a deep look you have, it seems that you are looking at me in the soul." And instead, I was just anesthetized, think of you. But these treatments never worked with me, because if I didn't feel anything calmed me, on the other hand that total apathy distorted me. A woman like me who walks around skinless, with nerves, muscles and tendons uncovered, preying on bad weather, preying on everything, first of all on myself, bleeding wound, how can she not feel anything anymore? Dress up dead skin? No way. And where I tried to put a piece of drugs in it, all I did was spread the crack.

After the separation of my parents grew in me the sense that I had been abandoned by my parents, but from this abandonment also gave rise to my great freedom. I began to take advantage of it, to combine every one. As an misunderstood and mortified child, I became independent. I started smoking the first cigarettes in elementary school, once I even made a joint with the nanny, I was nine years old. In the evening I threw myself on the couch and devoured old movies, smoking cigarettes and drinking a glass of water, pretending it was liqueur. I imitate what I saw, I also wanted to be free as my parents, to throw everything at me. After my father had left my mother's home, there was nothing left but a shadow that taught rules, often random, that no one cared to respect. Rules she forgot the next day. The most absurd was just to go around the building with my white bike, because going around the neighborhood was dangerous. But maybe she didn't imagine that it was that danger that attracted me. Anyway, it would have been worse to stay home.

It seems strange to say, but at that moment I did not understand what had just happened to me. In fact, the first feeling I had when I came back into my body was guilt. I thought about the provocative dress I had worn, the invitation I had accepted, the champagne I had been drinking... it was my fault. And then that couldn't be a rape, that wasn't how Franca Rame described it, the black chronicles in the newspapers that talked about unarmed women caught in the dark and beaten streets... no, I hadn't been raped, because I was still wearing the dress... [...] I remember slamming the door like an obsession. My friend and I locked ourselves in the closet fearing that he would knock down the door. Every time I attended a festival, which was Venice, Cannes, Toronto, Dauville, that monster showed up and I had to hide, helped by friends, bodyguards, advertisers... He showed up at every movie, equipped with paparazzi so as to get shot a photo hugged with me. At the time I didn't understand why he did it, I thought he was interested in my work, or obsessed with me, then when I saw the smiling photographs that portrayed him with all the women who had accused him of rape, violence and sexual abuse, I realized that the photo together was an alibi that had been created over the years to defend themselves: "They were all my friends" he would say at the trial. All reports were consenting.

When my children are older I will sell the house and live on the island, because I am not comfortable in the world, but when I am there I feel a huge sense of peace, when I walk through its dirt roads, when I swim in its waters, when I breathe the Mediterranean maquis that smells of myrtle and rosemary, I feel good, I feel life flowing in the veins. I know her better than myself and I love her as much as some people claim to love God. This island is magnetic, in fact, its land is as red as iron and attracts you and envelops you and if you let it go you will enter in to never leave you. This island is my tiny paradise. Here and here alone (it is on the island that I am writing) I feel happy and protected. People have known me here since I was a child, before I was successful and all the bullshit that came with it, nobody judges me here, here comes out my true essence.

At Villa Balestra, I used to hang out with boys older than me who were fighting coke on the tapes. They were so jealous of that substance that they wouldn't offer it to me even if I had asked for it. In any case, I wasn't ready to jump, I just needed the stun of the reeds, which never left me. One day, while in the car of my friend Lollo, on the Lungotevere, a song of an absurd genre passed on the radio that I had never heard before. That was Speedy J's Pull over. It was techno music. It was the first time I heard a techno song, and I was electrocuted. Previously there were songs 19 by Paul Hardcastle and Big Pun of Inner City but this was another story. I felt a great heat in my brain, similar to that caused by the music of the Pink Floyds that I listened obsessively at that time. In fact, the '60s psychedelia was back in fashion at the end of the '80s, but this of techno was a real revolution, indeed an evolution. Lollo explained to me what it was, it was music that came from afar, from Detroit, London, also from Belgium. I closed my eyes and started traveling and my mind began to dance. From that moment something changed, a switch clicked in my head. All my friends went to the raves and that music suggested that there was something beyond, a new world yet to be explored.

I was considered an intellectual actress. For Pinter's Old Times, Luchino told me with his Milanese accent: "You, on stage you will have to be naked! It was not included in the text but I immediately accepted it. She made me dye blonde, including sex. On stage, every night, I took off my white robe and my colleagues, Valentina Cortese and Umberto Orsini, sprinkled me with powder. You don't count the speed in which, later, I stripped myself at the theater, or in the movies. What's great when you play naked is that no one listens to you. You can say anything you want. You can misread the text, you can recite it in an aberrant way. No one pays attention to what you say. When I realized that power over the audience I found the wonderful thing! Luchino had emptied the audience and put a ring in the center of the Argentina theatre in Rome. I was there, in the middle, completely naked and much of the city had come to see me "it was always in the twentieth century " and at that moment I realized that I no longer had any problem with acting. It was all sorted out.

My brother got married in February. Then I suddenly decided to marry myself. June. With Fabio Mauri, a childhood friend of Pier Paolo Pasolini. Pier Paolo was our witness. In a photo, which I have certainly preserved, he wears a very elegant dress, of a shiny and shiny fabric. Fabio, who was an artist, a painter, tried to convince me to leave the theatre. Curiously, despite my great insecurity, I resisted. When I married Fabio, there was a psychological accident in me: I believe mainly because I did not want to be a married woman, a wife. It was a mistake. [...] One cannot escape. You can't really leave yourself. But I didn't take your advice. I flooded the apartment. I opened all the taps. The water went all the way to the ankles. My mother-in-law was having tea with friends. They heard the water go up to the calves. That's when you started thinking that I had to be crazy. It isn't normal to flood your apartment in the presence of your husband's mother. My father had done the same thing years ago, had flooded the entire apartment in Milan, and had also built paper boats. You can't imagine the speed of the rising water... then I ended up getting divorced.

I have no memory, the past, the future, are the same thing. So, I don't think anyone cares about my life. I myself am bored to remember and tell it. I'm like Alberto Moravia who hated the past. He said he hated the past so much that he couldn't even back out in his car. It has to be said that he was a terrible driver. [...] That's why I don't like to tell my memories. I like to forget them.

Luchino Visconti thought he would write me for The White Nights, but the production preferred Maria Schell and he let himself be convinced. She could do two things and she did very well: laughing crying and crying laughing. But it doesn't matter. It was the theater that got me involved. All I had to do was never have an ordinary life again, finally to be able to make the everyday environment disappear, to see no one, to be absent. What did I need movie theater for? Even though the cinema sometimes came to me, it was the theatre that fully satisfied my ambition, that of perfect loneliness.

It is with Luca Ronconi, for the Orlando Furioso, that I went to the United States. I sympathized with Andy Warhol's entourage. The transvestites liked me a lot. Everyone said to me, "We're the same, you're like us. They had brought me in the dressing room a tshirt that I still keep: "Adriana Asti, the Queen of the Gay". A gay journalist from Turin called himself Adriano Asti. I think he was a fan of mine. But no doubt many gays prefer to identify themselves as an actress rather than a housewife. I must say that I also acted in films that could be considered a bit scandalous. I did things that actresses back then wouldn't dare do. I was so happy. In Caligula, a nice Tinto Brass movie, slaves masturbating around me. The liquid that was made in Cinecittà's bar was offered to me so that I could spread it on my naked body. [...] I became a woman. I disguised myself as a whore, what I wasn't. I surprised my family. I was a fine young Milanese girl. I was exactly like transvestites, but I didn't need hormonal treatments. My body was authentic, feminine. And when I met Warhol's actors, I hadn't filmed that kind of movie yet. They recognized me before the time. They covered me with sweet words, joints, gifts, as I recited on my cart. If a transvestite had played my role, he would have done it exactly like me.

Besides, I wasn't pretty enough to make movies. In Italy the cinematographers, in the twentieth century, were really very beautiful. You had to have a nice ass, et cetera. Or, of course, be the wife of a producer. Perhaps I could have made a career in France, where beauty has always counted less, but in Italy a shining and, at the same time, conventional beauty was needed. [...] A producer noticed me at the scene and I was summoned. And when he saw me in reality, he said, "But how?" Is that her? Is that it? Anything else? He had seen me on the stage much higher, more beautiful, very well done. Well, yeah, that's it, it's the effect of the stage! I answered. - Your measurements? - Mm-hmm, mm-hmm, mm-hmm, mm-hmm, mm-hmm, mm-hmm, mm-hmm, mm-hmm, mm-hmm, mm-hmm, mm-hmm, mm-hmm, mm-hmm, mm-hmm, mm-hmm, mm-hmm, mm-hmm, mm-hmm, mm-hmm, mm-hmm, mm-hmm, mm-hmm, mm-hmm, mm-hmm, mm-hmm, mm-hmm, mm-hmm, mm-hmm, mm-hmm, mm-hmm, mm-hmm, mm-hmm, mm-hmm, mm-hmm, mm-hmm, mm-hmm, mm-hmm, mm-hmm, mm-hmm, mm-hmm, mm-hmm, mm-hmm, mm-hmm. mm-hmm, mm-hmm. mm-hmm. mm-hmm. On stage the proportions change. His long blonde hair? A wig. Its forms? Well, here they are. I'm who I am. I can't help it. [...] To be completely honest, the truth is that I did not correspond to anything.

Then they took me to a psychoanalyst, Cesare Musatti. My father accompanied me and, as I was ashamed, I carried books under my arm to make the doorman believe that I was taking classes with the professor. Only after a long time did I admit with him that I was definitely out of place and that, therefore, the only place for me was the theater, a place suitable for me because it did not exist. An imaginary, illusory place. My psychoanalyst was very particular, too. In the end he wrote for me Three men for Amalia, a drama that unfortunately I recited only after his death, It was not an exceptional work but let's say that a fund of truth serpented you. Then I also wrote for him Dear Professor, a comedy that also had some success.

I don't know why I started asking many writers who had never written for the theater to write something for me. I knew poets and writers and it was to them that I preferred to feel close, I liked to talk to them, to listen to them. [...] So I asked Elsa and Natalia Ginzburg: "Why don't you write for the theater? Alberto Moravia wrote his ugly comedy about Beatrice Cenci, you can take it too! Natalia was the only one who listened to me. I wanted his play, no matter which one. But you said you were incapable of writing jokes other than "Piero," where is my hat? I told her to keep going, because that was the perfect first line. She continued. But it made me die at the end of the first act and I protested because I had to be the main character. This is how Natalia wrote her first play. Elsa laughed at us, found that ignoble comedy. He despised us. He hated conventional theater, although it was not the case of Natalia's theater that was far from the usual patterns.

Bernardo, son of the poet Attilio, was still a boy and was assistant to Pier Paolo. The dry commando, Bernardo's first film was written by Pier Paolo. I played before the Revolution, his second movie. And I didn't feel like making a good movie. I didn't know. At the first screening I understood. The things that concern us directly never seem good to us, they seem disgusting to us, don't they? The character looked just too much like me. And Bernardo is not a cynical person. He is directed like a poet, like his father. He realizes what passes through his mind, does not judge, does not censor. The peculiarity of that movie is that it wasn't censored by the superior. A bit like in Pier Paolo's movies. Bernardo perd was the exact opposite of me. Of me, during my childhood, I often heard with carelessness, with a loud voice: "But what a pity that the child is so! From a young age he was understood and valued.

It wasn't the first time I wrote. When some rare reader tells me that he liked my book, I'm very happy. The idea that someone is reading to me while I am now somewhere, intent on who knows what, I really like it. I feel tremendous satisfaction. A satisfaction I didn't know could exist.

I always liked decorating my houses, but in the country I did more. Above almost every window I have painted landscapes that each take back the view that you can admire from that point. In one of these also appears the tree of Judas with pink flowers that Caesar Musatti planted on a Passover day many years ago. In the house there are many things: books, drawings, snakes under spirit... Of snakes, the summer countryside is full: every time I catch one, I keep it in a glass vase, where I can observe on every side the beautifully designed skin.

The theatre is like the desert for the Bedouins or the sea for the sailors: they do not even wonder why they are there, on the sand or on the water. And so they no longer leave the sand or the sea. Even those who play theater can no longer leave the stage. What else do you want to do? Anyway, I've gotten used to it. I got caught in his tables.

I ignore the maternal sense. How could I? Childhood was the worst time of my life: why should I bring forth another unhappy? Would I have been better than my mother or my father? No way. And then it's hard to survive, even if you have good parents. Not to mention I've always been a child. I missed the next step, the one where you become mothers.

Should we start with childhood? Do you have to go in line? It's easier. Because if you want to remember the most recent things, sometimes it's impossible. Nothing is known about the next past, while the remote past is impossible to forget. So, patience. I hope I promise you they want the endless future. And I would like an endless future.

They sent me to school at the German nuns in Boscovich Street, a private institution. [...] My class was mixed and also international: there was the son of a consul, with very dark skin, and at the beginning also two Jewish children who did not come to school on Saturday, but who then disappeared. [...] We studied, read and wrote in German. While terrible things were happening in Germany, the nuns made us copy Hitler's speeches and embroider small swastikas in beautiful handwriting. I was not a brilliant student, the nuns said of me that I was very little mature. In spring, even before the school year ended, I was forced to stop classes to move to the sea.

As I grew older, my mother became more and more fond of me. The attention he once had for my brother, over the years, moved them to me. When I went to see her, she was very old and my father had died for a long time and called me close to her: "Hold on, hold on!" she said to me, and she continually made the gesture of giving me something very precious, but her hand was empty. Like he wanted to pay me back for something that had been taken from me.

I was born in a tumultuous way, in advance of the times, at the Regina Elena clinic where my mother, pregnant at the eighth month, had been rushed after falling. Apparently, someone, perhaps my brother, stumbled her into premature childbirth. [...] The doctor who gave birth to me, Dr. Acerbi, in seeing me exclaimed: "Uh, what a muster of a tusa!" So, at least, I've been told. And that feeling of discomfort, the same that my parents must have felt, I remember. My first years of life are wrapped in vague darkness. I crawled around the house and saw threats everywhere around me. [...] I walked the floors in the certainty that I was in constant danger. No one would speak to me. But sometimes I could hear adults talking about me like I wasn't there. They said: "Too bad the child is so..." And they didn't finish the sentence. I didn't talk much either. I was so shy that at Christmas I had to recite the poems behind a door. I didn't feel any gratification in showing myself. I imagined that I was adopted, that I was not part of that family with which, for dark reasons, I was living.

When I was four, I was molested by a waitress. Her name was Alma, she was Friulian. I was so small: I couldn't understand what was happening to me, but I felt it was wrong. I thought it was my fault for a long time. Then she was sent away. Maybe my parents noticed something, who knows, but no one in the family talked about it. Not even my brother, even though he was part of those episodes. Between us, never a word, as if nothing had happened. After all, while living together, I could hardly see him. Each one had his own room, we only met at mealtime. The memory of that abuse, however, grew with me as an innominable secret. Talking about it was hard, so I made up more trouble. [...] I took painful stories of extreme poverty and made them mine. Some friends of my parents openly called me Miss Piaghetti: a nickname that they had to think completely guessed, since I was constantly complaining.

I was filming the Ferramonti legacy of Mauro Bolognini, when I heard that Pier Paolo Pasolini had been killed. I couldn't believe it. Because for me, Pier Paolo was immortal and I, too, at the time, felt that way. He was part of my life. It was inconceivable to think that I would never see him again. [...] There were no secrets between us. Some of my confidences inspired him for the subject of Young Husbands, who he wrote for Mauro Bolognini between 1957 and 1958. I was going through a painful and complicated period and I had fled recently from marriage and still suffered from the relapses of my mysterious malaise and he was the trusted and sensitive friend to whom I told my pains. [...] Today nobody doubts the value of Pasolini, the lucidity of his gaze. Yet, then whatever he said or did was challenged, criminalized, persecuted. [...] He took part in life with all himself by damning himself for all his ugliness. More than anything, the bourgeois mentality and the most miserable aspects of our society hit him. While most of us, while conscious of it, manage to live with it well, he was wounded in the underworld in an unsatisfying way. He was like being sacrificed in a human sacrifice.

I was seventeen years old, it was summer and we were on holiday in the mountains, where a theatre company, the Carrozzone of Fantasio Piccoli, was passing through. I hadn't even gone to see the show, but I made friends with some actors. Have you ever thought about being an actress? Why don't you come with us?They proposed to me just before they left for the next stage of their tour. They explained to me that they were always traveling and listed their repertoire: Shakespeare, Platuto, Goldoni, Giacosa... I was very attracted to the idea, but also amazed. I was wondering, who knows why they ask me, who have never acted in my life? However, I decided to follow them. And not because I had the sacred fire of art. No, I didn't even know what that was. I followed them because they were leaving.

Since I was apparently crying for no reason, my parents had me visited by a famous doctor. "No, he has nothing, but the child is hypersensitive and diagnosed him. A word that from that day on was used whenever it was necessary to explain my behavior. [...] Once, I heard my father say: "But do something for this child!" He meant, perhaps, to rebel, to do curls, anything to improve my appearance... He worked in Switzerland for a company that produced almost invisible screws for precision instruments and was always taken by his work, but, when there was, he was affectionate with me. Actually, in his own way, he gave me great tenderness. [...] My brother was said to be lively, funny and beautiful. Dressed like a marinatto was fine and my mother, who was attracted by the pleasant appearance of people and things, had a strong preference for him. The fact that I was so close, she liked me less. When she wasn't lying in the dark in her room because of the headache she suffered, she went to the hairdresser, which happened at least three times a week. In the rare moments that my mother dedicated to me, she read me very sad books: misunderstood, white Fang, Michael circus dog, Oliver Twist. Then who knows why, the readings for childhood were of only two types: terrifying stories or depressing novels that had as protagonists abandoned children, poor and mistreated, with which I naturally identified myself. [...] Nevertheless, I have become an incurable optimist.

Everywhere there were only rubble and destruction, but you could breathe a completely new, strange, exciting air that had infected me. I too, together with Milan, had been freed. [...] I was fifteen years old, my mother began to buy me beautiful clothes and shoes with a little heel. I was no longer a frightened child: my physical appearance had greatly improved. It's not like I'd grown older, but it's nicer. The maturity never reached me. The world changed for me, because men liked me very much, to my great satisfaction. No morality, no restraint, no modesty. When someone touched me, I was happy. I didn't see an affront, but a kindness. I've never disdained myself. It was nice. [...] I had become another person, and not only physically. I was always cheerful and funny.[...] I was very open, ready to establish a relationship with anyone who wanted me. The idea of being able to arouse interest in others was for me such a strange fact that it seemed to me that it was a great kindness to me. [...] I experienced a great sexual and intellectual vivacity and also a deep sense of revolt towards my family. I wanted to leave, where we don't know, and I didn't do anything to hide it.

Fabio [Mauri] and I were both disturbed, though in a different way: we looked alike. Instead, I couldn't feel comfortable with her parents. They had a very conventional mindset. In a short time it was as if I had returned to live with my parents again, with the addition that this time the family had created me. A hell of a trap. Eventually, the marriage turned out to be a scary mistake. Fabio, however, had no fault of it except that he became, in spite of him, a close relative of mine. [...] The marriage had lasted two years, and it took many more to separate me definitively. Then there was no divorce, so it was necessary to ask for the annulment of the Sacred Rota. After an infinite time, I was summoned to the tribunal of the Holy See for questioning. [...] Thus began the cancellation process which at the time lasted years, years and years. From time to time, friends and relatives and even Professor Musatti were called to testify: an infinite procedure. When the referendum on divorce arrived in 1970, the tribunal of the Holy See was still studying my case, examining the papers, grinding papyri, evaluating the testimonies. In my life I never participated in a piazza demonstration, but when they won the no, that same night I went dancing wearing a black dress by Dior with a velvet collar where the inscription "NO" was in rhinestone. The divorce law had finally passed and, suddenly, waiting for the verdict of the Sacred Rota was no longer necessary.

Just when I had reached the full awareness of having become a good actress and of pleasing the public, I discovered that I was suffering from severe psychic problems. And I was ashamed of it as an unconfessible guilt. No one then wanted to be taken for a disturbed person. At some point, I thought I'd shoot myself in the leg to have a visible and obvious illness. I used to call for a real wound to feel innocent. [...] Mine, very worried, began to consult everyone: doctors, psychiatrists, healers. [...] After many unsuccessful attempts, only the trip to Lourdes was missing, they turned to the psychoanalyst Cesare Musatti, who took care of me. [...] Musatti's were the classic psychoanalysis sessions: I lay on a sofa, while he sat in an armchair behind me, near a Greek-Roman bas-relief. At his suggestion, every morning I took note of the dreams I had made and, as I told them, I heard him write, write, write. He had stacks of notebooks he was taking notes on. Who knows then what he did with it... I had undergone a lot of care, even the most absurd, but psychoanalysis was the only one that gave results, and it really gave results for the whole life. It was like attending a university: I learned some things that I have left forever. [...] He has opened a way for me, for my mind, for my life, for everything.

In 1963 Bernardo Bertolucci proposed to me to act in the role of the protagonist in Prima della Rivoluzione, his second film as director. Of course I accepted, but for me it was a psychodrama. Bernardo took my soul in that movie: my character looked too much like me. And when something concerns you so directly, it may even seem disgusting. Only when I saw it finished, did I realize how beautiful that movie was. Bernardo was my partner for almost five years. I met him through Pier Paolo. I saw him for the first time in 1962, when he was shooting The dry commando, his first film, with Pasolini's script. [...] Bernardo was ten years younger than me and he was really a kid: I specialized in younger men, the only ones with whom I ever managed to have lasting relationships. So, such a difference in age was scandalous, but not for our circle of friends who from the very beginning cheered for us. [...] My work often took me away from Rome and he reached me. The last time was in Turin, where I was performing in Cesare and Cleopatra by Bernard Shaw. So Bernardo came to see me, but shortly afterwards we broke up. It ended because of me, because I used to destroy the real ties. Of the two, I was immature. I was resisting. I had a wedding gone wrong behind my back. The only relationship I could remain faithful without effort at the time was with the stage.

Buñuel, who was one of the world's most acclaimed directors, had a reputation for being very demanding. Just a few days before my arrival he had kicked out an actress and I was very worried, also because he didn't know me at all. [...] The last thing I wanted was to get away from the set! It would have been better, rather, to face his judgment to you without witnesses. So I called one of his assistants and explained to him that I needed to speak to the Master. I undressed and, naked as I was, slipped into a Valentino raincoat lined with fur. When Buñuel came in, did he just in time to pronounce the bonjour and the ça va? of rite that I, wide open my luxurious trench, showed me how a maniac does in the bushes of a park. ==References====External links==* Official website Then I explained to him that I wanted him to see me before I started and he reassured me, not without some embarrassment. [...] During the shooting, he placed the camera right under the piano. And in the meantime, all he did was say, "Madame, je ne suis pas a pornographe, vous savez", ma'am, I'm not a pornographer, you know?

In the 1970s, they offered me many roles in movies of which a serious actress like I was not going to be proud. Yet, I accepted them. [...] Now it is different, but in the twentieth century an actress who was undressing was criticized. I didn't care. [...] So I filmed some films more or less forbidden to minors. In fact, most of these films dared more than the pornosoft line belonged to that hybrid genre that was the most malicious, then popular Italian comedy. [...] Tinto Brass proposed to me a part in a historical kolossal set in the Roman Empire, to be shot in Cinecittà with an international cast: Caligola, one of the most forbidden films in the history of cinema. [...] Caligula was withdrawn the same day he was due to go out in the Italian halls. Then the director was tried for obscenity, and we actors were also involved. [...] Everyone can have their own opinion of what is really obscene. For me pornography is not showing an explicit sex scene: it is a form of mental squalor. You can be completely dressed and be equally indecent.

For an actor, the dressing room is a place of peace and isolation. A place where you can always take refuge, at any time of the day, to rest, fantasize, sleep, do what you want. [...] Yes, the life of the dressing room is really very pleasant, even if you are always a little alarmed. You get dressed, you get makeup, you exchange confidence. [...] When I was in the company of the Little One, I shared the dressing room with two other young actresses like me. [...] Then the company was governed by a strict discipline that prohibited actors from going out freely to visit other people's dressing rooms. So we were all three, each with his ambassadors, holed up in that little space of ours to wait. [...] I don't know if the Little One is still so strict, but today the companies are generally much more tolerant: in the dressing room you can invite anyone you want. I brought Scintina for years. It was a meek, obedient Yorkshire, as well as long - lived twenty - five years of escape even from a ruinous fall off a balcony. Now his place has taken Ça Suffit, who by last name Zampiz also took from Elsa Morante the habit of giving name and surname to my dogs, who instead barks continuously and, therefore, must stay at home.

I've died many times on stage. For example, in this evening we recite it as a subject, when I was in the company of Vittorio Gassman, in the sixties. Mommina, my character, died at the end of a long monologue. Every time I lived a better life, I received a big applause. [...] Gassman was a crazy actor, as well as an extraordinarily beautiful and charismatic man. When I wasn't on stage with him, I watched him from the scenes. He had a fixation: to bring the theater to the factories. He wanted to introduce the great classics to the workers. So he forced us to wear a suit and to represent works like Adelchi and Othello. [...] I also met another Vittorio: Vittorio De Sica. I starred in a short vacation directed by him in 1973, from a script by Cesare Zavattini. De Sica entrusted me with a splendid role, Mrs. Scanziani, who earned me the Silver Ribbon and the Golden Grotto as the best actress.

What about professionally? How do you look at the other actresses?Through the actress I see the woman. And the woman who acts as an actress, and not vice versa. We actresses, when we play the same role several times, it's because maybe we're stuck at some point in our existence. And then I know the difficulties to get into this male world. That's why I have a lot of respect for actresses that last over time.

The difficulty comes from the very instrument of this profession: our body. We do not enjoy, like a musician or a director, a protective filter. We must not express ourselves by caressing the keyboard of a piano, nor do we have a camera behind which to take refuge. So the limit is blurred. Between the image and the being, where is the margin? Acting requires a subtle dosage of diving and overflights. An interpretation requires love for the character without ever raping or possessing him, ability to understand its darkness and light to make his heart vibrate through our words and gestures. But be careful not to become that heart!

Despite the love they have for us, I think that parents also exercise control, and that about the thirteen or fourteen years it is necessary to get rid of it, to seek independence. Later, between the ages of thirty and forty-five, another trial starts. Period of construction, both professionally and family. Finally, around the age of forty-five, when the mask of youthful beauty vanishes, when this demon who has been given us by nature no longer protects us, the deep, true self emerges. The use of physicality becomes impossible. We must confront ourselves once and for all with time and death. It's a matter of choice. It's the moment when you start thinking about aesthetic surgery to escape the laws of aging. Or you have the courage to sabotage the reassuring schemes. Try to preserve the curiosity of adolescence on an adult face. Not easy at all...

I had as an actress offered, from all over the world, even hyperbolic, precise, and many realized, which probably had no equivalent either then or after. I say this not for monetary vanity; it would be silly exhibitionism, and then the currency counts and does not count: it comes and goes. It is only for a judgment on a historical period of our cinematography. It is only to show how even then, in the lucky era of silent cinema, Italy was at the forefront; both for productive boldness, and for width of compensation and means. Our silent cinema was victoriously established on all markets and was exclusively Italian without foreign co-productions, without the help of international "vedettes": there was no need! It was, I repeat, the first of the world, and it would be good, today, not to forget it!

The cinema was mute: effects and words gave it to us, the actors, with new and authentic art. I therefore called for no one to touch my negatives. Only I had to mount them. Cutting positives is one thing, but cutting negatives is another. I, then, cut the negatives. And I couldn't let anything be done without me. I even pretended to ignore the existence of development and assembly plants.

I reached the heights of happiness and wealth. And now I'm very often alone with my memories. Everything has necessarily escaped, because everything passes, flies away in time. Or that's at least the feeling you feel. But that's not true. Nothing has escaped: everything remains within us, outside of us: either connected to other things or already in the sum or in the product of ourselves and others. Of course for the final subtraction, hoping that the result will leave a positive rest. And it is for that little "remain " positive that you live, desperately attached to life.

Time passes, it's true, but I'm still creating. I find the same fervor that was in me in the golden years, from 1910 to 1921, when I had the courage, the enthusiasm to stay long hours, day and night, in the theatres of pose or in my Roman studio, to speculate on the plot of my films, to write perhaps the subject, to set up the paintings, to personally curate the shots, to sew piece by piece the shot scenes, armed with faith, agree, but also scissors and pins, because the editing I did with my hands.

Cinema was a true vocation for me, as was religion in my early childhood. As a child, I wanted to be a nun. [..] They said that I would be the most beautiful nun in the world, and time would give them reason, because beautiful I certainly became; but perhaps I would be left with only that quality over the years. And she wouldn't have been the best for a nun. There is no doubt, however, that I was born in a certain way, with a terrible sensitivity, with a great vocation and under excellent auspices.

Meanwhile, in America Italian films were studied in depth: in particular the historical films of Guazzoni and those of Pastrone, which largely influenced the whole production of overseas, to give rise to the two first American colossus: "Intolérance" and "Naissance d"une Nation" by Griffith, in 1916.

Now all of my being, my thinking converged on the character I was supposed to play. I called Mary, begging her to give me the script of the film, of which I reread the scene repeatedly. But, holy God, why make me start from the end instead of the beginning? I thought it was so crazy! Only later did I understand all the meaning of it. Mary was with me: she didn't lose sight of me even for a moment. When I returned her script, she murmured: "Courage, young lady; everything will be fine, you will see. "I don't know if it was the words of that good woman or those that, a few moments earlier, had addressed Gasnier to me, but now I felt a security of which I couldn't explain the causes. I thought a miracle would come to my aid. I hoped it at that precise moment from Providence or from my good star: one of those stars that I had so loved as a child. I felt like I was sailing in an unreality far more strange than the one where my stars were born. I was caught in a sort of spell and by now all I had to do was let myself drift away from that dream that could turn into a beautiful, bright reality. But suddenly the sun appears again: a shining, powerful sun, and the director's voice came to my ear like a trumpet ringing: "Ready everyone! It's turning! == sync, corrected by elderman == @elder_man

As for me, I found him an extremely kind man, even interesting, but nothing more. The idea of an engagement with him or others then had never crossed my mind. I was just a little girl and I had to study, so that one day I could dedicate myself to the Italian dramatic theatre. My dreams were elsewhere: they flew away, crossed mountains, rivers and seas: uncertain dreams, wrapped in fog: but dreams, always dreams.

So much it was written about him that it seems difficult to add something. All I'm gonna say is that the memory of him is still clear for me today. We walked quietly. I also wanted to say many things: to show him my feelings, the admiration I had for him; but I kept silent. However, a feeling of pride survived in me. On my way home, I wanted to be alone. I went to the library. I was anxious to see my Picasso, the Harlequin bleu, who I had placed in the central shelf that was leaning out of the library. The painting by Picasso sparkled in the austerity of that room with walls of ancient red damask. On the other side of the library there were other famous paintings: a delicious Bucher, a Fragonard. From the antithesis between this painting pervaded with an eighteenth-century spirit and the daring of Picasso's brilliant work, destined to imprint on himself the modern time, was born a set of colors, of tones that, while contrasting with each other, created strong harmonies. In that library I loved to gather among the portraits of my best friends. Perhaps they too were lovers of solitude as I was. [...] Women usually go to the Opera to show off the luxury of their clothes, their jewelry. As for me, it was quite different. I loved music, relief from my day of unnerving work. I used to get to the theater late, to the show already started, and I always got out before the end. I hated any form of performance. The most complete mystery enveloped my life.
