Even in the cinema the first effects of the war were felt. I was called back to Rome. He telegraphed the "Celius" that I would return immediately. The return journey was rather exhausting. Although Italy had declared its neutrality, the flame of the great conflict touched it. On trains, in particular, there was evidence of restlessness, discomfort. Travellers seemed concerned: the anxiety of the present, the uncertainty of the future, was seen in their faces. The speeches were all around the war. When I arrived in Rome, I immediately went to the plant, where I was greeted with relief by the staff and the actors, who were waiting for me. Everyone was concerned that the Cines, on which the CelioFilm depended, had placed personal and half-paying actors, on the pretext of war. In our area, these were the first unpleasant consequences of the European conflict. Amazement and irritation for that arbitrary measure can well be guessed. The artists were outraged and everyone was around me hoping that I could fix our situation.

That same night Carrier and I took the road to Rome. We arrived about midnight. My mother was waiting for me, eager to see me again. Soon after, Carrier left us to return to the Grand Hotel. I locked myself in my room. The emotions of the day had been too much. I lay on the bed, knowing I couldn't sleep. I saw that people stir around the coffin of Caruso and thought of cruel destiny. He couldn't fix my mind on anything else. So much success, so much glory, and then abandon everything so suddenly. And he was still young! The glory is avenged, and the reverse of the medal is always for everyone in life. Who knows, one day, I would have been there for myself. Then my thoughts overlapped: Cartier, the cinema. I should have chosen either America or getting married. But the thought of a possible detachment from Cartier made me crazy. It was during that night that I made the irrevocable decision to end it forever with the cinematographer. Enough, I told myself, enough: it's over now!

Writing is fascinating: it's like traveling inside yourself. The stories narrated represent a healthy vent, a symbolic and intimate denunciation, even before social. A woman on behalf of other women. A scream against the pirates of souls. But also a praise to the few, and sometimes exhilarating, "custodi" of mine.

When I returned to Rome, I finally left the house in which my husband had once again unbeknownst to me. I went to live, hiding under false name, in the Residence Marche, near Via Veneto, in the hope of finally being left in peace. In order to meditate and decide my fate. To gather and reconcile with myself, far from everything and everyone. Even at Jimmy's, but mostly at my husband's. I was desperately trying to escape, as the protagonist of Scott Fitzgerald's novel Beautiful and Damned. I also felt as damned as she was, persecuted by the evil, perhaps by the curse of my mother, always unjust and intransigent, in competition because of my infatuated stepfather of me.

I act only if there is a camera in front of me: I do not care alone, so suddenly, for example pretending to be a tree that loses the leaves. Perhaps it is good for those who are more religious than I am towards this profession: those who, in life and in work, need more faith than irony. It's not my case: I love to get into the characters with the experience I have of life, of my life. I like to act, for the possibility that gives me to live, beyond mine, other lives, other stories: I start from me, and I try to invent new ways to be a woman.

I was 17. I came out of that desperate house. I wanted to kill myself. And then... and then, I don't know exactly what happened in my head... it's like I decided I had to be punished for what happened. So I kept seeing him again for a while, after the one before, damned, evening. He insisted, he followed me, he obsessed me. I gave in, I accepted, and I felt sorry for him, for me, for what happened... every day more pain, and more disgust... Men will never understand this kind of mechanism: the ability, the will to get hurt that women have. They have had above all the women of my own and of the past generations: the irresistible attraction towards the abyss; the carrying inside a "fatality" of evil, with the consequent painful resignation; the need to prove to oneself their own weakness, even repellent, that shouts out loud the need for a male protector who in vain has tried to refuse... Or I don't know so much.

I can say with Edith Piaf: "Je ne rerette reen", I do not regret and do not deny anything. I know I owe Vides and Cristaldi my great films: it was they who built me, launched me. They gave me the covers of newspapers all over the world, but they took away my freedom and my personal life: for many years I felt like those saints in the midst of fake flowers and under a glass bell. For years I felt stupid, incapable: there was always someone who spoke in my place, who decided for me what I had to do, say and think. For years I resisted: I prevented myself from living and exploding. Then, finally, I did...

Today, I finally want to live as I please: to be and feel free. And when I say "free," I don't think about possible and trivial freedom on the sentimental plane: on the contrary, I believe in fidelity, I never loved the easy betrayals or adventures just to do. For twenty years, without repentance and rethinking, I have been the companion of Pasquale Squitieri: with him and from him I have learned to be a new person, more capable than ever before, to live my time, more aware of reality, culture and politics. Hated the divism, he gave me a hand to get off my pedestal: as a diva, I discovered myself a woman and began to reflect on my identity and on the female condition in general. I've learned to accept criticism. But I have also learned to criticize her: I do not let certain excesses pass by in silence. Although I am perfectly aware of how impossible this is: for him, his impruence, his vice and his pleasure of provocation, are life itself. And you can't stop him from living.

Life has put me in the need to do more than think. And once I started doing it, I had to go on this road: years and years spent going from one job to another, from one plane to another, from one film to another. In these conditions, it was almost impossible to dedicate oneself to the deepening of culture and thought. I am full of respect and admiration for women who have managed to cultivate thought, and not only action. I have respect and respect for Catherine Spaak, for example. I respect you for your choices, your work. The strength of the women that fascinates me is also, or above all, the strength of their thinking.

In the movies I have been all kinds of women: the people, the bourgeois, the woman object, also the woman in career. And in all of them there was also something of what I myself am: something that belonged to me privately, deeply, even secretly: the fact is (I have already explained...) that I did not base everything on the technique of acting. On the contrary, to be able to recite it, I need to live it, my character. The final result is that I left shreds of Claude and Claudia in all my movies. I am the Angelica del Gattopardo: I belong to his strength, his realism, the stubborn will to go on, the stubbornness. But I am also the protagonist of the film by Squitieri Act of Pain: the mother who ends up killing her son who is the victim of drugs, in a crescendo of tragedy that belongs to me, also that, and that I know very well. And I am the Ida of Comencini's History, with its absolute, total passivity: a creature on which they pass, trampling it, life and history, without her being able to do anything to oppose and defend herself. Because, yes, I am strong, stubborn, volitional, but I can also be very passive. It is a passivity, a fatalism, that come from far away: Africa, Tunis... And I am also Aida, of the Girl with the suitcase: it is mine, authentic, her sadness. I am Carla of the Indifferent: I can also be indifferent or, perhaps, more than indifferent, fatalist. I am the devil, the perdition, the female that destroys, as I have always seen that genius of Bolognini in all the films that I made with him: The beautiful Anthony, The Viaccia, Senility. It is that part of me that I find especially in my adolescence and early youth: when I used my strength, my physicality, my power of seduction for revenge and, by ransom, compared to the horror of which I had been the victim... I think in particular of the protagonist of Viaccia: an extremely cruel woman. I'm not. I've never really been cruel. But I was a woman full of grudges: so I put in the character of the film my grudge full of sadness.

I've got a nice breast, I know. It's always been. It is a part of my body that has never given me problems and I thank Mother Nature: it is all her, the merit. As well as it is the merit of mother nature to have given me a body that fortunately is not a trunk: I have always had this subtle life, very thin, and round hips, and a décolleté that allows me any kind of neckline. I usually wear flat, almost male shoes. But if I go to a party, or an important evening, I wear high heels: I think there is nothing more beautiful than a slender woman. How can I say that? "The charm doesn't suit the dwarfs... "Here, I started from here: from the closed and rebellious character, even a little masculine, of Claude; and from the very feminine physicist of Claudia. And with this material, I built my characters: my career as an actress.

...Once upon a time there was a girl named Claude: a boy's name. That name also gave her a soul as a boy: from a young age she loved the challenge, cultivated the physical and psychological courage, if she was offended she was angry, but she didn't cry. Then, one day, it happened that Claude looked in the mirror: and the mirror was the eyes of others, especially of the boys with whom he met at school or on the beach of Tunis. And in the mirror, she realized she was a woman. Then he began to question himself: what is a woman like this? What am I, woman? How do I like a woman? I like a woman strong, active: I don't like those surrendering, weak, tired, slaves of men. I like beautiful women: I am not ashamed, on the street, to turn to look at them. But their beauty, for me, is inevitably linked to their strength: and it is seen, in the eyes, in the rice, in the way of walking. The strength is also the courage of simplicity: I don't love women who are overdressed, overwhelmed and showy, because through all this they tell of their weakness. I like the dignity of women: dignity to accept that years pass without reacting, stealing clothes from teenage daughters with the illusion of rejuvenating. Yeah, women... how do I like me?

My father never raised his hands on me. My mother gave me some slaps: precisely for this reason, for my being too awake, too restless, too much male. I took the slaps, and I continued: a lot of physicality, and a few words...

The Tunisian Ministry of Culture sponsored films and documentaries. And, in that context, I was chosen by a famous French documentary filmmaker, René Vautier, for a film to shoot in Monastir: we were all in that film, my sister, me, many of our friends and schoolmates. All veiled: we had to play a group of Arab women. [...] In the final scene, we were at the top of a rock, greeting the men who were leaving: the director wanted me alone, in that last shot, to lift the veil from the face, with a blow of wind. And so I got the only close-up of that film: my first time in the film... And it was a lucky first time anyway: because the film was requested in Berlin, where he won the first prize in the documentary section. Someone noticed me. And the offerings, the demands, began.

One of the living testimonies of the unhappiness of the actresses, linked to the fear of ageing, for me was Rita Hayworth. We were shooting the circus and his great adventure. She was already ill, perhaps beginning to suffer from that Alzheimer's disease that would then take her away. At the time, he was 45 years old. And me, a little over 20. At the age of 45, what had been the atomic, one of the most beautiful women in American cinema and in the world, was completely shattered. But he drank and, drinking, he forgot: he seemed absolutely unaware of the physical state in which he had been reduced. He drank, drank, even in front of me. Then he would look at me, and he would say to me, "But I'm still beautiful..." That's also why I've never focused on my own body. Yes, for the press I was a sex Symbol; but in the cinema, thankfully, no; I was above all the actress of the great masters. It wasn't all and only thanks to me: the Africa from which I come taught me to deeply respect destiny, fortune...

First of all, what effect do you think his appearance has? About who? You're an actress, so on the spectators. They see me as a huge, imposing, very tall, majestic, monumental figure. Do you like being seen like this? Yeah, I like it a lot. Why? Because I like to be seen like I'm not in life. And what is it like in life? I'm a common girl in life, like all the others. Do you love her body? I don't know. I explain: the body is what it is and there is nothing but the body as the body is a form in which there is everything and outside of the body there is nothing. So she exists because she has a body and consequently if she doesn't love her body, she doesn't really love herself. I don't mind, but I don't love it. I'm not narcissistic. What part of her body do you prefer? What do you mean? The part that seems to you best express what you are. Arms. Why? I think my arms express very well what I am and what I feel.

Do you ever dream of people?Yes, I happen to dream of actors. Don't you dream of the man you love or who, at least in your dream, seems to love? I never dream of personal things, I dream of crazy things. Like what? Once I had a dream, I was on a balcony, on the fifth floor, and all of a sudden a blow of wind threw down a three-year-old boy who was on the balcony and I then went crazy going downstairs in search of the child's body, I came to the street and asked all the passersby where the child had fallen, finally under an iron arch I found a piece of the child's dress and then I kept looking for the body crying and finally told me that he had been taken to a place, I believe in the hospital and anyway he had not died although he had fallen from the fifth floor, then I went to visit him at the hospital and at that time I woke up. Always bodies that fall? Yours, the baby's? Yeah. Dressed or naked these bodies? The baby, dress. What about you? I'm naked. Now tell me: what do these dreams have to do with what you are during the wake? I don't know.

Often the actors are as beautiful as you can see them on the screen. But they are far more unhappy than they appear and do not know. They're more or less as unhappy as men and women. Because it is equally difficult, for both, to live with the myth of themselves: becoming a sex symbol means, then, for the rest of your life, constantly dealing with the anguish of not being up to the myth, with the fear of disappointing the people who will meet you on the street, with the anxiety of losing their power of seduction. From anguish and insecurity to neurosis, to, in some cases, illness and paranoia: the step is relatively short. Memory, when I think back to my "colleghi" and my "colleghe" and my "colleghe" of work, gives me the image of so many dark, silent, very little communicative people.

Yeah: So, how did she seduce, or seduce herself, Claudia Cardinale? You have to be endowed with authentic charm. You have to have a look worthy of this name. And then I like the mystery: I like a person to discover; while I don't like people totally clear and open, perhaps because I have never been interested in the easy things. So this explains that I'm the ones who fall in love first with their heads, and only later with their bodies. From a man, I want him to be strong. Because I know I'm a strong woman, and with a man I want to establish an equality relationship: I don't want to be dominated, but I don't even want to dominate. That's why, even when I get older, I never happened to make a fantasy about a much younger man: I enjoy that the boys, when I walk down the street, declare their admiration, but everything ends there. Nor have I ever made a sentimental or erotic fantasy about a woman. While I happened to have been the subject of this kind of attention from some women: there was an Arab princess who chased me for years, she also sent me embarrassing gifts, expensive jewelry, for example, and wrote to me... I had a hard time getting rid of her. And she was not the only one: who knows why I have often aroused these homosexual thoughts and desires. Among my most uninhibited admirers I had a priest, a general and a Japanese journalist. The latter, in particular, was to marry, and on the day of the marriage he did not show up because he had discovered shortly before that the future bride did not give sufficient guarantees as my admirer... The priest? He was texting me post office from New York. And he continued to write to me for years: he addressed me a bit as he would address, in prayers, Our Lady. For Christ's sake, never a hint of sex: he worshiped me as a sacred image.

In 1957, at the Venice Film Festival, I saw my first film in the room: and it was Luchino Visconti's White Nights. I remember the award ceremony perfectly. When, to that of Visconti, the film of Satyayit Ray Aparayito was preferred, and he had the Silver Lion, he went up to take it, on stage, with a couple of ironic jokes: "I prefer to make the Indian," and then quoted the Koran: "The silence is gold, the word is silver." I sat in the audience next to Rossana Podestà, to Rino Salviati, to Bozzini and Salvatore Argento, who at the time were the rectors of UnitaliaFilm, organizer of the Italian film week in Tunis, within which there was the competition of the most beautiful Italian of Tunisia, won by me in a completely involuntary and unconscious way. All these people had adopted me, treated me like a daughter. I was staying with my mother in a Lido hotel. And from there I reached every morning the beach of the Excelsior, where they were all there: I remember Renato Salvatori, an incredible amount of directors, producers. And there was also Franco Cristaldi...

Thank God I never got confused with the girl who appeared photographed in the papers. Otherwise, I would have lost my mind. People are sometimes surprised that my friend Alain Delon talks about himself in the third person. The fact is that you usually ignore what a star's life means; rumors about us, clouds of courtiers, servants who make us see the world as a vast satellite. The more we are idolized, the stronger we must be in order not to lose our identity as human beings. I was lucky to have always been aware of my identity. When I look on the screen, I know it's not really me, but someone who looks like me. I've never confused roles. And when I meet someone on the street who smiles at me, I'm just happy, as if he compliments me for working well. I've never tried to seduce, to lie, I've just tried to make people dream.

In 1962, Marilyn Monroe committed suicide. He was 36 years old. That news exploded like a bomb in those years of sweet life, like the announcement of a divine curse, of a punishment launched on a woman too beautiful who had played too much with the desire of men... I was interviewed by some journalists about it. They asked me if I was afraid of that fate as an actress. Why the confrontation? That was crazy. We had nothing in common, Marylin and I. I didn't feel more involved in that death than any other girl of my generation could be. But I understood Marilyn's loss, despair. Here's a star who got lost. But she had nothing to hold on to! Not only did she lack great love. The presence of a secretary, a doctor, a passenger lover, could nothing against the loneliness and suffering of childhood, the absence of the family. She had been fed to the public without anyone protecting her, increasing her fragility. On the contrary, I felt very strong, and above all deeply normal. I owe it to Dad, Mom, Blanche, Bruno, Adrien, and Beppo, our wolf dog. My family gave me security and normality. At home, they were talking about my brothers' electronic studios, the same way they were talking about filming my own movie. We all had dreams, desires, jobs. Adrien became a cameraman, but Blanche couldn't follow my footsteps in the movies. It was difficult to impose another Cardinal. Anyway, that's what Cristaldi said. Of course, my job was a bit strange, not only because I earned much more than everyone else, but because it continued outside the set, during the evenings in long dress, the sessions by the photographer, the interviews. But I was Claude, the older sister who had a minister's agenda, and who others treated like a star. The reporters came to interview me about this, about that, but in the family my opinion didn't matter anymore than the others. They didn't even talk about the famous people I knew. It would have been a long time if my mother had allowed them to call them "good people." They had too much money and they were too famous. He pretended they didn't exist. That somewhat misunderstandingd world remained outside the home walls.

Rita Hayworth was shocking. He lived the worst tragedy for a star: a slow decadence. Was she already ill with Alzheimer's when I met her? I don't know. All I know is that she was deeply unhappy. One day, on the set of the Circus, we saw her coming with bruises on her face. I don't know who did that to her. But I think she had a lot of problems with her kids who couldn't stand to see her drink. It was heartbreaking to meet a goddess like that. I felt almost uncomfortable, with my twenty-four years, my smile, my successes. I wanted to hug her, comfort her. One day, we met together in front of the mirror lights, for a touch-up between scenes. Looking at me he told me: "Anch'io, I was beautiful" (Translated with Google Translate) A compliment that did not hide a certain hostility. I couldn't hold back, and I cried. My emotion touched her. In front of that blinding light, which does not forgive, we dropped the mask. I felt helpless. All we could do was surrender to destiny, and perhaps, for a moment, accompany it along its last stretch of road. One might be surprised to know that on the set the intimate relationships are tightened in this way, and that they often end up with the last take. But it is. We actors, we only have one family on the scene: the one the casting gave us.

Visconti had fun taunting us. "Claudine, I don't want fake kisses, fake caresses..." "I acted cunningly, I played in my turn, and Luchino appreciated me more: I didn't fall into the trap, showing him that, despite my smile, my sensuality, my apparent sweetness, I was a strong woman. After all, he could only love women of character; the Chanels, the Dietrichs. On his desk, in Rome, there was only one photo, signed "I love you", the "Angelo azzurro."

No one in the house ever thought to call me "Claudia," I was Claude, an androgynous name that was well suited to my nature as a male, always sweaty, noisy, digging neck, refractory to every female frill. My register of civil status also includes the names of Joséphine and Rose my grandmothers, also adapted to the colonial style.

And I had never met love, what girls dream of. The great love. On the sets, I was the producer's lady for everyone. A very common actress model. You could mention De Laurentiis and Silvana Mangano, David O. Selznick and Jennifer Jones, Carlo Ponti and Sophia Loren... but also Franco Cristaldi and Claudia Cardinale. I was undoubtedly the only one to have been surprised by this finale, this metamorphosis, slow, progressive, of our relationship. Franco had always taken care of everything, he had always taken care of me. By virtue of being like a husband, he eventually became one. Almost. Because he was already married. But at the time of our meeting, he was separated. I never stole anyone's husband. However, was that the freedom I dreamed of when, at the age of eighteen, I decided to find the means to raise my own son? Cristaldi had many qualities. He was seductive, and I was infinitely grateful to him for helping me at the time when I felt lost, but he spent most of his time in a small three - story building: his offices. He gave me jewelry, furs, but I had the feeling he did it more by convention than by a genuine emotional impulse. He kept buying me. And it was above all this that made me uncomfortable, so I went to work to show him that I didn't need anything, I never asked for anything, I didn't encourage anything. Besides, we almost never were alone.

Cristaldi had been a lawyer before he became a producer. Dozens of pages organized my professional life, fixed the amount of my monthly salary, determined beforehand and regardless of the importance of my cache collected by the producers. Pages that laid down the names of the tailors who dressed me, the expenses that Vides took on, the following composed of driver, secretary, press officer. My life was controlled. Step by step. With those articles, those paragraphs, those chapters on which I had signed, I had sold my soul. The deal forced me to give up my free will. I had no right to my image anymore. The Vides had bought me as a commodity: color and length of the hair, measurements, everything was written without me being able to make even the smallest modification. Every decision about my personal life had to be made in agreement with my producers. Cristaldi was good and generous, but he never failed to remind me that he had created me and that consequently I belonged to him. Even today, many years after our separation, many years after his death, I have not forgotten anything.

Women don't consider me a rival, men talk to me with respect, although sometimes they call me Gina or Sophia. Rarely have I been attacked, once maybe someone shouted to me: "You make up too much!" I put an end to the conversation, saying, "Don't mind your own business!" Probably the fact that I was never afraid protected me, making my life easier. Today, I have nothing to hide. I'm the way I am.

To reconnect with everyday life, each one has his own rites. I need solitude, silence. After the passage to the dressing room, after having put on my makeup, like the clown of the circus, Claudia becomes Claude again. And I go home, where everything is in its place. All those who are not strong enough to find their way home, in the perennial earthquake of our careers, risk their lives turning into tragedies.

Without needing any confidence, Visconti understood everything about the violence I was hiding. He loved to feel that a shadow weighed on the past of his actors, because it added that part of mystery that gave depth to his characters. The directors are always a little medium. But no one better than Valerio Zurlini guessed what a different girl, an actress who did not belong to any group, no clique, made me. It was with him that I would turn the girl with the suitcase, my first big role.

When they asked Mastroianni what he preferred about Visconti, who had made him debut in the theater, or Fellini, who had made him a great movie star, he replied: "Visconti was the teacher that the schoolchildren love and admire, Fellini the classmate". Visconti was a teacher to me, too. I admired his culture, his curiosity that allowed him to be perfectly aware of fashion and makeup in vogue, the songs of Sanremo or the best sellers. He was an enthusiast of history, literature, music. A hard teacher, sometimes sarcastic, ironic, cruel, who did not know how to show us his love and expressed it with a thousand little gifts. [...] Fellini, on the contrary, was at ease improvising. We called him the magician, because he possessed a special genius capable of involving us in his dreams. He did not elude problems, he put them before our eyes, as an integral part of his fantasy.

My father had two passions: the watches, which disassembled and reassembled with meticulousness, and the violin, which played to make us fall asleep. I grew up in a family of musicians: my sister practiced piano and singing, Adrien with guitar. The big exit of the family was for the concert. But no movie theater! Of American films, I knew the posters well: how not to notice the faces of men and women chained in a kiss... yet, at fifteen years old, neither I nor my sister, younger than a year, were allowed to attend shows contrary to morale. We could dream in front of the pictures posted on the panels at the entrance of the halls. But we girls didn't have to know anything about loving life. And let's forget about the "sexual" life, a word we wouldn't have said without blushing. And not even the simple functioning of our bodies. In those days, at school, we studied the reproduction of amoebas... and I remember the panic that struck Blanche [his sister] at her first menstruation, the upset cry that called my father to help, and the slap that earned her that scare! At our house it was a ritual, a quick way to teach daughters not to talk about those things there. Every month I heard Mom tell Dad, with much mysterious, that the British had landed. A no-girl who used to put my brothers on the windows... I was squealing. I didn't do it...

In Mayrig (1991), I am thirty-five years old. It is the time when the Armenian family, escaped genocide, arrives in Marseilles. In 588 rue Paradis, released the same year, I have eighty-five and curved path. The makeup magician, an Italian, had come on purpose from Los Angeles to work on this metamorphosis that required hours of makeup. In the end, looking at me in the mirror, I was shocked. Was it really me? Would I have become like that old lady one day? I don't like to look back, but I don't even like to imagine the future. Let us live the present!

Of course, being in front of the camera began to please me, I soon realized that it would be my best ally, yet I was too easily detached from my characters, letting emotions attack me waves, intense but short. For me cinema was still a job like the others. It's the Germi movie that served as a revelation. Peter did not like to speak, he had a shady character, he expressed himself in a grumpy way, he had to grasp his suggestions. Yet, he knew how to make me realize that you couldn't get on the set with your hands in your pocket and wait, limiting yourself to obeying the director. You had to give yourself up, let yourself go. That is to say letting yourself invest, transform, enslave by your role. And at the price of this abandonment that the actor gives life to a character. This new investment may have saved me from the evidence I accumulated: there was no place for personal concerns on the set. An actor must be emotionally available. He cannot be overwhelmed by problems, his personal history. You have to get to "virgins" and accept the adventure: let yourself be guided by the director on an unknown land, without fear of danger. Photogenius wasn't enough for me anymore. But I could listen and I liked to work. On the basis of these two qualities I built a career that I had not chosen.

For me, at that time, the stars were Brigitte Bardot, Sophia Loren, Gina Lollobrigida... A star was more than a human being, it was a kind of goddess made sacred by lights, customs and scenarios, it was someone who lived on another planet. I wanted to jump, run, and go home untied and covered in dust... The cinema demanded real women, with long nails and lipstick on the lips. I never thought I'd undergo the transformation that would allow me to be like them. Besides, I wasn't in a hurry to grow up. Cinema was just a game.

Lutka lived on the island two young sisters, Lutka and Liuba, very different from each other: Lutka was beautiful, with a pale and delicate face, the emerald green eyes and long blond hair that fell soft on her white shoulders like milk; Liuba instead was ugly, with marked features and dark complexion; she had long black hair and ruffled, but the eyes were sweet and the smile kind.  Liuba is so good!  They said to the village.  But Lutka is so beautiful!

One night, on her way back to bed, with two nice chocolate mustache on her chubby cheeks, she saw her mom talking to a gentleman in her study.  Doctor, is it that bad?  She asked her mother crying.  Yes, her mother is very ill answered the doctor.  She needs care and that encouragement that only a daughter can give. He asked for her, and only her affection and self-sacrifice may save her.

To see me smile again, Father Joseph built me a small theater, I remember it very well: an axis and a seabed. It was simple, without puppets, but I created them in my imagination, pulling invisible threads and stories came to life by magic. That theater made me dream, it was my great place. That my future was somewhere else, I warned him. I felt that one day, not far away, the time of detachment would come and I feared it. My brothers and playmates warned me that I wasn't like them, and sometimes they made me understand, making fun of me. The tender remembrance of my first small theatre always accompanies me and I like to think of it as an omen of the destiny that awaited me. Later, much later in the years, I had another Piccolo Teatro: the immense Piccolo Teatro in Via Rovello. And this time the puppet was me, a puppet aware that being tied to the threads of Giorgio Strehler was an incomparable discovery of new horizons: in the fatigue of work, in the freedom of feelings, in the everyday life. A life that tells it seems like the plot of a novel.

How many times in life have I come to meet rich men, very rich, but for me the maximum of wealth is my work, the joy of living, the luck of living in a house magically laid in a garden... I, as I write, am looking at poetry, nature, the sky among trees, the little birds that jump from one branch to another, more happiness and wealth than this, what am I looking for?

I know people who claim that an actress should only devote herself to her work and not fall in love or have a private life. No. I want to have my life. I want to be a simple, fragile person, even desperate, without the robe of the divine pronunciation... Think about the character you see at the beginning of the Scala or that I can seem even now, as I write. I know who I am. For me the important thing is that, in front of the camera or on the stage, I can enter the most intimate recesses of my sensitivity to identify myself in the characters to a degree so deep that I allow the audience to share them with me. Audience who is then free to love, criticize or destroy. I only see my work as an act of love. And I say more: between cinema and theatre I love the theater more.

What you feel every night reciting in front of an audience is a great emotion, a deep love and an endless fear of not knowing how to give to the bottom what you have inside, which, when it happens, demeans you and makes you feel useless. But when that fluid made of magic, indescribable, delusional is established between the audience and the actor, then delirium is brought to the greatest unbridledness. You can't even imagine how nice it is to act, how nice it was to interpret the characters I've lived and suffered over the years. Although sometimes I've come to pieces, dragging shreds of my life and my characters behind me. Sometimes I wonder who I really am and if, perhaps, by virtue of being different people, I have not, in the end, lost myself. But the answer is no: I gave form and concreteness to the words written in the scripts to make them flesh that pulsates, that breathes, I dare say that I have embodied them.

People are led to see in us actors creatures a little different and a little strange, but in the end I am a woman like all the others, with all its contradictions, its fears, its solitudes and its pains. A woman with her joy and despair to live. Perhaps I am not as simple as I am, but I am certainly simpler than others can judge me.

Stired in a snowflake, I was born in Milan, on January 1, at sunset time. After a long descent from the sky, I slipped into a fireplace and down I found myself among the loving and sweet poaches of a Lombard peasant who immediately gave me some of her milk, and I wanted more and more... then covered me with a warm blanket of wool... but stinged!

Mother Rina nursed me and her little girl named Uliva [...] My mother certainly thought about my maintenance, but she was too young to care for a daughter: she had just graduated from the Conservatory on piano and was trying to assert herself. In seven years he came to visit me only twice [...]. They told me it was Aunt Olga, a very beautiful lady who, however, I felt to be very different from the people I was used to living with. [...] Make-up, elegant, smelled of flowers: it made me a strange effect, with her I felt uncomfortable. His image caused me a dark and indefinable anguish. I watched her with terror. Though beautiful, she seemed to me a horrible woman; in those seven years she brought me only one gift: not a toy, but a box of cookies. Mom Rina made me the only doll of my childhood by putting together some rags. I loved that doll with so much love.

I managed to reach the bar, I would have had a drink so that I could pretend to be sick, an excuse like another to leave. But Zanuck approached me and brutally laid his hands on me just as I was drinking a sip of whiskey: sciaff! I despised whiskey in his face. I knew it would be the end of my Hollywood career, but what satisfaction, what joy. The pleasure of throwing that whiskey at this idiot was much more important to me. Just because he was the great producer Zanuck would I have to stand at his feet, be his slave, become his lover? I had to let him boast of having an Italian woman, a French woman, another one of who knows where?! I threw whiskey in his face with such a taste but with such a taste that I came to say, "You disgust me." And after I sent him to hell, I took the door.He had me called after two days. I thought it was weird that he waited so long, he wanted to prepare a speech. I reached him at Fox in his beautiful office in boiserie, with all the windows containing valuable books and prizes of polo. He was very elegant, with his zuava pants and boots, he seemed ready to go to a game; he walked up and down the room and at a certain moment stopped and said to me: "Don't you know that I can make you the greatest star in the world or I can just like you that?" (Do you not know that I can make you the greatest star in the world or can I destroy you in a moment?). I said, "That's exactly what I want, I want to be destroyed just like that." That's what I said to her face. "Goodbye. We don't have anything more to say" (Goodbye. We have nothing else to say to each other). And so it ended. What actor doesn't care about a Hollywood career? I had made films that had been very successful. But I didn't want to accept the producer's court.

In Turin the grandparents had a stage at the Regio and always took me with them: my baptism was Strauss' Elettra, a complicated and mysterious work for an eight-year-old girl but I was fascinated by it. I grew up with music and for me every time I went to the theater to listen to concerts it was like going to a party. Even today, as I read or study, I listen to music: notes help me to make my memory flow better.

The next day I was enrolled by the nuns, at the primary school in Piazza Vittorio, an ancient building, with a large garden full of trees where, as soon as I could and no one could see me, I would take off my shoes and walk barefoot on the grass because I wanted to hear the pebbles and the freshness under my feet, as when I lived in the countryside. It was an operation that I secretly did because the nuns wouldn't approve of it since they wanted to make me a young lady educating me according to the best etiquette, just me coming from the countryside and talking dialètti And a little girlfriend didn't go snitching?! I hated her: because of her I was put under surveillance and from that moment on I could no longer walk without shoes. I wanted to punish her by putting a pebble in her mouth like I had already done in Rivolta d'Adda to a little girl who had been teasing me.

In those first days in the capital the uncle offered to accompany me to see and know the city. His work allowed him to have some days off, my cousins were at school and he was my chaperone. We took his car and first wanted to show me the dome of St. Peter from the hole in a lock. The idea made me laugh, unbelieving, but we set out. After a while we stopped and said to me: "Take two or three steps and you will find yourself in a magical square dedicated to the Knights of Malta. Go, I'll let you find out for yourself and then you'll tell me your impressions." I went there and was enchanted. In front of an old garden of cypresses, oleanders and roses, an architect in a state of grace had built a long wall of white marble about two meters high. On the bottom, on the sides, two benches of the same marble; on the top, on the right, a sculpted frieze with a base that carries a large beautiful stele of grooved marble, very white, with a majestic coat of arms that emanates sparks of fire. In the center, instead, stands a votive newsstand with friezes of an ineffable grace, flanked by two wonderful obelisks that raises no towards the sky, and light wavy cypresses, as to crown so much beauty.

During the summer season, and strictly after finishing our homework, we were allowed to go to the zoo of the public gardens. We would go around the animals a little bit, watch them fantasize that one day we could free them all, then we would walk away in the park and lose ourselves in our Indian pursuits. One of my playmates came from Trieste and, after his father's death, had come to live in Milan with his mother, a cellist, at 55 Corso Buenos Aires, very close to my home. His name was George. Giorgio Strehler. Many years later and in another situation he looked me in the eye and said, "But then you are the little girl who spoke only dialect." "Yes," I smiled. "The only child among three boys. I was already in love with you." This memory deeply moved us. It is one of the many mysterious threads that have guided me in life, as if the case were amused to make me fall into those spaces that would then become the places around which I would have lived my destiny in the future. And if this is the case, the case knows a lot.

I went back to Hollywood, and I lived with Dick [Richard Basehart] in a beautiful house at 8 North Palm Drive where I spent some of the most beautiful moments of my life. Jackie was born and when I took him home, in the middle of October, the roses along the driveway at the entrance had bloomed with a thousand white flowers. I loved singing his lullabies, holding him. I wanted to be the sweetest mom in the world. We had a lot of friends. One of them was Charlie Chaplin. He and Dick saw each other almost every day just to play a tennis game; he often came to us for breakfast with his wife Oona O'Neill, the daughter of the great playwright. One day Chaplin told me, "Valentina, you'll be the star of my next movie." It was Lights of the limelight, but I had to say no because I would have given birth after a few months. We often went to Cary Grant; he invited us in the afternoon to have tea or he would come to us for lunch with William Wyler, the director of Roman Holidays, and Gregory Peck.

On December 11, 1967, I was in Geneva at Jackie's. I got up before seven to get back to Milan with the 8.30 train, turned on the radio and heard: "De Sabata's hornies were donated..." Its green eyes like the stormy sea, always veiled with nostalgia and sometimes crossed by steel glows were no longer there. Victor was shot in the air. He died at one o'clock at night, and I learned it from the speakers of a radio. I slipped to the ground affected by pain and guilt. Why didn't I come up that day to hug him? Why couldn't I bring him some joy? Fifty years had passed since his first entry to the Scala in 1917; to his extreme greeting the instrumentalists wanted to interpret the same Funeral March that Victor had directed at the funeral of Toscanini, but they did not want another conductor to replace him because no one could reach his height. They said, "For the Master we play," and the whole orchestra of the Scala tore the soul of those who were there to listen to them.

Every winter night, coming back from rehearsals and shows, I prepared hot chocolate and we were there, George [Strehler] and I, drinking our cup in front of each other. It was such a good time. He went crazy, made him come back as a child so much that he couldn't resist and, when he had finished it, he licked his cup properly. But in the end, George was a child. He used to do the same at restaurants, sneaking around, and I had a lot of fun imitating him. That George was a child was clear to everyone: he had the mirror of the bathroom covered with a towel and made his beard by heart, without looking at himself. I, every morning, threw it away and he, punctually, got angry. He couldn't stand to see himself. Nice as it was. But that's what he was like. With our two characters we often argued, but after all it was just a game: he was chasing me and I locked myself in the elevator, pressed the halt and I was stuck between one floor and the other. I heard him screaming, he wanted to join me, and I was just standing there to piss him off even more. As soon as I heard him slam the door of the house, I would start the elevator again, I would run and look out at the window and scream, "Giorgio, did you call me?"Once upon a time he was so angry that he punched the elevator wall so hard that he left us a hole: I think it's still there. The problem is that I don't even remember the reason: for me, for work, for taxes. Or why did Inter lose?

But let's get back to the serious things: I like shoes crazy! I find the shoe itself a wonderful object. We immediately clear the field from possible misunderstandings: the real shoe must have the heel. The heel is essential, essential, fundamental, more: it is the shoe. It is a philosophical question, I would say ontological: the shoe, the noumeno of the shoe, has the heel. High: under the seven centimeters it is not even taken into consideration. In fact the so-called dancer is not to be considered real shoe, shoe to the bottom. Maybe only Audrey Hepburn was able to be charming with that insulting little thing on her feet, and in any case she was better off with a little heel. Otherwise he always risked the Sabrina effect, which is not by chance his best film.

To musicians I envy grace, and above all freedom. In my profession I am totally conditioned by the word, limited by the language; they can play everywhere, for any audience, with any colleague, met maybe an hour before: just a look, a score, a diapason. Happily stateless, free from national, ethnic or ideological barriers: Daniel Barenboim is right, music awakens time. And he tame it, dilates it, spells it, cradles it.

I wonder if I can explain myself when I say that I was lucky: in both cases there was what technically is called "incompatibility with life." The choice had already been made by nature, or by chance, or as the hell we want to call it. I just had to decide to anticipate an event already sanctioned. As De André says: I didn't create pain. But there is only one adjective that comes to mind if I think about those days: devastating, it was devastating. And to think that I, an idiot, believed that in a certain sense the world had to thank me, because by making that decision I took on a pain that would otherwise burden others: on my husband, on the daughter that I already had, on my mother, on society. And that's how I took it all. Let no one dare say that women miscarriage lightly. This is never the case, never the case, and especially when it comes to therapeutic abortion. You'd give anything for someone to say to you at that time: "Go ahead, lady, that everything will be fixed..." Anything.

It is a short time before the massacre of the Carretta. Paola Masino was present. He tells it in my house, on the terrace to all those people who are shocked. She seems herself invaded by the murderous fury that has swept her into the midst of that crowd. Among his words of atrocious evidence appears trembling, for a moment, the white head of the Carretta, his terrified eyes. [...] Paola is pale in the dark of the terrace. His beautiful, dramatic face has a ruthless light perhaps for the flash of green eyes still soaked with horror. Pandolfi, miraculously released from prison on the very day of the exodus of the Germans in a risky circumstance of which his grateful heart loves to be grateful, silently stands, his hand entraped against the chest closed in the black glove, his head bowed in silence. He raises his eyes to Paola and says: "In there, you see, we have desired these things, these sold. Now they don't make sense.

At the theater, with a kind of exaltation, he threw himself on the comedies that had been spoken of in a low voice for many years. Those who gave themselves to themselves in Paris, London, America, when we tried in vain to obtain from the Ministry the right to represent Dumas. Authors read clandestinely, sometimes overrated by our nostalgia for intellectual freedom were represented without selection of works, with an urgent anxiety of which the public became involved. The Italian authors, protected for many years by the fascist laws, paradoxical point of the nationalist autarchical utopia, began to protest, to write [...] insulting actors and directors, delevating the public who rushed to applaud works "according to them mediocre who had the sole merit of bringing foreign acronyms. The truth is that in everyone was burning the thirst for words in which it was not felt the mortification of censorship, of the obligatory conformism and those comedies, even the most modest artistically, were all healthy " if only for the sake of independence of taste and intelligence. [...] I remember the riot that shook the Theatre Eliseo at the first of the closed doors of Sartre, presented admirably by MorelliStoppa, for the genius direction of Visconti. The disconcerted people, dragged by the violent theatricality of the text, even in its gruesome immobility, shouted at the end of enthusiasm and rebellion, but lived its fate as a public, realizing itself with a vital participation that actors of my generation had never even conceived. It moved, finally, this shapeless and passive thing that was the audience, bound from the walls that enclosed it and limited it, to become passion, revolt, universal feeling.

At the theater there was no talk of politics or war. Who knows the detachment from everything that actors live in their lives may perhaps mean me. The stage is a sort of floating island where for centuries reality is allowed only to communicate the name of a good restaurant or a good hotel; as among sailors who stop in unknown ports to resume an eternal journey, the only interest in the mainland is represented by some necessary speculation to survive. [...] then they go up on the raft of the stage, closed to every other voice and study the part, try the part, [...] The unreal life of idols without temple is lived; big or petty words are pronounced and, in pronouncing them, the unappreciable, conscious torment of one's inadequacy begins. One is deeply united by the tension of achieving an expression of which only the impossible perfection is intravvede: that between us and that perfection, lies the clumsy limit of our body, the obstacle of our members, of the breath of the ansimose, of the blood that tumultuous. The trials are for everyone, even for the best, strict discipline accomplished in true humility.

Nanna hated bourgeois hypocrisy. He had confusing ideas about these things, but grandiose of rebellion and courage. His disgusting speech, his taste for people with resounding vices, was born from a deep hatred of bourgeois conventions and from the pleasure he felt in reviling them. She had known life early, as a child, and at the age of fourteen, beautiful and weird as a foal, she was found to have to obey the hierarchical laws of the stage in one of the most elegant drama companies that delighted Italy. Those famous, exquisite actors understood absolutely nothing of the personality of that young girl with unpredictable, revolting, drizzling, stubborn like a gypsy. They continued to give her obvious parts in bourgeois comedies where they discolored that sculptural diction, as a plautine mime, which flowed from the sound throat, and which she had learned, barefoot, in the streets of Rome, when in the summer they become a triumph of semi-naked children. She was one of them, the Nanna, and all her blood boiled with that malice and that melancholy.

In short, we worked in Cinecittà that day, and no one expected what happened. Everyone spoke carefree, as if they had been there for a normal stop of their ephemeral and hard work. Suddenly I saw the smile that Camerini was making to me from afar, and I found myself immersed in a sudden silence: the radio had begun to transmit Mussolini's voice. At first nobody understood. That voice spoke briefly and was so dry and peremptory that no one, when silent, could believe that he had finished talking, to explain. He was accustomed to the rivers of words, to the invectives, to the threats: nothing, ever, had been more sinister than that silence, after those unheard of words. [...] The radio, always so solicited to transmit the reactions of the crowd, could not this time record the vast silence that went up also from there, from Piazza Venezia, from that sea of men who had also been chosen, summoned, transported there only to applaud. [...] I looked around one by one at the faces that surrounded me; in each, the same desert that was in the air without sound. On the face of Camerini, over there, hit by the sun, there was a astonished desolation. Blasetti had his eyes barred like a mistreated boy. Valenti bit his upper lip and his face was hard, bad.

In the evening the darkening appeared to us as different, darker and insidious. Rome emerged, on that night without moon, white and immense, as a refugee flock under the mestitude of its domes. Pavolini entered almost immediately, serious, his curious forehead, stubborn and unhappy under the black hair that, with the mustache, interrupted the pale face. I had only seen him once before, but I remembered him clearly. With a courteous gesture he invited me to sit down but I, vehemently, hit him with crowded words, the inflamed face, the eyes that filled me with tears [...] "Go there, Your Excellency, go to that hospital to see with your eyes the ignoble mockery to which those thousands of mutilated, dying people are exposed. Go and see the faces of the officers. [...] I was almost at the end of my explosion. And as I was exhausting the arguments of that rebellion, I heard myself say, "They will hang you." They can only hang you, don't you understand? I was done. Almost waking up, I looked my silent interlocutor in the face with a heart beaten with dismay. What did I do? What would happen to me now? [...] The minister moved slowly and went to sit on a low chair, his elbows on his knees, his face resting on his closed fists. I stood still where I was silent. [...] He looked at me for a long time as to contemplate me curious, then, sitting there, almost on the ground, he said slowly, and his voice was beautiful, soft: "He is right. He's right. In Bologna, as everyone knows, lives the most mystical and lyrical among contemporary painters. Morandi. He paints in his own bedroom; on a table his bottles, absorbed, wait for him to draw from them and from the spaces that separate them, the music of serene mestizia in which they will appear in the luminous and compound paintings. At the window a rigid curtain placed like a sail, dosing in the various hours of the day the reflection of light granted to the model objects on the table, among which, often, is the life of some flower. On the ground, with chalk, are drawn the footprints of the master's feet, so that, if he must move, he can find the exact point of light and perspective. That's all, the universe of this great artist. To paint its landscapes, it drew inspiration during the usual summer holiday, from a modest village, not far from Bologna, named Grizzana. [...] As soon as Sandrino [Bonaccossi] was in Bologna, he naturally sought Morandi, to pay homage to him. The painter received him in his room of life and work, among the dust of the bottles, dinoccolato, maidenly, full of shy, festive kindness. He noticed very little of what had happened in Bologna all that time. Instead, he was very concerned about what might have happened to Grizzana. [...]

Carlo Levi had said at the time of the allied victory: "And now what do we do? [...] The mental habit of living according to the stimulus of such a great opponent to fight which had been necessary every resource of energy, intellectual, ideal, had made the antifascists armed only for supreme struggles. The position of the Italian anti-fascist was a generous, heroic, idealistic position, far from any concrete political program. Especially for young people, it was difficult. They had never posed the problem of having to happen suddenly to the legacy of a government, irreparably spoiled in its bureaucratic structure, at a time when the presence of the allies, the testing of democracy made it very risky. They had no memory of previous democratic governments. [...] They had fought against fascism since adolescence, isolated, looked at you with suspicion and some rebuke from their families. In some of them the anti-fascist rebellion had arisen late, already under military shoes, among the atrocities of that megaloman war. They had gone to fight after September 8 in partisan formations, controversially forgetting their military degrees, their cultural premises, living only in the ideality of that common struggle. Instead, they found the old strong and serene as if nothing had happened. [...] Elections passed: the Christian Democrats won.  [...] The slim flame of the MSI sprang up. In Parliament, they sat down overflowing men who had killed, persecuted, praised, the most horrible events that had ever offended the civilized world. They sat beside the exiles, the anti-fascists, as in their homes. Some of them had spent, as atonement, a few months in prison or in the countryside and boasted of it as a martyr. Next to them sat the quailists, the macabre jesters of our tragedy. Young people weren't there. [...] Italy found itself so, after its long fearful adventure, still in the hands of politicians. His democratic expression, after so much blood, bore the MSI, the Qualunquism. Even democracy, so badly conquered, mocked martyrs. The peers disappeared from the scene.

With Nanna [Anna Magnani] the orgy of freedom became frenzy, in Rome. Brilliant magazines were composed for her, who animated her with a pathos and a conturban irony. Erompeva in Rome, from the torture of one of the most unhappy and intelligent peoples, an explosion of truth that would have disappeared forever, and not only in the field of scenic expression, the last misunderstandings of an empty aestheticism. [...] It was the Nanna, from those naked stages, who launched that message. From a film shot with her clandestinely, when the nightmare of the German occupation was still chilling the blood, that great face of her conscience and pain, came, crossing the sea and the borders, all over the world; and the people of the world, troubled, welcomed the story of pity: to Nanna, De Sica, Rossellini, Visconti, Zavattini, we owe a great spiritual moment of our time, we must, perhaps, if our mortified suffering, our misery, they had interpreters able to make her understand and love after so much isolation. Brunette and powerful she sat among all looking around with phosphorescent flashes of the gray eyes, in paradoxical attitude of rest, the half-open mouth in a half-smile generous and bitter. [...] For the solicitation of someone, he stood up like a cat taken away from his torpor and slowly thoughtful, almost solemn, he walked towards the scene; the subtle life, erected on the vast kidneys, the living hair, twisted in conturban disorder. At its entrance, from the audience, through the scenes, came something that was not noise, but as the anguish of a tumultuous held by an irresistible silence. Only the voice, the thousand voices of the Nanna, burlesque, infantile, brunettes of sudden sadness, were intrigued by that silence and charged more and more with emotion. The wide gesture of the arms raised above the head sad and haughty, seemed to conclude by freeing it, all the intensity that his words and his song had evoked until that moment. The audience exploded, then, with drags. He asked for innumerable encores, especially of the Romanesque refrains where for the first time he heard criticism, mocking them, all those things that had tortured him until then. And the deliberately nasal voice of Nanna, as a Roman brat, insinuated, stimulating the pasquinata, the motto, the rebellion. Then, in a moment, his face drizzling in a grotesque lazzo stretched out in the vastness of melancholy; his voice rose from the dark depths of the chest, and all those people passed from rice to crying with the same prodigious changeableness of the artist. [...] I led Massimo Bontempelli once. He too had a deep impression. How can you maintain that lucidity of diction, that highly supervised scenic intelligence in so much abandonment? He was wondering.

The work on the set in those years was massacred because of the very small budgets: a film was shot in just over thirty days, wrapped in a huge amount of light, because the film was not sensitive, and a frightening heat. I was getting up at 6:00 in the morning. At 7:00, the production machine would pick me up, then makeup and stage dresses. I was ready at the theater around 8:00. Sometimes, at the end of the day, after twelve hours with a single interval, we were asked for two or three extraordinary hours, unpaid, of course, because there were no unions to defend us.

Even today, if I think about it, I relive the terrible jealousy that caught me as soon as we were on the beach and [...] I saw [Clara Calamai] move in swimsuit: Clara was already the fatal femme that soon would have exploded in movies like La cena delle scoffe and I thought it was the best that a man could want. I felt I was worth less, like one of those wretched girls I played on the screen.

The film industry of that time imposed me roles as a simple, poor girl, and alas rather unfortunate: often those I loved married the others, those who married me, they did it for fallback, after giving up on the richest and brightest girls; sometimes my life died tragically, I could not get rid of this character [...] My character as a strong, determined woman, and, I believe, very courageous, is rimast[o] totally unexplored[o].
