He preached a bold and wild project, a beautiful death [...] And we women behind, with peeled stiletto heels or cork soles, bundles and old furs, without, whining but not even illusions. [...] I had common sense left. [...] I did not let myself be enchanted by the gloomy triumph of the Milanese "days" of December 1944 when Benito Mussolini gave the last speech at the Teatro Lirico. [...] "It's forty thousand!" said Sandro. "Forty thousand skeletons," I thought.

For the rest of the winter I continued to rumble on the nebulous Valtellina project without daring to ask Sandro where we would live up there, whether in huts or military tents; what we would feed on; if I too would have to shoot myself. The more I thought about it, the more the "good sense" suggested that I didn't think about it. The sleeping pills and the Campari, always my good friends, helped me.

The program of my existence was more than ever loving and palatable; but in order to achieve it at a high level I had to contain myself, pretend backward and humble, insinuating myself in the environments of success without beating before the doors of the powerful to enter.

Her name was Angela Bellomi, but she had always lived in France. She had militated in the Resistance and had been the protagonist of a complicated and passive of messages and money between Italian and French partisans. "One risked his own skin for his homeland and ended up in this way," he said. I forgot to go further: on the other hand, mine was also an absurd affair. Both overwhelmed by the same tide of violence we became friends but I put a condition: that my soul should not break with its prowess from partisan: "Let's keep our ideas and try to live. It didn't go well for me or you anyway.

Maybe I learned how to act. [...] But I certainly had not yet learned to be a diva; my behavior continued to be shy, shy, that of a girl who sought work obstinately but with humility. [...] at the projection room where I tried to go unnoticed and almost hide in one of the last files. That's too bad, because I was running the car too. I risk wasting the effect (and expense) of the dress that the Gori sisters of Via Condotti had advised me: a long black skirt with a large shaded rose tunic and a jacket of tiny black feathers. [...] At this I thought as I saw myself as a nigger on the screen [...] Open-scene applause began, in the dark. When the lights turned on, it seemed to me that the room turned against me: everyone turned their backs on the screen to chant Doris Duranti curled up there at the end. [...] There was no critic who found the film less than wonderful, but only the American Gloria Wander [...] he gave me the compliment I expected: "White or black, you are the sexiest woman in Italy."

The men of the crew, I must say, did not prove to be suitable for the honors and burdens of the imperial climate.As soon as they landed they began to complain about the dust that was mixed with their streams of sweat, they tried to protect their hair glued to the temples with colonial helmets and for the fear of snakes and scorpions they armed with boots in which their feet boiled. Looked like the caricatures of the misfit colonist, the bourgeois in stunned slippers in Eritrea. They would give full satisfaction to the detractors of fascist Italy, I instead, with my beach sandals like Capri, a cotton handkerchief in the head and my polka dots clothes, I was fine. Then at the Asmara, where the air was finer, I felt wonderfully and noticed the truth of what I had read on the Sunday of the Corriere.Our Asmara was a garden. [...] We stayed for ten days in a hotel in the Hello chain. Hello, Italian word became world-wide just as fascism was also indicating the friendliness with which we treated our good natives. Friendliness, reciprocated: n'ebbi the trial during the dinner in the governor's residence where along with us were invited some notable Eritreans, worthy people, who knew Rome, spoke Italian and knew how to stay at the table like us. We had taught something and, at the time of the toast, I said with sincere pride that we actors were also doing something for the empire and for the duke. [...]

At a dance at the Hotel Quirinale, in the middle of summer, I introduced myself with two large circles to the ears, the hair pulled and stopped with two golden combs, the skin tanned. When I danced, I twirled around in a light flower dress until I touched a table with the edge of lace and I realized that two gentlemen were staring at me. [...] "Now Marrabo is becoming a movie" she said to me, "and the protagonist of the book has a face identical to hers. "Was she also an Egyptian queen? "No, she's just a nigger, but she'll become famous. Do you agree to audition tomorrow itself? [...] It was again Santoli, the makeup artist of Cines, who took care of my appearance; but this time it wasn't a game [...] I had to become black and spread on my face a chocolate-colored wax, put a tight suit on me and put on my head a wig of very black spicy hair. In front of the mirror of makeup I saw Doris Duranti turning into a beautiful girl of another race; but inside me the aspiring actress Doris Duranti, who reached the threshold of the most difficult examination of her life, was shocked by anxieties and feelings much stronger than the hope of success. [...] Luckily the audition contemplated a part that corresponded exactly to what I could really do better at that time. Against a backdrop there was a palm of cloth: I had to reach it, sit under it exhausted, take my face in my hands and weep for a long time. I did it so well that in the end the chocolate makeup was soaked in some kind of jam.

The same night, by candlelight (I thought Angela had already fallen asleep) I got up from the bunk, I broke the oil bottle of the massages against the wall helping me with a handkerchief to make no noise and with the bigger fragment I engraved twice my left wrist: a sign of cross that I would carry for the rest of my life. I felt no pain: I went back to the cot, my right hand drew eyes, my left hand abandoned on the floor for blood to drip. That's when I saw Angela stand up, pick up the same fragment and repeat my gesture. We didn't tell each other anything.I was born in spite of those who brought me into the world, I had lived against all the arid rules of society then, and in spite of those who wanted to challenge my destiny I was leaving. [...] Those who try to commit suicide coldly and use rudimentary means to cut their veins, as I did, usually imagine one after he will come, not always lived in the other world. Think of a thread of blood flowing under the door, a cry of alarm, a gesture of mercy in extremis, a pitiful rescue that urges everyone, willing or unwilling, would bring in conscience the remorse of an early death. As I watched that the thread of my blood, good blood, unstoppable, snapped the crack under the door, I was anxiously looking forward to a watchful nun noticing it. I was on the verge of leaving this world in spite of those who wanted to challenge my destiny. I was born in spite of it, and, in spite of it, I wanted to survive.

No one would lend me money to be photographed, so I acted as a thief while promising to return the malt (and mantenni). The victim was Aunt Evelina. I knew that in the dresser drawer he hid a box of purple malacca full of savings and that the key always carried it with him, in an ivory ring. I made a jig with a stocking needle. Do not ask me how I succeeded: the willpower combined with patience and a bit of acumen manage to blow up any lock: certainly it was not easy, but the money to pay the best photographer of Livorno, Miniati, by now I had them. [...] I also told a lie to the photographer Miniati: he knew mine and if he knew that I wanted to pose for the cinema he would either refuse or be a spy. So I invented a story of Carnival and Miniati also by virtue of my innate skill took taste of what he believed a game. After printing the first images, he was so satisfied that he didn't demand a penny as long as I allowed him to display the photos for the showcase of his studio. He did what he preferred: by now I had the photos, the decision to change his life was stronger than ever.

Since the film was supposed to be true African, it was necessary to collect the other actors among the indigenous. If years later the neo-realist cinema invented the actors from the street, we preceded it with those from the bush. [...] Explaining to these people what cinema was was complicated. The interpreters were amazed because, on the most beautiful of the lesson, there was always someone who exploded in an unconsulted laugh. Others, on the other hand, listened to impassible, closed in a severe expression, as a reproach. It was to be hoped that they would become actors. (Afterwards we would have noticed with wonder that they were acting so well, so intensely, that they displeased the professionals.)

The scream with which I had deafened operators, electricians, actors and director on the set of Geneva of the Almieri echoed long enough in the environments of the Cines, he slipped into the gulfs of Naples of â â â â â â â â â â â through oases of cloth, camels of pelouche of life oscillating on rockers, touched the eardrums of the director of Vivere!, Guido Brignone, who offered me a participation in the film. [...] I had a more consistent contract to shoot white Amazons by Gennaro Righelli, protagonist Paola Barbara, a diva. I became friends with her by showing humility my admiration and I discovered that her divine forms contained a very simple and kind soul, [...] I was being shy, I seemed clumsy and uncommunicative, ready to blush, helpful with everyone and not at all anxious to arrive.

When the war broke out, I was six years old when it ended 11. My eyes were full of images I couldn't erase anymore. If I think of the first memories, I feel the bombs, the sound of the anti-aircraft siren, the bites of hunger. And then the cold, the darkest dark. Every once in a while, suddenly, I get scared again. It sounds incredible, but I still sleep with the light on today. First came the Germans, who were our allies at first. In the morning they marched under the house, tall, blond, with blue eyes, and I watched them from the window, kidnapped, divided between fear and excitement. At my look as a child they did not seem bad or dangerous, but the grandparents, who sometimes happened to listen without will, spoke of Jews and deportations, tortures and torn nails, reprisals and betrayals, and gave a very different image. At that point, I ran into the kitchen and questioned them, but they denied it. "No avimm said nothing" answered impassible. The truth is that we were in the eye of the cyclone and we would have noticed it soon. Slowly everything stopped the school, the cinema theater Sacchini, the concerts of the band in the square, everything except the bombs.

If in cinema I prefer the passionate and tragic parts, the strong, emotional characters, in life I would like to be all the opposite: cold, controlled, introverted. That's normal. But normality escapes me, my joy of living, my vivacity, my temperament prevent me. Therefore, I try to assert myself through art, interpreting uncommon characters to which I feel myself led precisely because they are so different from what I would like to be in life. That's it. And that's not a small thing.

Fortunately the fate, close to the obstacles, often makes you find a surprise, a trace of wind that reverses the course, the announcement of a solution. And so it was for us. Goffredo Lombardo's wife, the producer who had invented my art name, had lived a similar odyssey to mine and had run into the right person. A man before a doctor. His name was Hubert de Watteville and he ran the gynecology clinic at the Geneva Cantonal Hospital. He was a tall and very skinny gentleman, in his 60s, aquiline nose and aristocratic ways, a little detached. At first I was disappointed, looking for a welcoming father more than a professional aseptic. But I was wrong. Knowing him, I would have found in him one of the most affectionate and sensitive men I have ever contracted. He himself without children, had poured his desire for fatherhood into work, and the children he helped to be born were also a little his. After studying my case for a long time, he came to far more optimistic conclusions than I expected. There's nothing wrong with her, she's a normal woman. Next time she gets pregnant, we're gonna have to monitor her closely to figure out exactly how to intervene. You'll see, this time everything will be fine.

Charles [Ponte] was thirty-nine years old, twenty-two years older than me, and it was already a name, in the midst of his brilliant career. As he asked me when he came to appear at the end of the contest, he had discovered great divas such as Gina Lollobrigida, Sylva Koscina, my beloved Lucia Bosè. Want to take a walk in the park? It is a lovely place, they call it the garden of roses, it will smell...In the meantime it made me rise, laying on my shoulders my light shawl of organza. "There we are again," I told myself, preparing myself within myself to reject the usual advances. In fact, Charles was very professional and instantly gained my trust. He told me about the movies he was making, he asked me about me, he sounded my intentions. Where are you from, miss? Ah, Pozzuoli? If I'm not mistaken, there's a beautiful Roman amphitheater... I was there a few years ago.................................................................................................................................................................................................................................................................................................................................................................................................................................................................................................. From the very beginning he communicated to me an extraordinary sense of security and familiarity, as if we had always known each other. I had the strange impression that he understood me, that he had read behind my impetuous beauty the traces of a reserved character, a difficult past, a great desire to do well, with seriousness and passion. It wasn't a game to me, it was a lot more.

Three years later Marcello would also disappear [Mastroianni edr.]. The last time I saw him was in Milan. He used to get into his car to go to the theater and try Furio Bordon's last moons. I was on my way to the airport. Marcello gave me a very long look, perhaps guessing that we would never see each other again. I still have a hard time getting back to the days of his death. The world spoke, exclaimed, attended, celebrated. I was locked in my room, wrapped in my modesty. I didn't want to drain my pain to that of others, I didn't want to flaunt my feelings for media use and consumption. I was just trying to figure out what had happened. On the day of his funeral, I sent a carpet of orchids to accompany him with their delicate freshness. And they brought him my love of all time.

Meanwhile, I lived my life as I could, hidden behind a thin but tenacious veil of shyness. Yes, it is difficult to believe, but I was really shy, perhaps also because of our condition. My father was not there and my mother was too blonde, tall, alive, and above all not married. Her eccentric beauty and above the lines embarrassed me. I dreamed of a normal and reassuring mom, with black hair, crumpled apron, ruined hands and tired eyes.

As I started to relax, I let an innocent joke slip away. Or maybe not... Cary sent me a bunch of yellow roses before I left. Jealousy yellow? How cute he is......Charles turned around and printed me a slap, in the eyes of everyone. Avvampai, the white print of his fingertips on my red cheek burned with anger and shame. I felt tears coming down one by one on my face. I felt like I was dying, but inside of me, I knew that somehow I was looking for him. And I don't regret it. At the age of twenty one must learn to live, and Cary's love had given me much. Maybe even the courage to fight for a normal life with Carlo. On the other hand, I was young but not stupid. I knew that that slap, today as it is difficult to understand, was the gesture of a man in love, who had been threatened by another man, had risked losing me and only now he was recovering from fear, from sorrow. I cried but not too much because the plane was full. The hostess came up and asked me shyly if I needed anything.

All of a sudden I had become a diva, with a press office dedicated to spreading my image to the best, but inside me I was always a little girl with dark eyes who wanted to become an actress, a woman who wanted to get married and have children, like all other women. I lived up and down like everyone else, and I worked with passion and discipline, as always. I grew up inside my little big fairy tale as I built it day after day, row after line, page after page.

The first to believe in me for Sin that it is a rogue was Suso Cecchi D'Amico, the only woman in the parterre of the great screenwriters of the time. The oak that has flourished among its branches as much Italian cinema as Lina Wertmüller defines it. Reading the Roman Stories of Moravia, Suso had come across a beautiful story that was right at the beginning of the novel II fanatic. I remember meeting one day on the train. After seeing me at the station, he came to my compartment and sat beside me, with a confidence that immediately made me comfortable. "I have in mind a subject that is right for you," he simply said, looking straight into my eyes. Why not?I immediately responded with my usual momentum. Suso had worked on the story with Flaiano, Moravia and Continence, and they had proposed it to Blasetti, who at the time was looking for the subject for a film. [...] After our brief train meeting Suso, supported by Flaiano and Blasetti himself, insisted with the producers until he got the part for me.

The contractions intensified, as if in labor, and I was as yellow as a lemon. My mother, who had joined us, threw herself at him [the gynecologist, ed.] with all his strength: "Can't you see his face? He's losing the baby! But it was useless. His cocktail couldn't wait. When the pain suddenly stopped at four o'clock in the morning, I realized that it was all over. It was Ines who called him, in front of the accomplished fact. He arrived calmly, around six o'clock, and was to say the least tranchant: "Madam, she certainly has beautiful hips, she is a beautiful woman, but she will never have a son." His words made me feel incapable, arid, deeply inadequate, and destroyed all my hope. That's not fairy tale. My life was taking a sad drift and no future. The newspapers, who did not waste time waving our pain to the four winds, made everything more difficult.  Now I can go back to the set to finish the movie I told Carlo, as soon as he stepped into my room directly from the airport. I was trying in vain to be brilliant, to be tough. His smile grabbed himself in a smile, letting all his sense of impotence leak out. Only then did I let myself go, abandoning myself to a harrowing cry that no longer ended.

[...] with the money earned a million lire, Mommy, Mary and I moved to a larger apartment in via Balzani and I could finally redeem my sister's honor. My father, if ever for a moment had been, had definitely disappeared from my life, I didn't try to love him anymore. That's why it was not difficult for me to turn Aida's cache into a last name: for me an empty shell, for my sister salvation. Maria Villani became Maria Scicolone and she could finally move on, go back to school, start living. Needless perhaps to dwell on the continuation of the sad story. I was safe, by the way. By my means, as I could, I had retraced our family history, to understand what seemed to me as a child too big to deal with. And I came to my conclusion. Not all men were like Riccardo Scicolone, not all events had to repeat themselves. By my side, I wanted a different person who could make me really happy. I still didn't know for sure, but life would soon prove to me that I, my ideal man, had already met him.

It was during the shooting of One particular day that Riccardo Scicolone died. One morning, my sister called me on the set, in tears: "Sofì" come now, Dad is sick. I ran to the hospital and around her bed I found the women of her life. Mommy, Maria, his last German companion. I approached and shook his hand. He was staring at me. And I supported his gaze, as paralyzed. I smiled at him, then walked away to the window where Mary was crying. I looked outside. Facts from above, cars, passers-by, bicycles seemed nothing but toys. I tried to cry, too, but I couldn't.

Carlo [Bridges ed.] He had learned that Stanley Kramer, the producer of Mezzogiorno fire, was in Spain, where he was preparing to shoot a costume drama on Napoleonic wars. At first, there had to be Brando and Gardner. But Marlon had pulled back, Sinatra had taken over, but he was on course with Ava... I mean, the usual Hollywood mess. The only certainty was Cary Grant, who, however, by contract had the right of approval of the partners and certainly would not have satisfied himself with having at the side of an almost unknown, moreover Italian. Charles, as usual, did not get discouraged: he gave a telephone call to Kramer and invited him to Rome to show him The woman of the river. And the director, as soon as the screening was over, anticipated him, taking him by surprise. What about $200,000 for Miss Loren? I think it would be perfect for the part. Carlo didn't break down, he let a handful of seconds pass and then he replied with a certain detachment: "Interesting, let me think about it, I have to verify his next engagements, but in principle I would say yes, it can be done." "It was the easiest decision of my life," he commented that evening, waiting for Stanley to join us for dinner. He was a star's cache. Now it was about earning it.

And there the experience begins, when you learn to believe in yourself, to treasure your successes and mistakes.

Too bad that she was a villain, Marcello and I met for the first time in a beautiful black and white and fell in love with each other immediately. Cinematographically, of course. I remember the first time I saw him again. I was on the stairs of the palace where we would turn, and he, from a few floors up, would look at me. Hello, he greeted me as if it were in the air. Hello, I replied, shy and excited.Ours was an immediate harmony, which remained intact until the end, without ever a crack. In that first set we met in the background of the happy and luminous Rome of the post-war period, where people had resumed to travel, to go to the sea, to take a bath, to love and to have fun. We were good together, Marcello and I. What a couple! Simple, beautiful, real.

They gave me a single cell that had, as the president of the Republic of Pertini pointed out, even the luxury of the television set and advised me not to attend the other prisoners: in fact I was in a delicate situation that could become dangerous. However, I tried to pass on to those unlucky girls affection, kindness, hope, and when I left, I greeted them one by one. I had tried firsthand, although for a short time, what some of them would have to live for years and, with the help of the nine beautiful nuns who cared for us, I wanted them to know that I would never forget them. And so it was. In prison I discovered that time changes pace, it swells with dark thoughts, it becomes bitter. I tried to tame it by reading, observing, sometimes cooking. And especially by writing.

Today, compared to my time, women are probably luckier, they are judged on the basis of what they can do more than on their appearance. But, by increasing the choice, difficulties in balancing family and work also increase. The world is more complex, more demanding, and requires great sacrifices from girls, proportionate to the satisfactions. And yet, in the end, you always come back to the same point and each of us deals with itself. [...] I have said it so many times, and I repeat it today: it is them, my children, my best films. And it is their happiness that honors me most.

Lombardo was responsible for my artistic baptism. ==References====External links==* Official website He needed a short name, easy to pronounce, that had a certain allure. It was so that, staring at a poster hanging behind her, where the beautiful Swedish actress Marta Toren was standing, she made a round of the alphabet until she stopped at the Soren, Toren, Loren... Yes, we are: Sofia Loren! As he was renamed me, he replaced Sofia's "effe" with the "ph" and "voilà," an international star name. Too bad Pozzuoli would call me "Sopia" forever, not understanding the reason for this useless change.

I presented myself, all cheated up, to the address that Ponti had given me and found myself in front of a command of the carabinieri. My arms fell: the instinctive distrust, which perhaps instilled my own mommy, immediately made me think of a bad joke. There he is, he's teasing me. That makes the producer like I do the dancer!I felt mounting anger mixed with a bitter sense of humiliation. How could I believe him? Stupid fool!The thought ran immediately to my father, and to the pussies with which he had lured Romilda into his spies. Thank God, though, stories don't always have to repeat the same, and mine was still to be written. The young Carabiniere thought of it to reassure me. If you're looking for the production house PontiDe Laurentiis, it's the door next door.I felt silly and childish, I thanked with a big smile and I got ready to enter the heart of Italian cinema. I'd have turned 17 in a few days.

During the shooting, Carlo called me to give me some bad news. I was at the hotel. We had just finished filming. "Sophia..." "What is it?" Why is that voice? It's... it's Marilyn's death, it's now known. Barbiturates. They talk about suicide.I remained silent, hung on the phone, without knowing what to say, so much so that Charles got restless. He knew well that behind that rigorous and determined face of mine there was an emotional woman hiding. "Sophia, are you there? "Yes, yes, of course, where do you want me to go... "That so early, so ambiguous death troubled me in the depths and made me think a lot. I let myself be invaded by a thousand swirling and confused images. I thought about the sense of beauty, the loneliness, the need to feel loved that is in the heart of each of us. Relive that seductive, veiled smile of nostalgia. It's not enough to be the most beautiful woman in the world to be happy. Marilyn was a great actress, crushed by the weight of her talent, by men who had asked her for everything without giving anything in return, or who had pretended to transform it according to their tastes. She couldn't find her way. I felt a thrill, as if a shadow rested all around. Ours is a cruel world, which feeds itself, is content with appearance and rarely wonders what's underneath it. That's why it's up to us to keep the fairy tale anchored in life, not to lie to ourselves who we are, where we come from. Marilyn's charm ended up destroying her by reducing her to a joyless sex Symbol.

I dream that I am on a beach, at sunset, and the sea is very calm, immense, smooth, similar to an exterminated blue satin. The sun is as red as fire and it's going down. All of a sudden I'm gonna run to the beach, and I'm gonna run. Then, always running, I wake up. Moravia reads it like this, "in the manner of the Chaldean wizards who in the end contemplate the dreams of Nebuchadnezzar": the sea is normal, which I try in vain to reach. The sun is my success. I could stand still and watch the tranquillity of the sea and instead I want to chase my sun. And like all those who want to reach the sun, I have a long way to go, but I do it because that sun, while being far away, comforts me and illuminates me in my race.

When the nomination arrived at the end of February 1962, I didn't want to believe it. "L" Oscar? The Oscar Prize?I was rereading the names of the other candidates, passing me in front of Audrey Hepburn and Natalie Wood, Piper Laude and Geraldine Page, and between me and myself I said: "What, are we kidding? In addition, La ciociara is an Italian film, played in Italian, when have you ever seen it? Yet, I was flattered at the idea, it made me feel good, I thought it would be enough for me to get there. Inside me, however, I knew that it was not true, every step I climbed underneath made me dream more and more of victory. Maybe I was too daring, but there was nothing to do. Hope and ambition were part of me, although I knew that disappointment was around the corner and the triumph was destined for a few. After a lot of time, I decided not to go to the premiere. If I had lost, I would have fainted. And if I had won, I would have passed out anyway. I couldn't afford it, in front of that audience, under the gaze of the whole world. "I'm staying in Rome, on my sofa," so I told myself, and so I did.

It was Vittorio [De Sica], once again, who led me on this adventure: "Cesira is a mom all round. He is humble, he has always worked and lives for his daughter. His approach to things is simple and direct. You've already tried all this on your skin, Sofi. You know exactly what we're talking about. You're gonna play without makeup and no tricks. Be yourself, become your mother, and everything will be fine. After so many years of Hollywood, La ciaciara brought me home to the harshness of my childhood. The war, which had long been buried within me, now came back out to give voice to this wounded woman, to her suffering, to her courage. I was thinking of Mommy, how he fought to defend us, how to get us water and food. How it was supposed to feel those nights when Moroccan soldiers camped in the hall knocked drunk at our door. To how he kept silently guarded, without being noticed, the young American marines who came to our living room to drink the brandy. Because he knew that danger was lurking everywhere, at any time, even where you think you're safe. When they introduced me to Eleonora Brown, the little girl they had chosen to make Rosetta, I immediately felt responsible for her, for us. He had a shy, smart face, and together he was waiting for a hard job. How could I be his mother? Suffering for her? How could I help her trust me? My instinct moved me to look at her with the sweetness with which I felt watched as a child, with the love from which I was protected and caressed. And it worked.

1954 had been a year full of changes, surprises, rapid and irreversible growths. I had met De Sica and Marcello [Mastroianni], I had been directed by Blasetti and Soldati, I had sung and recited with great masters, I had gone from comedian to tragic without losing my identity and had consolidated my relationship with Carlo. I was becoming a star, and the world was starting to talk about me. In the spring I had been for the first time in Cannes to present Carosello Neapolitan, the only great film by Ettore Giannini, considered the only Italian musical able to compete with those Americans, in which I had done a small part singing, dubbed, "O surdato" (O surdato) '.In June I had also been in Berlin, where a famous photo portrays me alongside Gina Lollobrigida, along with Yvonne De Carlo. In October, on my return from Comacchio, it was the turn of London, where I was invited to the Italian Film Festival.

We went away for a moment, looking for a refuge to talk away from the spotlight. There, in that repaired corner of the set, Marcello looked at me mischievous, like a young boy who thought it big. I stared at him having fun, fearing the worst. "What do you say, Sophia, do our striptease again?" "You're such a scoundrel..." "I answered pretending to be shocked. Actually, the idea was really annoying to me. I had left behind the insecurity of the past, I no longer needed the experts of the Crazy Horse, the lesson of De Sica I carried inside me. As for the passing of the years, I had learned with experience to make time my best ally: I didn't contrast it, I didn't suffer it, I lived every day for what it was, leaving, like Mona Lisa, my beauty to mature in peace. Marcello and I returned to Altman with the certainty that we were on the right track. He looked at us, he understood and shouted, "Action!" Although fate had separated us in the work, geography, customs, all this by charm vanished in the moment of turning. We were young again for a few hours, ready to bite life, to love it. The striptease of Mara in Yesterday, today, tomorrow, redone a thirty years later on the same languid notes of Abatjour, welcomes with a smile the flow of age and discovers in the vulnerability of each of us the true juice of existence.

"Put this on," said the technician, tending me to a swimsuit, with such sudden ways that they left me breathless. And to think I was there with the producer. "I wonder how they would treat me if I was here alone," I asked myself with horror. I changed behind the screen and went back in front of them. I felt naked, and my shyness burned like a wound. Indifferent, the cameramen lengthened a cigarette, ordering me to light it and then to walk back and forth, looking at the target. I had never smoked before, nor had I ever been alone in front of a camera. I felt that I was completely inadequate, and the operator seemed to agree with me. "Dotto" is impossible to photograph. It has too short a face, too wide a mouth, too long a nose!It's like always, I looked too much again. And yet I was like this, what was my fault?

\u003cp style="textalign:justify;"\u003eFor years and years I had kept a personal diary, a protected fence to let myself go completely to myself, as if it were a small inner camera in front of which to recite finally my whole part. I started in prison, and I hadn't stopped. In the solitude of writing, I found comfort and companionship and discovered the veins of my unfamiliar voice. I felt safe in my intimacy, as if only there I could really be home. But life, you know, goes down steps, and one spring morning, suddenly, I looked at myself in the mirror and I was afraid. "What will happen to my diary when I'm gone?" I said to myself looking a little further. I am a woman who, albeit emotional and sometimes vulnerable, knows how to make her decisions. I said to Ines and Ninni, and I went into my room. I watched for a long time that black notebook, with a hard cover, which had been a companion of so many thoughts, of so many emotions. I browsed it slightly, without haste. Turning the pages, I smelled the scent of the years, I felt my mood changes from the differences of the handwriting, now harsh, now nervous, now more relaxed. Finally, I went to the bathroom and got a can of matches. One small gesture was enough, and my words became fire, and then ashes. I had no regrets but, at times, a little nostalgia. And I didn't stop writing. From that time, however, every end of the year I entrust myself to a match, as if it were the magical instrument of a small rite between me and myself.

The more time passes, the more I come to speak in Neapolitan. Perhaps because in Neapolitan I express myself better, I can say things that I cannot say in Italian, much less in English or French. I put so much love in this language that even my children understand me when I speak it, and now even my grandchildren. The same goes for traditional dishes, which take me home, in the kitchen of Via Solfatara. There I spent my days, among the scents and aromas of poverty. There the singing of mother and the warmth of the stove kept me company and, when there was money, the ragù muttered in the pot. Today I don't cook often, I eat little, always caught between thousand thoughts. But when my children come from America and ask me for a special recipe, they bury me in my kingdom and I feel again in Pozzuoli. The task that gives me more satisfaction is the Genoese, those five kilos of onions browned to become flies, and inside the meat rolls, to cook for four hours. In this life that has become so fast, four hours is an infinite time, what I need to go back to my years until my distant childhood.

The real paradox is that even Giuliana [Fiastri], the first wife of Charles [Ponte], desired her freedom and, as a lawyer, studied the situation in search of a way out. Three times the Bridges asked for the annulment of the Sacred Rota, and three times it was denied to them. The only solution to get us out of the trouble of bigamy seemed to be the annulment of the Mexican marriage, which, however, represented more a step backwards than a step forward. I suffered so much, but at the bottom of my heart I had a clean conscience and, no matter how upset I was by all the public and private accusations that were raining on us, I knew I was right. I felt married, and that had to be enough for me, even if it was not always easy to live with the pillory, branded with infamy. On the doors of the churches were posted posters that put my films to the index and at the same time invited the faithful to pray for our sinful souls. We were filled with often ferocious letters, the worst of which came from a group of women from Pozzuoli. Feeling attacked from my city, from the heart of my childhood, from my land, caused me a deep wound, from which I took a long time to recover. Italy was divided between those who were with us and those who were against us. It was the Italy of Coppi and the White Lady, Rossellini and Bergman, a bigoted Italy that did not have a long future. Ten more years, and everything would have been resolved by the divorce referendum. But then we were still far away and we only had to suffer without being annihilated.

All the stages of my life and career are marked by my fierce will to gain protection and security. I had to fight a lot, especially on the level of my private life, but every time I won what I wanted, and it was never about whims or chimeras. Food is the symbol of security, along with the roof that shelters us. So it's a symbol that, always accompanying me, has become sacred to me.

Even man, today, can establish a new lovable relationship, solve a misunderstanding, a conjugal rust, taking his wife by the throat.............................................................................................................................................................................................................................................................................................................................................................................................................................................................................................. May the man occasionally put himself at the stove, and enjoy cooking, and may boast of a successful dish: here is a beautiful picture of carefreeness and family serenity. [...] The men who say they are "all" ancient, but who in the past have learned nothing and for the present are limited to ignoring that the woman now works and is present in every field of social activity those men will be struck, if not scandalized, by these my reflections. "How" they will say "a man in apron, and between the stove, just like a little woman in the house!" Well, yes. In fact, they are the ones, with their ideas inadequate to the evolution of society, who would need this, let's say, culinary therapeutic. With their ideas, in fact, their family menages must be teeming with problems, and how! Precisely to them, that is to say to these husbands who are still recanted to the image of the modern woman, I address my invitation to search and try among my recipes. They'll be grateful. I'll bet you anything.

Going back to the years of my very early childhood, when there was war, I convinced myself more and more than in the horror and difficulties of that period I met two things, two certainties that a child absolutely needs to live: protection and nourishment. It seems absurd to talk about each other when every day, now for now, we lived under bombs and massacres and we were hungry from morning to evening. But there, in Pozzuoli, where I was with my mother and my grandparents, every day there was a ritual that could satisfy those two needs of certainty. Each evening he expected the last train to pass, and then all the people of the country would pour into a long railway tunnel, where the next train would only pass at the next sunrise. We would go in there full of blankets and any rag that could keep us warm, form beds and go to sleep. A closed and heavy air, and so much frightened humanity. But in those hours under the tunnel you felt protected, and not only because you believed it to be bombproof, but because we were all there, next to each other, breathing the same air, taking courage, even finding the game and the joke. It seemed to me that I bought a huge family, my protector angels multiplied by ten, percent: safe and protected, with such a precise perception as never again would I have had in my life. Today I understand that that tunnel, deep down, was for me the peace of the unborn child in the womb.

[On Franco Interlenghi] It was in fact at that moment of our life that we decided not to make movies together anymore, proposing every time the usual couple also on the screens. I could not remain for eternity the naive number one and he forever the boy of his beginnings. We chose to channel ourselves into other stories, with other people, just to look for new characters more suitable for the times we met. It was a necessary choice.

[About Stelvio Cipriani] Our feeling depended on me and my need for freedom. Stelvio was good at understanding what my needs were at that precise moment, and so he always behaved as I had long wanted by a man, allowing me to do everything that seemed right for me. Actually, not that I did much. But I just had the idea that I could be autonomous, that I had the chance to manage my time and not to feel suffocated anymore.

At some point in my career I rebelled against this naive image of water and soap, because everything has its time and a change happens when you least expect it. I realized that I could no longer be the one who was being piloted and put there as if it were a puppet. I needed to assert myself as a person and no longer as the one to be guided, by the parents before and by a husband after. I started to say mine and it was a trauma in the family, because Franco didn't know me as a reactive and autonomous woman, but I couldn't do it anymore and I had to change, in any way.

Of course, Franco was also involved in these choices of mine. Although he never came to the set, avoiding official exposure, he worked first on the script, when there was reading the character, and according to that, once the terms were established and signed the contract, he eliminated the nude scenes. If the production accepted, it was quiet, because I was protected by my signature and that of the production.

It is said that with psychoanalysis, retracing the especially dramatic events of one's own life, one can heal from depressions. I'm going to say a big "thank you" to the writing of this book: to go back with memory to my and Franco's history in all its aspects was for me a real catharsis. It eased me from the oppression I fell into after my husband disappeared.

Some of my Italian colleagues chose the easiest route by getting engaged or marrying important producers, i.e. those who at the time had the power to make great films. I made a different choice and paid her dearly. I was among the most famous Italian actresses and demands of the time, I spoke French better than all and often called me to go to work in France. However, when they found out about some co-production, the boyfriends, companions or close friends of my colleagues would step in and say that they would contact me in Italy. [...] They told me to be quiet, that everything would go well and then disappear. After years, I learned from the French productions that while they were dealing with these gentlemen to have me, they heard that I was busy, that I was working or that I was in America, instead proposing their actresses.

I was a little obscured by Franco's personality, but being in the shadows didn't cost me much. My husband fascinated me and I liked to talk about him. So I didn't tell a lot about myself, because what he was talking about seemed more important to me. During the meetings with journalists I rarely opened my mouth, I let Franco do it, also because I liked what he said very much. He was always ironic, did not take himself seriously and purposed with much simplicity. He captured his way of being, even on the set, where he didn't take himself seriously and purposed very simply. [...] He was the man and the protagonist, and he was always at the center of attention. But I, just like the women of the Middle East, spoke little and often remained silent for a long time. However, I was very lively and dynamic, a side of my character that binds to the vitality of Greek women, just as I like to trace my appearance back to the Italian roots. This mixture was fascinating for those who came to interview us and pushed them to discover some of my hidden sides, which was apart from my relationship with Franco.

In this period love remained in the second line, also because I still had the burns of the first wretched crush and I did not want to fall back. I couldn't even afford it: I was too focused on the construction of my character. This, however, does not mean that I practiced abstinence. For the undersigned, sex is a physical necessity that must not necessarily have brain or sentimental implications. And like food: with your head or without your head, you must always eat.

The Rome of those years of center-left was much more sophisticated and enjoyable. Many dinners, many relationships between politics and entrepreneurship, entertainment, journalism and state apparatus. [...] [Politics and cinemas often met] at home MiloErgas [...] attic and superptych: the ease in those years overflowed in the important houses. [...] Ergas was a very famous producer, very quoted and very important.

In the cinema my career [...] had not been fast at all. I was a bigger girl, as we then called ourselves girls with prosperous breasts and opulent hips [...] but I had an ungracious voice, as a child. What about the face? Vittorio De Sica claimed that mine were dramatic features. [...] The svampita was born with [...] Adua and the compañeras, a name that brought me luck.

I want to tell you my story, the story of those who without will found themselves fighting against the political power, the judicial power, the religious power. Always living first person the years before terrible, then splendid of a very beautiful and coveted woman that this beauty used, when served, without shame and hypocrisies.

'60s and then the TV was the poor relative of the cinema. I was the first, among the movie stars, to understand its importance. One evening, live TV came down from the ceiling of the theater, standing on a platform, wrapped in a white dress and singing the song of Lina Wertmuller who was doing: "Too blonde for someone, I'm too blonde to be up here, too round for someone, I'm too round for the TV."

How beautiful and ugly people in the penthouses of Rome with carnation. [...] The representation was this: a sofa with Craxi, I sometimes sat at his side. The people who walked past him to be seen, to beg for a cross of eyes. [...] Those evenings [...] began to disgust me. All those courtiers, the race to prove better than another in the lackey category, servility even in picking up a half smile, were frankly too much. What a circus, the Grand Bittin...

[Vanina Vanini] was a film that Roberto Rossellini had taken from a novel by Stendhal [...] a simple story: beautiful and very young Roman princess (I) falls in love with a beautiful revolutionary (Laurent Terzieff); he died, Vanina locked herself in a convent. The few critics who see the film in preview call it a masterpiece. [...] On the evening of the first at the Palazzo del Cinema at the Lido of Venice [...] everyone gave me as a sure winner of the Volpi Cup. [...] after about twenty minutes the audience begins to noise [...] screams, whistles, invectives: the ladies jeweled and the gentlemen in tuxedos cry out against Roberto [Rossellini] and against me. [...] The next day the newspapers called me "Canina Canini." I was done. [...] I was forced to withdraw: no one wanted me anymore.

Ergas has always been very jealous [...] one evening he crashes me in the trailer finding me with Ottavio [De Lollis]. We were just talking, but it was enough [...] to beat me wildly. The last memory, the last image I have of that evening is his foot on my face. [...] The production [...] places the shelter at the clinic [...] where I am housed underground [...] at the craniolesi department. Yes, there are broken ears, dislocated jaws, swollen eyes, various bruises and a great pain everywhere, but I declare to the doctors amazed that I fell because I tripped into the electrical cables of the set. [...] I think of my daughter with fear [...] [Ergas has turned her away and] does not feel that he has to explain to me: I am neither his wife nor the mother of his daughter.

Eight and 1/2 was the masterpiece of Federico [Fellini] and an amazing worldwide success. [...] I was Carla [...] the character [who] represented the erotic dream, the lover par excellence, buttery, docile, moralistic and futile, complacent, understanding, absolutely devoid of those female claims so annoying. [...] Beautiful, beautiful in the flesh: the secret lover. [...] Expressions such as "Pavoncina" were coined, and "Laughter at Cascatella" were coined, and Camilla Cederna dedicated a large part of her book to her.

I, being still married to Cesare Rodighiero, married when I was fifteen years old, am not his mother by Italian law [...] Debora was a daughter out of marriage, she was a mother's daughter who does not want to be named. [...] Moris Ergas had become my master, whom I had all granted so as not to lose my child. [...] So I thought of writing to Pietro Nenni, who was vice-president of the Council [...]: the Psi had promised the voters to conduct a battle on divorce, he was proposing the most socially advanced party, open on the female issue.

Debora Maria Ergas was born in Milan on February 10, 1963 by Morris Ergas. Enough, the mother canceled, killed, disappeared, shamefully nonexistent. [...] The children are born without the possibility of even having a mother, like my daughter, and mine is probably a case like so many others [...] I can no longer see my daughter because the legal father prevents it, takes away my daughter [...]; and I can do nothing because nothing proves that that is my daughter because the men who guide us and who should protect us have never done anything for the illegal children. [...] I turn to her [Nenni] and I ask for justice [...] I swear that I do not ask it only for myself but for all those mothers who are like me never mothers. [...] I will ask, I will ask with all my strength even at the cost of taking my life if necessary, as long as the silence and mystery surrounding the birth of our children is opened.

Mine wasn't a very comfortable position [...] l eighth on two fronts, I fought two wars that required the same commitment of forces. One took place inside the house, where I wanted to be great as a wife and mother. [...] I had against the many enemies who waited for my mistakes as a symbol, as an actress, of a sensuality that is not identified with the stove. [...] Outside the house, in order to fight against power, I was forced to play the role of the sinful lover perfectly and presented me with the image they wanted. The only one that made me strong. Hate and anger for this constraint unleashed in me a dangerous and exciting force. I planned every meeting and it was always a victory. After each meeting, with the vagina burning, I felt a sense of humiliation, but I couldn't stop. I only had my body to fight. And I used that one.

When I reached the top of the tiny landing, I found myself in front of a small green and gold door. Bettino opened it and appeared in front of me a little girl's bedroom of the nineteenth century. [...] "What is it, you play dolls?"I asked with a smile. Bertino kept silent without smiling and I quickly found myself lying on the bed, with my skirt lifted up, and I felt myself searching long from his hands and then beating from his tongue, and still his hands, and his mouth on my mouth and so many words that I didn't understand. Eventually he was on top of me and the quiet came.

I remember the promenade on a sunny day. It was dotted with rubble of houses standing in half and inhabited by poor people, children with black skin and narrow yellow curls. The mothers had oxygenated their hair to hide its origins, or rather those of their father, some African American soldier. But how beautiful the sea shining, lapping the golden sand, the breeze that caressed my face. [...] Meanwhile, the famous family golds came to an end [...]. Sometimes my mother, like most of them, was prostitutes to the American military.

To which tomorrow can anyone who has before him a few months aspire? For one who has always lived fully the present moment is not so important to project forward. [...] The verdict is without appeals [...] I want to be remembered as a woman who, even in misfortune, made her bulwark. [...] Choosing how to die is an inalienable right of any individual. [...] If I turn back, in an attempt to reorganize my memories, I repeat to myself that there is nothing I would not do again, I would make even the same mistakes, even those have contributed to the construction of my inner self. I have to hurry before the fog wraps them in a narrow vise and takes them away. I must hold them, stop them on paper before they abandon me. [...] I will tell you the true story of a child, a witch, a saint, an unquenchable grass.

Pietrangeli wanted me as the female protagonist of La visita, whose work was carried over the twelve weeks of scheduled shooting, [...] [this happens for] accidental causes and [for] a heart-circulatory collapse that forced me [...] [a] absolute rest for a suspected extrauterine pregnancy. [...] I was so thin and digged that I induced the director, the operator [...] and the makeup artist [...] not to want to film again until I was healthy. [...] Moris Ergas, the producer of the film as well as my partner, accused the director of having consumed too much film [...] On the contrary the director protested with me, seeking my support, and I could not but agree with him. Antonio [Pietrangeli] came to hate so much Moris [Ergas] to pin on the set the nickname of dwarf thigh.

[For the auditions of La risaia] I was rigged, combed, gave me a hat of straw, short shorts, blouse necklined and black stockings hidden torn here and there to art. [...] A long line of girls, all beautiful, dressed like me, waited their turn to pass in front of the camera, say a few jokes, turn a couple of times on themselves and go on another with director Matarazzo who [...] examined it. When it was my turn, I did exactly the same things that the others had done, but with a lot of energy. I wanted that role. [...] I was chosen out of 200 girls. [...] For me the great dream had gone away. And the wait began. [...] [But] my boyfriend had phoned Carlo Ponti [the producer] saying that I gave up my role because I suffered from rheumatism. [...] I was furious. [...] I didn't love him anymore, I thought of Moris [Ergas] as a majority partner. One day I would have been the mistress.

I left for Spain to turn The woman who came from the sea [...] I was very excited to recite next to him [Vittorio De Sica], who also had the merit of being kind and to appreciate me. He said I had dramatic skin, intuiting something tragic that no one else had ever seen.

I know I'm an Oscar actress for my unsurpassed performance in Otto and 1/2 by Fellini. [...] Am I self incensed? No, nothing would bore me more. [...] Cinema has always been my second family. My daily life took place between trilles and small thoughts on the set, in a happy and carefree atmosphere. But not at night, not at night... on my way home, my mother was waiting for me to be paralyzed, dying from cancer. I comforted her as I could [...] but she asked me the same thing every day: to help her die. [...] In the morning the car came to pick me up to take me to makeup and, after the dress, go on to act in the role of the conturban, cheerful, very light Carla. [...] Then one evening my mother asked me, "Why don't you help me die? I would have done it for you. Suddenly my selfishness, my lack of courage, the limits of my love, all I owed to that extraordinary woman who was my mother. And so I decided and helped her swallow the hosts soaked in a deadly dust. [...] It was my last tribute to her. I was close to her,... aware that those were her last moments of life. [...] That's why I consider my participation in Otto and 1/2 from Oscar.

Fellini loved the character of Carla [...] who represented the erotic dream, the lover by antonomasia, so soft, remissive, complacent, almost stupid, free from any irritating feminine claim. [...] she was the perfect lover in charge of men's erotic fantasies, while I didn't go crazy for her. [...] The all male idea that Fellini had of a female prototype made of feathers and violets did not look like me at all. Now it's clear to me why I hated her. I can't think of Carla without associating her with my mother's death. But the role of the lover was in a certain prophetic sense because he moved from the screen to reality.

My career in cinema did not have a lightning ascent [...] and was studded with numerous setbacks. [...] I left Milan and my work as a model to arrive in Rome in search of luck [...] but I could never overcome the auditions [...] My statuary physicist screamed with my voice as a child. They said she was graceless. But it was mine.

[Pietrangeli] was the first one to allow me to dubb myself when all were strongly opposed. My birdish hues in contrast to the opulence of my physique were considered absurd, but he realized that that singing voice was part of my charm. He always respected my personality, never made me look vulgar [...]. He described me in the way that best suited me as absurd 'levity, as a pathetic soul of birdsling who wishes to sing and who is perennially wounded by raptors stronger than him.
Was my strength alone in that crack in the middle of my legs? Of course not! But it was a strong attraction and I didn't forget it anymore. Like sitting on a strong, invincible, dangerous star.

The bachelor with Alberto Sordi, directed and written by Antonio Pietrangeli, [...] began. Pietrangeli [...] hated me and I didn't understand why [...] and humiliated me as he could. [...] The director [...] Franco Zeffirelli [...] gave me suggestions on how to recite the jokes and encouraged me by telling me to think only about my goal: [...] to become a diva, without caring about who despised me. So I did, I no longer took care to save my dignity from the ridicule of those who within me now considered vulgar poor. The film ended and was a great success.

I got a passport registered to Rosa Palmieri [...] I left first. Buenos Aires enchanted me [...] one morning I entered a cinema and watched the movie there were me and a cat [...] [The city] appeared to me as a dilated Paris, much larger. [...] In Buenos Aires I did this mainly: I painted, painted, cooked and sewed sheets, bedspreads, lampshades, pillows. During the day I bought the fabrics and at night I sewed, all strictly by hand. [...] I had a restaurant, it was called "Portorose" and it was in front of the River [a great stadium]. [...] I had succeeded in erasing all my conquests and defeats. I was a new, different woman.

Pina's character, for an actress like me, who had built a career on the adventure, represented freedom. Piero Tosi invented a padded panties to spread my hips and ass and a facial trick to give me the tired air of forty-year-old province from unsatisfactory life, [...] he added tyrabacus that made me look like a poodle and a heart-drawn mouth that remembered Betty Boop. [...] I could prove that I was good without having to look beautiful at all costs. [To Pietrangeli] I was always grateful [...] had written for me the character of Adriana, whose costumes were already ready, in I knew her well but the rental today distribution opposed my engagement [after the disastrous reception of Vanina Vanini] saying that I was not a good actress.

In Vanina Vanini, Rossellini with a handcrafted medium, called a pancinor, that put together the variable focal length through the zoom and the use of the cart, [made the actors act] without pauses allowing the director to grasp every slightest nuance of their facial mimic, avoiding to resort to the detachments [obligating them] to shoot twenty scenes before a good one came. He used it as an experiment in Vanina Vanini, a film with which I was thrown into the mud.

My mother was a beautiful woman with beautiful legs and gray eyes. He belonged to a good family, but he did not share the scruples, so that he immediately became the lover of the podestà [...]. He loved Mussolini and became secretary of the Fascio di Vicopisano, with his beautiful black uniform and the sachet on the twenty-three often went to the conferences of the Fascio, which took place in Rome. [...] My mother's relatives [...] were fascist hierarchs [...] sometimes they came to Vicopisano with their Balillas and were organized sumptuous banquets. I don't have an exhilarating memory of that time. The bombing had begun and we were often forced by the alarm sirens to escape into the nearby countryside. [...] The food was scarce, there was no more oil, the flour had almost disappeared, largely requisitioned by the Germans [...].

Everyone was chasing talents, photogenic faces, lustful bodies. [...] Beauty contests flourished everywhere, like spring primroses. Absolute word of order: beauty. [...] Miss Italia's contest was a nursery of wonderful girls. [...] In short, in that fantastic time a girl, with a swimsuit and an evening dress, could conquer a kingdom in one night. And the Cinderellas were a lot.

Federico started screaming and screaming like a madman. [...]. He gave me some ungrateful, shameless, and many other definitions that I don't think elegant to report. In silence I listened to his river of screaming words, sometimes I looked out the window at the peaks of the pine trees of Cinecittà, which are beautiful [...] After a long time, tired and perhaps disappointed by my lack of reactions, he kept silent.

I haven't changed, I haven't lost the desire to feel, to live everything intensely. I would even like to go through my death, I wish it looked like me, that it didn't suddenly come into my sleep. I want to live it, like a candle that comes to the end and goes out.

My heroic mom, she who had not feared the mines walking in the middle, who had fought hunger; she survived the bombings, the machine guns, she who had rescued her girls and her mother from every danger, she singing "Ohi May, Ohi Mary" laughing at the desire she aroused in men; she from the nylon underwear and pink scented arms, grey in her face and eyes invaded by terror, went away from her home, from her family, with a pack of wolves. Were those the heroes? [...] I began to understand that the truths were many, each different, each adapted to the needs of the moment. [...] In war there are no good and bad, defeated and victorious, all of them come out defeated because people lose their humanity.

My father [...] was one of the last veterans to be released. I was 13 when I saw him again. [...] I don't know why, after waiting so long for his return from the war, I didn't feel happiness; [...] he stayed a little more than a week, he returned to France, where he had always lived [...]. He never came... nor did he give his news. [...] He must have made himself a life again, he must have had another family. [...] I grew up without a father. All I had left of him was my last name. [...] After our abandonment, I grew up in a family of only women.

It is at the end of the fifties, in a world of master men and consenting women, that my desperate and compelling struggle begins with political power, ecclesiastical power and legislative power. I fought using any weapon, from seduction to blackmail [...] I want to talk to you about the passions that I unleashed with my sensuality; about the blackmails to cardinals, magistrates and politicians; about that energy that, relentless, pushed me to fight for me and for my children. I cannot understand the painters who, while saying they are interested in modern problems, are still facing the picture as if this were a surface to be filled with colors and shapes, according to a taste more or less appreciable, more or less catchy. They trace a sign, retreat, look at their work by tilting their head and sucking off an eye, then leaping forward again, add another sign, another color of the palette, and continue in this gymnastics until they have filled the painting, covered the canvas; the painting is finished; a surface of unlimited possibilities is now reduced to a kind of vessel in which they are strained and including unnatural colors, artificial meanings. Why not empty this container? Why not discover the unlimited meaning in a total space, a pure and absolute light?

From my childhood I remember facts and situations very confused, but of a particular I have clear memory. I constantly kept a long bruise at the top of my forehead, just below the hairline. I was thinking, now they're taking me to Triste Villa. Villa Triste was a small villa at the beginning of the road that led to my school, where everyone in the city knew it was questioned and tortured the partisans. But I wasn't a partisan. I was nothing. "Stay calm" I said, "Stay quiet..." Then suddenly, at the first question I understood everything. And the heart down, beating faster. Now he's taking my cuddle and I'm going down. My God, what happened? They were menacing. I don't see him anymore... and I'm standing there with my mouth open like a loo. When they came to the principal's office, they searched my pocket. I was talking to myself imagining my mother's voice: "You're unconscious, you're a wretch. If your father finds out, he'll kill you, and it's good for him. I took a very deep breath, then I said everything out of breath what I had to say and I fainted. But how can you be so fragile and emotional? I could never become a partisan; not even a relay...

It's April 25th, the war is over. In Varese the partisans parade through the square Empire. Among them are guys I know. They were of the first group of fighters who had formed in the mountains of Val Cuvia just after September 8th. They had perched on Mount Grino and Cuvignon. The Germans had attacked them by shooting with heavy artillery, a real massacre, few had survived through the tunnels dug in the First World War; they were almost all inhabitants of the valley, the only ones who knew the path of those ancient walkways. To celebrate liberation, a flood of people had fallen on the streets: at some point from the bottom I see an armed group coming forward. With them there was a woman. She was the mother of one of the fallen of Mount Grino. Many recognized it and applauded it. Someone was going to hug her. Suddenly she stopped and sat on a wall, begging others to continue: the emotion was too great and she could no longer walk between the applause and the shouts.The next day the Allies arrived. We saw them passing through the city on trucks in the afternoon. They were also on my way. They threw chocolate and cigarettes in it. I blush at the thought of jumping with others to try to pick something up.  In the evening, in the courtyard of my house, great party. A gramophone, [...]. Then I look up, towards the dark window of the first floor: my house. More than seeing her, I sense her: my mother is there, she is looking at us. I know his thoughts, his torment: my brother, deported to a concentration camp in Germany, hasn't reported in over two years, in a moment I'm close to her ashamed of my joy. I'm holding on to her.

With the advent of the sound cinema (1929), my father, my uncle and all the company realize that the puppet theatre will soon be put in crisis, crushed by this new extraordinary and also a little magical means of show. To the great pain of Grandpa Pio, they decide a radical change of their program and their condition: "We will perform our shows, we will enter the stage, instead of puppets." Thus the two families of the Copper are replaced by wooden puppets (true and own articulated sculptures, three of which are still exhibited today at the Museo della Scala in Milan). They chose the texts, tried them and tried them again, they got the costumes and great backdrops painted for them by a stage designer friend of La Scala and they debut in a normal theater: the theater of "persona" and she, my mom, becomes the first actress. An actress who was raising her children during the day, helped them study, took care of the house, kept the administration of the company as if it were a normal family ménage. And in the evening, climbing the stage, here it is transformed into Juliet and Tosca, and the White Sister of the Sons of no one, and the Fantina of the Miserables, all roles that we daughters and cousins, one after the other, would then interpret. I see myself going through the theatrical apprenticeship by interpreting all the roles that growing up were suitable for my age, whether male or female.

At some point in my life I realized that the work of an actress didn't drive me crazy, that I didn't love him so much. People don't believe it, they think it's lies. But it is true, I would never have chosen this profession, which requires a type of character that I do not have: presencealism, arrogance and good elbows. When I realized I didn't love this job, it was too late, I was already married to Dario and we had our company. If I hadn't been addressed to this profession by my family, I would have been involved in social work, I could have been a lawyer or a doctor.

The movies bore me to death. Early in the morning you got up, they picked you up at 5:00, 6:00 and 10:00, you were ready, at 11:15, the director finally showed up. Don't waste your time like this. I don't regret the movie theater. In the theater, your audience is in front.

I got the threats when I was a senator. Terrible phrases, like smashing all your teeth in a stick. I was very afraid, also because I was seventy-seven years old and I would return home alone at three o'clock in the night, after hours of meeting at the Budget Committee (May God have it in glory).

If I were to be born again, but I really hope that doesn't happen to me, I wouldn't change a single comma of what I've done in my life. With Dario I lived an overly happy life, the only thing that bothered me at first was the unpleasant feeling of being transparent, especially with other women. How many times the famous feminists, leaders of the movement, who know everything, who speak to you from the top down, arrived in the Palazzina Liberty. They would come in, maybe see that I was cleaning the big club with a broom (unusual role for a first actress), it is not that they noticed it and said at least: "Hello". No, they were just asking me, "Where's Dario?" The woman's worst enemy is the woman. There is no comedy where he did not repeat the concept.

When he took the Nobel Prize, Marta Marzotto sent me a basket with five hundred red roses. The accompanying note said: "Behind a great man there is always a great woman." I called her on the phone to thank her, but I also told her: "Marta, don't put me behind you, it makes me impression, it's like I'm running away to keep his pace. Put me by the side. I'd be better off. It's strange that an intelligent and funny woman like you has fallen into such banality.

Peter contacted me to ask if I wanted to run for national elections in 2006. I had so many commitments, so many things to do, it seemed crazy to me, and I told her. In that Senate I made the unimaginable.
Of course, you supported the divorce and abortion campaigns. Let's joking, divorce and abortion. Sure, we've made all kinds of things.

I never studied the art of acting, and when I hear the art of acting, the term already makes me nervous. I was born into a family of actors. I went on stage eight days in my mom's arms. That night I didn't talk so much... [...] We were acting on the subject, following the towel. The traditional actors, when they came into our company, were forced to be very careful, someone already at the rehearsal was saying: "Excuse me but I have always recited following a written text. With you, I never know when it's my turn to say my line. I can't do it. Too bad, if they stayed with us, they would have had an experience that could change his life.

The exploitation was everywhere, even within the family. Feminism, if understood in its true meaning, is a very high concept, of an inestimable value. The so-called feminists, leaders of the time, addressed themselves to modest women, to workers, with a high-sounding and senseless language, imbued with ideologies. I, on the other hand, tried to implement my conception of feminism by bringing our shows to the factories. After every performance, such as for example, tie me up that I break everything the same, it was discussed, and the worker understood. He understood, if you used the right language, otherwise it was like making a hole in the water.

The actors I know do something bizarre: before going on stage, they touch my hands, because they are so excited that their hands are freezing, or sweaty. An actor would never confess it, but that's exactly what it is. They can't explain why this doesn't happen to me. I never have sweaty hands, I never have cold hands, because this is my job. It may seem a little rough, but it's as if the salami maker, slicking the ham, felt a great emotion. He has done so many times, he was born by cutting the ham. And I was born in the theater. That's where I accumulated my luggage, which then allowed me to play all the roles.

It happens to me like this: as I see a person, after seven minutes I understand whether or not he will screw me. As well as, when I read a theatrical text, I immediately feel where the error lies. Even on my scripts I write: "Laughter, here there must be a laugh." And the laugh comes. It'll be DNA. I must have spent my life acting. I was already acting in my mother's belly.

With the nut job, we made a huge mistake. On the other hand, we did not have the means to do better. We should've picked him up and shot the scenes again, missed the rhythm.
