They put themselves in six to write The unscrewed (maybe a little too many), with poor Dario [Fo] trying to intervene but was silenced: "This is not theater, we are making a movie." Dario and I didn't have the strength to pick it up or do the scenes again.

I do not hold a grudge against Darius [Fo], because at the base there is my greatest defect: that of having no ambition. It's not like Dario ever thought, "Franca gives me ideas and I'm a smart guy!" No, it didn't really come to his mind. If I hadn't been his wife, if I had been someone else, he would have, of course, had to put my name as an author. Einaudi has published several texts by Dario edited by me and initially did not even mention the words "edited" by Franca Rame. When I asked Roberto Cerati for the explanation of this, he replied: "We hadn't thought about it." The trouble is that I'm the wife, and the wife, sometimes, is a home furniture, a painting on the wall, something that's close to you but you don't see. I know hundreds of women's outbursts on the subject. If I were to be born again, but I really hope that doesn't happen to me, I wouldn't change a single comma of what I've done in my life. With Dario I lived an overly happy life, the only thing that bothered me at first was the unpleasant feeling of being transparent, especially with other women. How many times the famous feminists, leaders of the movement, who know everything, who speak to you from the top down, arrived in the Palazzina Liberty. They would come in, maybe see that I was cleaning the big club with a broom (unusual role for a first actress), it is not that they noticed it and said at least: "Hello". No, they were just asking me, "Where's Dario?" The woman's worst enemy is the woman. There's no comedy where he didn't repeat the concept.

When I was seven or eight, I promised to become a man. The female world didn't seem as attractive, free and fun as the male world. Apart from Pia, the only woman I wanted to be. I never forgave my father that I had only pulled my brother out of school the day the Soviets announced the success of a man's first mission in space. My brother, not me and my sister, too! My father, excited and moved, had rushed to our school to announce the historic event to my brother and his companions. Then he took Roberto home with him, unable to even imagine spending that day without his son. My sister and I were left in class to finish our daily routine. We were girls. I felt anger and rage. I didn't want to be a girl. I wanted to be a boy.

With this book I intend to answer the many questions that are always asked to me and to which I never know how to reply. But to whom do you ask me "how can you keep your private life separate from your professional life?"I know what to say. I won't separate them. In fact, I don't separate anything. I don't even part with anyone. Everything for me gets intertwined and stirs like a single soup. Not only do I not separate my private life from public life, but my two professions also mix: model and actress are the same job for me. I cannot separate my childhood from adulthood and, worse still, the living from the dead.

My daughter has the same problems with me as I have with my parents. My photos appeared in the massive Lacôme advertising campaign around the world for fourteen years in a row. When Elettra was born, my image was already in magazines, TV, posters and billboards. One day, at kindergarten, the teacher taught children what to do in case they were lost: to look for a policeman, to memorize their address and telephone number, and other solutions like that. Then he began to question the children. He asked my daughter, "What would you do if you got lost at an airport?" and she: "I'd look for the billboard with my mom and wait for help under there." In fact, Elettra thought that all people in advertising were mothers and fathers who were exposed everywhere, in airports, roads and highways, in the event that their children would get lost and need help.

When we were kids (there were seven of us), one of our favorite games was to jump on Dad. He, lying on his side, made the sow and we the pigs. My father was always sorry he couldn't breastfeed us, although at some point I thought he was pregnant. I had come to this conviction because I misunderstand the confusing explanations about the birth of the children of my babysitter, and also because of certain scandalous articles about my family. The articles spoke of the divorce of my parents and of a mysterious and exotic woman (only later I realized that it was my stepmother, Sonali, considered exotic, I imagine, because she comes from Calcutta) and a child "extramarital." This word, extramarital, made me think of extraterrestrials. Maybe that's why the press cared so much about us. "Extramarital" also reminded me of paranormal phenomena, and my father had a big belly, just like a nine-month pregnant woman. That's how I liked it, fat or sturdy. The people who advised him diets irritated me "I didn't want to lose even a little bit of my daddy. It was soft to hug, it was a lot, and I wanted it all.

You're right. I apologize if I appeared bored or annoyed and if I was abrupt with all those who pointed it out. I really look like Ingrid Bergman. Really. So... what do I do? What do you want from me? Having admitted the resemblance, I have the impression that you expect me to do something, but what? You want me to say something? What? As a daughter to them, I could look like my mom or dad, I think I should say I was lucky to have taken it from her. And... tell me, isn't it normal to look like a parent? You want me to agree with that woman who wrote to Vogue, after reading an article about me: "Isabella Rossellini has the presumption that her career is the result of her own talent, rather than of her extraordinary resemblance to her mother." Ow! That kind of claim hurts! But is that all? Am I just the rough copy of Ingrid Bergman? Some kind of imposture?

The objects that decorate my apartment have a history, they're not just there for beauty. There is the miniature of the market of Naples, which makes me think of a city that I love so much but that I rarely see. Around my sink, there are stones each from a significant place for me. One is Dannholmen, the Swedish island where we spent every summer holiday with Mother and where she wanted her ashes to be scattered. There is a black stone of Stromboli volcanic, where my parents met, fell in love and shot the movie with that title. There is the sampietrino of Rome, which reminds me of my hometown, and the movement of 1968, when the sampietrini were launched against the police. [...] On the bedside table next to my bed there are Madonnas, Christs, Vishnus, crucifixes, devils, fiery souls, horns and Buddha: all together, perhaps one protects me more than another, and also to celebrate the coexistence of religions. There is the "bee board", the "pannello d'api", which David Lynch made for me baptizing to one by one the bees that had found death near a strange end: Hank, Bob, Dave... etc., a tribute to my love for animals and his "chicken kit", chicken kit, with instructions on how to put together a butchered chicken, like those that you buy at the supermarket.

My mother, on the other hand, had great physical energy. She walked fast and when she wasn't busy filming a movie, she cleaned and reordered the house meticulously. Mom loved acting and cleaning. Acting more than anything else in the world. It took me a while to resign myself. I wish I could have been the first. When she asked Mom what was most important to her, she blushed and became nervous, but not knowing how to lie, nor be diplomatic, she would reply, although I knew that, for our sake, she would want to be able to answer my family. Once in his dressing room, he said to me, "If for any reason I couldn't be an actress, I would still work for the theater. I would do, I know, the seamstress, or any other profession that would allow me to work in the theater. This was the only time I heard my mother mention an alternative to acting, in case she didn't get lucky as an actress, but obviously this risk never existed, so her career was extraordinary from the beginning. The alternative of seamstress makes the idea of how few times Mother has taken into consideration the concrete alternatives to acting. There is this saying among the American cowboys: "I want to die with my boots on," I want to die wearing boots, that is, on horseback, still young and strong, pierced by a bullet, rather than getting old. Mom used to say that a lot. He was worried about not being able to work until the last moment of his life. She was frightened of losing her health, energy and, consequently, of not being able to act. To the question "what is the most important requirement for an actress?" he always replied: "Good health". To the question "what is the most important requirement for a happy life?" he replied: "Good health and little memory".

My father [Roberto Rossellini ed.] He died, and four years later my mother died [Ingrid Bergman ed.] Dad died of a heart attack, all of a sudden. As my mother said in a letter to Jean and Dido Renoir, he died quickly, just as her Ferrari drove. My mother died slowly of cancer, but with her boots on (with boots on) as she said and as she had hoped. [...] When Dad died, I couldn't imagine life without him. [...] I was still too young to know the power of fantasy, dreams and memories. If I were to divide my life in the same way as the story is divided between B.C. and A.D., I would choose as the dividing date on June 3, 1977.

Don't think it was my family's frequent divorces, homosexuality, extramarital relations that made me disrespectful of traditions. My pets were the main culprits. Living with dogs, cats, rabbits, birds, pigs, you see of all colors. Animals force against the most secret and dark aspects of nature: homosexuality, intersections between different species and even relationships with inanimate objects, such as my home furniture. Spanky, my little pig, was trying to mate with the living room furniture every night. In the morning when I woke up I had to put everything back in place: the sofa in front of the fireplace, the chairs around the table, the pillows on the sofa, etc. As a child, I picked up every stray I could find on the street and kept it with me in the bedroom. Once, entering the chamber I found myself in front of a strange vision: the bed, the curtains, the carpet, everything was frying, a bit like the AlkaSeltzer in the water. It seemed to me a cheerful vision, until I realized that this effect was given by the fleas of the four dogs I had picked up on the street. My dad said he'd send me to boarding school if I kept bringing home stray dogs. I kept doing it anyway. He never sent me to boarding school.

In one of his self-portraits, Frida Kahlo, she drew her husband Diego Rivera in the middle of the forehead, where she is believed to have the third eye. I guess a mind x-ray machine would photograph thoughts like that. If there were such a machine, we would find many images that unite us all. I believe that is what is called the collective imagination. By image I mean a synthesis that condenses into a frame, common to all, an entire event.

With my mother still round, intact and inhumane, but reassuring, with her big breast and her tall and clear forehead without a wrinkle and with the quiet rhythm of that train that sang within me of boundless freedom (vibrant roads, whipped by the wind of smelly libeccio of iron and salt), protected by the dress of black silk, made to crochet by her, with that cry "La mala Pasqua a te" which, after repeating it, tasted, chewed up to understand the most hidden flavor, slept in me buried by winters and winters and that now began to wake up: "It had to be like the glass cry of the cock hurled against the air of the morning and falling crumbled down (breaked in so many small shiny pieces, throbbing of frost): "I was waiting for the reduced of the small theatre in via della Croce, the Eleonora Duse theatre lighted that day in Santa Cecilia, lost in those fable names, waiting for you to be beaten by the shiny, bright, bright, untied by the moments from the little theatre on the turn. From morning to eight, an endless row had paraded in front of me swallowed by the darkness of a door wide open and mute. Certainly in that room there was the stage, a stage of the continent, Accademia di Santa Cecilia, Via della Croce. The theater Eleonora Duse. The Royal Academy of Dramatic Art, director Silvio D'Amico, a great theatrical critic of the continent, Mrs. Carini had been a great actress. I was waiting. That hole swallowed young men and girls who walked in to succeed with a low head or in tears or bravados for a few words or reassuring look of the commission of tests. A girl came out running and tearing her hair: she passed by me screaming. I wasn't allowed. Failed! Failed! I failed, I know! I'm gonna kill myself! I'm gonna kill myself! I'm gonna kill myself! It's up to you, to dreamer! Oi, I say to you, dead of sleep: wake up that is the hour. Hey, what are you deaf? Don't you feel it's your turn? Don't mind that crazy woman. She's been showing up for four years, and she does these scenes every time. I say, with the practice he has done all these years, we should be accustomed to flunking it. Failed, crazy maybe. I cried with the voice of that mad woman: "The evil Easter to you!" My knees were sore on which, in the cry, I threw myself and my chest as torn. Did I yell too loud? The hands that raised me up now were many and warm, reassuring. Did they say good? Or did they say talent? Sure, the diction is scary, the accent is crazy but temperament... temperament. With my knees and broken chest I had been admitted to the Royal Academy of Dramatic Art with the scholarship.

Ah! Yes, I took the exam at the academy. Yeah, I had a fever at 40 that morning. It was so cold in Rome. I was allowed the same, with the scholarship, yes, but I felt blood quailing in ice lumps. I didn't have to talk: I still didn't know if the bag is open or closed. I don't need to talk. You can see that I can only say automatically: that the words of that short scene... (already! the old crazy fish of O'Neill). If he finds out, he'll come back to his decision to confirm my scholarship, and he'll take it from me. He's in charge here. I'll be rude, but I don't have to open my mouth. Well, well, little girl, you've done miracles. That's incredible! Bravo, the bag is confirmed. Well, aren't you saying anything? You can be happy... "The master was silent, with one eye he stared at me and with the other he spied among the faces in silence, waiting respectfully behind the tables. He was the master, and he could see how they looked at him, and he had an eye for everything. The masters have a hundred eyes. He was waiting for an answer, but I couldn't thank him. Is the "I'm happy" open or closed? I might say I'm excited, but were the emotions closed or open? I don't have to open my mouth.

I went alone to a military hospital [...] A boy, operated for a serious wound [...], was semi-conscious but he still found the strength to thank me for the visit [...] The honor was mine for having had a chance to give a few minutes of serenity to him who had been at the front to defend my own homeland. The doctors [...] said that there were little hope of healing. The next morning I phoned for news and the doctor said to me: "Ma'am, a miracle happened, the boy is improving." [...] in another hospital, again in Rome, [...] I saw a submersibleist completely paralyzed. [...] I met a boy who had both legs amputated and after a few weeks he came to meet me walking happily. They had applied two artificial limbs to him.

[With Amedeo Nazzari] I always got along, we never quarreled. We were a couple that the audience loved very much. [...] Amidei, the screenwriter, did everything to make this couple become such even in life. I was undoubtedly very attracted to this fascinating man, with an overwhelming character, endowed with great generosity and sweetness. An actor loved by millions of people both for the winning roles that saw him protagonist in the movies and for this so communicative smile of his.

My first stages were amateurs [...] I was nine years old. I started [...] [at] the Teatro dei Fanciulli [...] we recited fairy tales for children. One of them was The Cat in Boots. Author of the fairy tale Vittorio Metz. [...] In the cast there was also Marisa Merlini and Claretta Petacci's sister, Miriam, who later wanted to embark on the film career but with little success. [...] We were doing some tours in Lazio [...] with this small company of children. [...] By now the role of protagonist had become mine. We recited fairy tales [...] [that] were the ancestors of modern musicals. We were acting, singing and dancing.... That's funny, that's funny!

On September 8, 1943 we were going by train to Aqui. [...] In full Maremma Tuscany the iron train: a plane was machine-gunning us. [...] We held hands, saying the act of sorrow, and turned to the Lord. [...] We saw part of our fleet passing by. It was the Navy ships that were leaving the Germans to join the allies. We arrived in Aqui to buy a piece of land. [...] We should have [...] returned to the capital the next day. [...] Twenty-one months passed before they resumed the way back. [...] It was the worst time of my life. [...] The change of life that we experienced was frightening.  There was no electric light, the bathroom was an outdoor barracks made of reeds, we had very little money and the Piedmont cold [...] penetrated the bones. [...] The coexistence with my in-laws [...] was not idyllic. [...] I had no news of my family and this gives me tremendous anxiety [...].It was only during this period that I became aware of the persecution of the anti-Semitic. [...] I lived in a golden world that perhaps obscured what was happening terrible in Italy.

At the age of sixteen I was enrolled among the Little and then Young Italians. We had a uniform with a white shirt, a wide skirt under our knees and a black tie. The activities of the Fascist organizations [...] thrilled us all.  [...] To become foreman I took a one-month course in Caprarola [...]. It was some kind of strict boarding school. To go out alone was forbidden... an idea came to me: we took sheets and knotted them and went down. [...] From time to time we made parades with the uniforms in Via dei Fori Imperiali passed in the magazine by the Duce. Once we were at the Mussolini Forum and he passed before us girls. Arriving in front of me, he caressed me. I felt a great emotion: a caress of the Duce. His great charisma had conquered all of us young people. A few years later, during the war, I learned that his favorite films were those of Greta Garbo and mine, and I was very proud of it.

I knew very well that something serious had happened. He [the husband] very politely, sensitively and tactfully told me that Mom was in the hospital [...] did not dare to tell me that she was dead. [...] I slowly understood the truth. It was all for me, Mother, friend, confidant. For the first days I lost the compass of the intellect. [...] that loss was the greatest pain of my young life.

And finally came my lucky day. [...] is preparing [...] Unjustified absence whose protagonists were Alida Valli and Amedeo Nazzari. A very important role was vacant, that of the schoolmate of Valli, called Baccalà, a girl who had the devil in her body. Amidei did everything to give me that part [...] The organizers were not very convinced of my abilities, [...] they decided to give me their trust and called me to make the contract. The contract operation was a bit enlivened because the scripture officer did not behave as a gentleman. [...] He made me a very heavy advance. One of my solemn slaps solved the problem. I could do this because my family was wealthy, but if it had happened to a poor girl in need of work?

I introduced myself to the door of Cinecittà, guarded by the fierce goalkeeper Pappalardo who, with extreme severity and hardness, barred the passage to strangers. But my stubbornness, combined with disarming smiles, conquered the cerberus goalkeeper and I was able to enter for the first time in the fairy world of cinema: my greatest dream came true! Walking along the avenues of the world of celluloid, meeting known actors here and there, seeing the sets where the movies were filmed, I was very excited, but despite my excitement I felt safe and already belonging to this world so much desired [...] I felt like "Alice in the land of wonders".

My fortune began with the meeting [...] [with] Sergio Amidei. [...] In the hour of the break, I entered the restaurant of Cinecittà and saw at a table Amidei in the company of the [Elsa] Merlini who asked him: "Who is that girl?" and Amidei answered with his sarcasm: "His name is Lilia Silvi and it is said that he will take your place." The actress' reaction was instinctive: she got up from the table and threw bread crumbs in my direction.

[In 1941] We went my husband and I to the Vatican to ask for a private audience, but we were denied it. We turned to a politician who received us from the pope along with a small group of people. When His Holiness appeared on the door of the hall [...] it was a wonderful vision. In his white dress he seemed like a celestial apparition. [...] Pius XII said, "You wanted to speak to me, come." [...] We knelt down but the Holy Father helped us to get back up began to bless us and asked what we wanted. I told him that I was an actress and that because of my work sometimes I could not go to Mass and, like me, many other people of the show as to Cinecittà there were no churches. He said with great delicacy: "In these very painful times, I am a little busy, leave a note to the cardinal." The church was built perhaps after a year near Cinecittà, but probably now there is no more.

This comedy [Scampolo] wrote by Dario Niccodemi for the great actress Dina Galli. [...] As soon as I read the script I immediately entered the character of the exile scugnizzo. [...] Certainly it is the most beloved film [...] and that gave me great satisfaction. [...] [At a worldly evening in Switzerland, at a festival where Scampolo appeared] Fosco Giachetti [...] approached our table and [...] asked me to dance. From under the table I immediately got a perital kick, delicate but persuasive, which made me realize that I should not have accepted: "Thank you but I can not dance" (Translated with Google Translate) How much it cost me to say this sentence! [...] That evening when the jury gave the name of the award-winning film, I was not in the skin. My Scampolo had won. What a great joy.

I give myself to gardening, even if I don't really have the green thumb [...] The TV is almost always on to hear a voice, a presence, a company. But what most helps me is the faith that has steel roots and has never abandoned me. You will say: "But where is the spirit and the joy of Scampolo?" He will return... [...] I came to the end. It is certainly not Hemingway's pen that filled these pages, but an old actress who wanted to tell the story of her life with all her heart and with great simplicity.

Before we started filming the film, there was a beautiful party at the Hotel De Russie in via del Babbuino. So I saw Amedeo Nazzari for the first time. He invited me to dance. I wore a delicious white dress with slight shades of pink and blue, dressed bought, for the important occasion, in via Condotti da Schubert, the tailor of the divas, and that had cost a real capital! During the dance I felt in heaven in Nazzari's arms. I couldn't believe something so extraordinary was happening to me! Nazzari was the number one actor of the moment.
We started shooting [...] from the first shot I immediately felt in the character the camera did not impress me at all. The film was released in 1939 and had a huge success.

[On tour with ] Carlo Campanini, Franco Scandurra [...] and Sandra Mondaini [...] arrived in Foggia I wanted to go to San Giovanni Rotondo to see a friar who was talked a lot about, Padre Pio. [...] Campanini, the spiritual son of his father, accompanied us to this extraordinary man. [...] I too became his spiritual daughter and with Campanini we made half of a glove that Padre Pio used to cover the stigmata. Bell towers and I used to recite the rosary every day.

With Roberto Villa I filmed [...] The vispa Teresa and Violette in the hair [...] was a great friend of mine [...] graduated in medicine and a gentleman in every aspect. [...] After I stopped working, my husband and I continued to attend him [...] He was a passionate Lazio fan. He also wrote the story of his heart team. There were dozens of books. I didn't read them, because his colors were in contrast to my Giallorossi, but I'm sure they were perfect. Always discreet, polite [...] closed with the cinema, it was an excellent dubber. [...] The cast of La vispa Teresa was made up of Gandusio, Scotti, and the then wife of De' Sica, Giuditta Rissone, and of course also Roberto Villa. The plot of the film was very simple: a girl lives with two other companions. Their finances are scarce, but there is great harmony [...] One of the two, classical dancer, played by Isarema Dilian, gets pregnant. [...] he entrusts his child to me. [...] Luckily in the meantime I know a rich boy, played by Roberto Villa. We fall in love and everything ends in general happiness.

With these two directors [Neufeld and Malasuma] I had a great time. Their characters were diametrically opposite. The German was jovial, cheerful, even if Italian didn't know much about it [...] Malasumma, while being Neapolitan, was closed and professionally demanding [...] we actors talking with him used the "lei" very rare thing in our environment. [...] The most absolute silence reigned in the study.

The film was to be shot in two versions: Italian and Spanish. Before starting the filming, Minerva Film [...] sent for me to ask if I was speaking Spanish. [...] I was in front of [...] one of the producers of Minerva Film who [...] spoke perfectly Spanish. He began to speak the language of the bullfighters to me absolutely incomprehensible. What to do? The contract depended on this examination.  [...] A moment of panic. It was in the middle of my future. Lightning of genius. I staged a faint. [...] They: "Don't worry [...]" [...] The next morning they called me for the signing of the contract. There was no more talk of Spanish. [...] As soon as I started filming the film I said: "I don't speak a word of Spanish" you can imagine the dismay, and not only, of all production.

One night they came to fascists [...] they feared that they would discover the three Jews we hosted. [...] There was a young man who saw us and said, "Oh! Mrs. Silvi and Mr. Scarabello. [...] was the messenger of Minerva Film [...] for that time it went well.

Almost at the end of the movie we would have to deliver the stage for which the production recommended us to get to the final as soon as possible because otherwise he would have had to pay a heavy penalty. The last day we entered at seven and we went out at four o'clock in the morning, all of us, actors, directors, operators and workers, were exhausted but happy to have finished the film in time. [...] the entire cinematic world of 1940 did not care for fatigue and discomfort but did its duty to the end.

After just six months of engagement [...] on August 28, 1940 we were married. It was the wedding of the year because we were two famous characters, in fact my husband, in addition to having made the Serie A championships, was also gold medal at the Berlin Olympics in 1936. [...] A great crowd was waiting for us outside the church. To see their kindnesses, they have been waiting for about an hour. [...] For cinematic needs, my hair was blond. Gigi wanted me for the ceremony with my natural color, black raven. My simple, white and long dress, I think, had been packaged by the Fontana sisters. [...] A few days before the wedding, Nazzari called me and asked me if I would prefer, as a wedding gift, a refrigerator, a radio or a jewel. [...] I didn't have my home yet, so I immediately opted for a jewel. [...] he asked me how many movies I had made until then. I honestly said five.  [...] I got home a bracelet with five [...] rows of tears. Nazzari's generosity was such that if I had said that the movies [...] had been thirty, I would have arrived a bracelet that would have stood as a sleeve of an evening dress. This gift is one of my dearest jewels and every time I wear it in my heart there is always a tender and affectionate memory for Amedeo.

After Mom's death inside my heart, despair and pain reigned. Mimmo [the husband], having seen my state, organized a theatrical tour for me. The work helped me a lot. [...] One of the comedies we represented was Scampolo.
I was poisoned by anger and faced with his words of apology, I reacted by giving her a solemn slap. This was and is my impulsive top-of-the-line. I was such a pain in the ass!

Amedeo [Nazzari], with his well-known generosity, offered him to return to Genoa his new and splendid cabriolé Alfa Romeo red with the inside of white skin. Gigi obviously didn't accept it because to him, who came from Genoa, such an extraordinary loan made him dismayed. [...] However, the sparingness absorbed by Giggi has served me not [...] to waste in useless expenses. If [...] had not immediately taken over the economic reins of the family, now I would probably be a guest of Termini station, lying on a sheet of the "Time" or "Corriere" sheet.

The welcome of the relatives was very affectionate. My mother-in-law tried to be kind because she hadn't accepted me at all. My profession was not to your liking [...] it was easy to understand how difficult it was for her to accept a daughter-in-law who was an actress. The reputation of my environment was certainly not good [...] I want to [...] [break] a spear in favor [...] of our environment. I am sure that in the area of work, of any kind, [...] there will be betrayals and stories [...] outside the family environment. The difference is that the employee, the peasant, the manager [...] can go unnoticed if she goes to the restaurant or enters a hotel with her momentary love, while an actress, known to all, can not afford this luxury without being noticed [...] the next day there will always be in a newspaper: "The new love of..."

The contact with the audience outside the set was quite different from today's. [...] Then it was rare to see an actor from close up, we were a bit of myths, of the true divines. One day [...] in Cinecittà [...] [a little girl came to know me]. I took her by the hand and with such tenderness I led her around the studios, presenting her many actors her idols that she had seen in the films [...] she looked to the right and left with a charm full of amazement for this fairy tale that she was living, almost reminded me of myself just a few years before... Finished turning the door to my home. [...] The next morning it was I who was amazed at the thing that happened during the night: she had become a lady, the first cycle had come to her. Fortunately you were aware that a round would happen, but it was still a great emotion for both of us.

Knocking on all doors with insistence, I managed to do a small part as a gas station in the movie Villa paradiso, perhaps filmed in 1937. In the same year I made another appearance in the film Il Signor Max with the great Vittorio De Sica. I played a florist and I said only one joke: "These are orchids that come directly from Bermude". After so many years I still remember her for the emotion she gave me to say it. Having recited with Vittorio De Sica filled my heart with joy. This great actor was extraordinary. [...] hearing him speak was a music full of spontaneity and professionalism.

The war was inside each of us. The nightmare of the bombardments, the darkening, the headlights of the masked machines to hide the lights and the future of tomorrow in the hands of God. The entertainment was limited. In restaurants, meat was served under vegetables, because it was only allowed to be eaten twice a week. There was an annoying paper so it was called the card that allowed to eat the doses of food granted by the state.

[...] Once while the partisans were at home, we were warned of the arrival of the Germans. [...] In front of us were the guns ready to shoot us if they had found the partisan. We were terrified [...] they didn't shoot and for us it was just so much fear, but at the same time we were all satisfied for having silenced the truth. [...] There was a request, which was much more like an order, made by the partisans to my husband: considering his law studies, they asked him to become president of their Tribunal. [...] accepted the assignment. He thought that with his honesty and impartiality he could perhaps do things with justice to save, on both sides, some innocent soul.

The relationship between Mom and Dad at some point cracked [...] the jealousy peeped. Mom had doubts about Dad's loyalty. The worry encouraged her fantasy: she disguised herself as a child with a bloom on her head, short socks and mixed herself with the girls present in the garden where she was supposed to meet her so feared. As he went unnoticed, he followed Dad. They were all fantasies born of his jealousy. Things that happen to people who love each other. On December 23, 1921, in Rome, at 5 a.m. I was born. [...] My coming to the world was received with great joy by the whole family. I was the queen of the house.

I swung one evening in the summer, suspended between thoughts seemingly without logic. I remember my scent marrying beautifully with the night scent. In my mind, ideas, memories, words, events were uncompromising. I let fragments of my life emerge freely, parentheses also chosen by the imagination: emotions. Pushing loud and messy, all of a sudden they entered the woods that I dress characters, facts, fairies, inventions and lies: those that I carry inside usually appeared to me surrounding me... It was so that I thought to welcome them "From me" all together for a night...

For me he had written a beautiful script [...] Just at that time I was shooting a film in Italy The Parmigiana by Antonio Pietrangeli; he knew very well because he had signed the contract. One night he phones me and tells me "As soon as you're done you have to come to Paris immediately to start Germinal. "Dad" is not possible. "But as it is not possible, I signed. "You hurt Dad because I'm pregnant."

He needed men at his feet, men in love lost. He wanted with all his strength that men loved her. To make a man fall in love or be courted by him, pleasure, to be looked at carefully, to feel the desire as a perfume was his greatest enjoyment. [...] Hers was a request for absolute love, as in fairy tales, which she translated only with the body, fulfilling with admiration and gaze, the mad love that she nourished for herself. [...] He was playing to seduce our boyfriends. It was the means to prove to herself that she still liked her, that she was desirable and beautiful more than we girls. Then I didn't understand. I was just disconcerted by his behavior. I realized much later that it wasn't mean. He did not realize the insecurity in which his attitude plunged us.

Teeth have something to do with bones, too. [...] After all, smiling is, in many cases, synonymous with a certain ambiguity. [...] Laughing is a formidable expression, liberating, revealing of the innermost; a spasm torn from abandonment. [...] The exploration of the palate with fingers is an experience full of tenderness and sensuality. Eroticism cannot be taught. [...] True eroticism is its own act, unpredictable and spontaneous, which is born intact by the absolute drive. [...] To return to the erotic, sensual bone, to the secret language of the bone structure, an intimate vocabulary must be elaborated. I would recommend to everyone, including those who have the privilege of being able to show off healthy teeth, to smile sparingly.

Strangely I never feel alone, I think I have invisible people next to me, those commonly called angels; I often feel their presence, even someone or something touching me. I am convinced that I can be close with the thought of some distant people and that, if these people were a little less material, they would certainly feel me too. There are particular places where I can feel presences near me. Sometimes I even turn so strongly the feeling that I perceive clearly. I sense someone's looking at me, touching me, knowing me. The idea that I have of God resembles a gigantic wave that produces a strong vibration, divided into other vibrations more or less strong. For me the essence of life is an intense light, harmonious sounds that compose a heartbreaking melody. I feel that life can be translated into different forms of vibration. Sometimes round visualizations, sometimes angular depending on diseases, impediments, fears, emotions; even our thoughts translate into more or less powerful waves and vibrations; they are recorded and belong forever to collective memory; they will exist forever.

I wish I had a big room, a room that I don't see, that I'll never have to see and that no one will ever have to see. [...] is the seat of voluptuousness. The place of games, of eroticism without bridles. [...] I want my eyes closed on a veil of fresh and light cloth. [...] I will know that there are others near, men and women; also they in absolute inability to see. Just hear us out, breathe us out. [...] Then, with hands, feel others; touch one arm, with one foot touch another foot. Understand if I hear a man or a woman, but I absolutely don't know who she is. Imagine how she might be, or how I wish she was, and decide whether or not to continue the approach depending on the elements I can pick up. [...] Waiting shall not know of sin, nor of forbidden. Only a game that is born to become the altar of secret desire. [...] And you laugh, you play, you dream. You overcome perfectly the embarrassment, the modesty. I'm gonna rip the brake inhibitors apart. They're skipping taboos! All prohibitions disappear. [...] The idea of having felt wonderful sensations, of having made love in a wonderful way with a stranger, exalts me.

I read Lolita of Nabokov secretly; I had taken it from my father [...] I had to be twelve or thirteen years old; I was living the last months at home; I was about to leave, but I didn't know it yet: I only intuited it at certain times. Lolita was the revelation of desire; the awareness of my femininity. While I was reading, secretly, I did not feel Lolita, but the mature man victim of seduction. On second thought years later, I found perceptions that hit me watching Bardot movies. In...Et Dieu créa la femme and others she aped very well the seduction, proposed a carnal image, which was certainly not the true, or the only image of herself. He worked to represent the idea that man had made himself a feminine seduction. She offered what men wanted to find, what they should like. It seemed to me violence done to herself; it hurt me for her; it was understood that she was not, that "female" or, however, not only that. I felt her out of position, wounded in order to be able to transform herself into a magnet, emptied also to be able to arouse so much desire, hit the mind to capture pure desire. In Lolita I immediately felt the same mechanism. Instinctively I recognized a gene that we women have: what ironically I like to call viruses: a shameful tara, one of those elements of the worst legacy that the female passes to the other female. Silent learning of the gesture, of the gaze, the soft indugiare of the hip, the sucking of the lips with the stupid face of the tongue, without the justification of a soft to be removed. The caricature of the female.

I wish I had a big room, a room that I don't see, that I'll never have to see and that no one will ever have to see. More than a room is a place of memory, dark secret. It is the seat of voluptuousness. The place of games, of eroticism without bridles. It must be, first of all, a game, then, perhaps, it can become something else. I don't know the shapes or the colors of this room; maybe there is, maybe not. It's a non-place. It's a maybe. And I want to keep not seeing, but listening. I want to have my eyes closed on a veil of fresh and light cloth that is also beautiful for those who do not see... I want to be dressed in white and walk barefoot; I do not want to know what I am wearing or what others are wearing, do not even imagine it; I will only feel the transparency with the tip of my fingers and, with this feeling, I will feel good inside. I'm gonna blow something fresh under my feet. I will know that there are other neighbors, men and women; also they in absolute inability to see. Just hear us out, breathe us out. I don't know if this place has windows from which the sun could enter, or if it's completely dark. I think I'd like it better if it was dark, wrapped in a mauve-colored shadow, reassuring. A liquid dark, fresh and restful. The night darkness of a summer night without moon, without crickets. Then, with your hands, feel others: touch one arm, with one foot touch another foot. Understand if I hear a man or a woman, but I absolutely don't know who she is. Imagine how she might be, or how I wish she was, and decide whether or not to continue the approach according to the elements I can pick up. A dry scent of limoncello, the silky grain of the skin, scarely lukewarm fingers, hay breath with short breath... It must be a cheerful tension, without fear of silence. She can't be nervous. Waiting must not know of sin, nor of forbidden. Only a game that is born to become the altar of secret desire.

When the atmosphere at home, at the table with my parents became heavy, I decided not to listen to the words anymore, nor to hear more the hard shot of my mother's lighter smoking at mid-dinner and not even the embarrassing noise of my grandmother's dentures. I wanted to get away from the family with all of myself. Then I went into the picture: it was my game. It consisted of entering the Delvaux bathroom. Going among those women, becoming one of them, becoming, I still a child, the woman in the painting.

Over the years, Mom has been abandoned by all, ruined the existence of relatives and friends with her madness. After so many years of struggle I gave up too, I left so as not to be completely devoured [...] She always acted like she was hurt, skinned. Even when she was young, she was always deeply dissatisfied. She brought herself into the pain and anger of those who think they deserve much more from life and do not resign, unable to ever rejoice in anything or to fight. She felt persecuted by a fate that wanted her to be defeated. It was. He didn't do anything to change it, to redeem himself. He's waited his whole life and he's still waiting for the others to come to her to solve her problems, take care of her. I think she was too taken by herself to dedicate herself to something else; then over the years she discovered herself immature and too fragile to test herself: coming out in the open, finally looking inside and being able to change. Unfortunately, there is no room for her by me; it is too late. I would have liked it; I wanted it intensely. Too bad.

Before they got divorced, they had a lot of fight, and they had a lot of fight about me, too. I was the most interesting in the family in terms of foster care: I was underage and I worked a lot. Success had come suddenly, dragging me and, by the time, I made a lot of money. There have been raging struggles between them. I was entrusted to my mother, but it was my father who signed my contracts and held the money. Shortly after custody my mother said to me: "If you want to be with me in Paris, you have to help me economically. "At the time she had no financial concern. My father had left her half the apartment we had lived in, and gave her a more than correct monthly. He lived well. I was only seventeen years old and often, I think, I would have the right not to be forced to work. I could have studied; I could have chosen another job. I needed to be protected, not loved. When she said, "If you want to stay, you absolutely have to provide economically," I left. I came to live in Italy. I think the hardest thing I've learned in my life has been to accept that I'm not loved.

I took the bus every morning to go to the new school, this time public. I met Catherine Deneuve at the stop. She's a few years older than me. Women look at each other much more than men, they look at each other in a very primitive, almost animalistic way. Even more at that age when you are not yet a woman, but you are not even girls anymore. As you turn into the future woman, you are perfectly aware of how beautiful you already have physically and of what are or will be our faults. I was looking in all the mirrors. I was looking into other people's eyes. I looked more in the eyes of other women than in the eyes of men. By shyness and instinct; I already knew that the eyes of man are a deforming mirror, even if I was not familiar with the opposite sex. And then I didn't have the boldness that would allow me to look a man in the eye. I was just looking for an answer that selfishly concerned me. I remember very well our examination of each other, as if we had intuited the mutual future, as if we had recognized each other; two persons belonging, although in a very different way, to the same family, bound by a similar destiny. There was dismay in our eyes. It wasn't about who was the tallest, who was the slenderest, who had the biggest eyes, the thickest hair, the thinnest ankles, how you dressed, how you behaved. Everyone studied the way to get on the bus. We didn't go to the same school. We used to spy naively on each other. I always remember the mirror, Eric's mirror. The mirror in the way of being Anthonny, my cousin. I was Catherine's mirror, she was mine, and each one through the other looked at herself. We did not express feelings towards each other, our gaze was only in search of an answer that was about ourselves. Ours was a relationship of anxious, anxious expectation. Everyone was waiting for the other, but not the other for the other; he was waiting for the other for himself. I didn't know her name was Catherine, too.

I have always felt like a mark of great poverty having no ideas, initiatives, loves, passions. I could never live without falling in love with the concrete and the ephemeral. I remember my first love; I went crazy about a blouse. He wore a little boy, an ugly boy; he already had bacon and it was all soft. I hate soft things! And yet she had a blouse of heartbreaking beauty... It was a blouse in pictures and flowers, green, blue and turquoise, with something of an imperceptible rose. I was chasing the blouse like Merlin the enchanter the stone of the Grail through woods and forests, and I was running wild behind that charming little chubby little monster; I wanted to touch those pictures, to be able to look at that blouse without breath: get lost in its beauty. All the choices of my life have a little print of his blouse. I despair if I don't fall in love with the call every day, an attraction for a perfume, a gesture, a look, a smile, a foot, a book, a new music, a particular light...

About thirteen, fourteen years old, always in that villa rented in the summer, my father discovered me while reading an illustrated book on sexuality. The functions of the uterus, ovaries and male were represented and explained: scientific explanations. He, so anti-conformist, made a scene to my mother saying that they were abominable readings and that a little girl of my age didn't have to know those things! Probably if she had caught me with Juliette or the prosperity of the vice of the divine Marquis would have thought: "Well, the girl is proving to have an inclination for the literature that perhaps should be encouraged. "Who knows, I scandalized my curiosity about the physiological functioning of our bodies that appeared to him as a perversion. How contradictory his indignation was to his arrogant chatter with his friends on the left!

From me I would like a jester, a savory and shining dwarf, an antidote to melancholy, an element that breaks the reflection after having provoked it, loosens the knots in turmoil; breaks the magnetic power of uncertainty. I know who I want. I'm thinking of the movie "A Year Lived Dangerously." I would bring this film to me, I would often project it in the evenings of thunderstorm: it wouldn't fade emotions. I don't want to know that he is a woman, I want to think that Linda Hunt is a man. In fact, I like it a lot more to know that it can be both. Well, that character calls me back to the best of me. I could love him, give myself to him. His appearance no longer counts, as powerful as his profound humanity, his passion, his charism: transcends the intranscensible. I would really like to meet him, live the emotion that arouses me in my sleep. Pulling the threads of his Indonesian puppets, shaped shadows, the dwarf whispers: "The strength of light and of darkness in constant struggle... Everything is clouded by desire, possession, like the mirror from dust..."

The next day there were auditions; I returned to the hotel by car with Tognazzi. We walked a kind of freeway with the sand squeezing the asphalt. There was wind. I was cold. After a few kilometers Ugo began to stretch his hand by touching my knee, then he tried a more decisive approach. I immediately put my hand on his to stop him and laughed stupidly. I was very embarrassed. Throwing it on the prank, I said something in French like that. ==References====External links== Je suis trop vieille pour vous.He did not hear me and became more pressing: he even stopped the car and tried to kiss me, to hold me, to touch me. I turned him down with all my strength. I was shocked. I thought, "How does this movie start badly?"

I have long been ill or in a psychological situation of profound disturbance. I haven't eaten in many months. They fed me by flinging me. I couldn't cross the street alone, walk into a store. I'd lost my own life. I was depending on the others. [...] It all began with a vague feeling of nausea, which in time became profound, absolute, terrible anguish; it ended up preventing me from any positive thinking and many movements. [...] I went to the clinic first, had an IV, had a little rest, then went to the theater [it was under contract]. [...] By now I weighed forty-seven pounds; I could not stand. One spring day in Naples I threw away all the useless tranquilizers and entered a pharmacy. Exposed in the window I had seen jars of homogenized, those that give themselves to the children: something inside me in that moment shook. I decided that I would be my own mother, and I started to feed like this.

Over the years, Mother has been abandoned by all and has ruined the existence of relatives and friends with her madness. After so many years of struggle I gave up too, I left so as not to be completely devoured. I took him with me on the set of many films, sometimes even abroad; the scenes were invariably repeated, aggressiveness rose; his devastating unhappiness grew unstoppable. Then I quit. Last year he wanted to be admitted to a nursing home for mental illness. They came to pick her up with an ambulance. They put her out after a month, taking a lot of tests. He's driving the doctors crazy! She was so healthy. The three of us, my sister, she and I, met in Paris. I see the corridors, the bars, the palpable anguish, the faces excavated by terror. It was back in the center of attention, even though the circle in which it moved was terribly bleak. He had an audience again after so many years; we were unexpectedly gathered. We could have found each other, learned with humility to know each other. I hoped. Instead it was atrocious: the old grudges have re-exploded more devastating than before. I was helpless to the deadly consequences of his total immaturity.

I made two films with Vittorio Gassman. When I met him, he was the lover of my friend Annette Strøyberg. As soon as I arrived in Italy I saw the photos taken in via Veneto of the stars of the time on the rotogravure. The sweet life of Fellini under the parasols of Cafè de Paris with Anita Ekberg and many other famous actors assorted in front of glasses of colored liqueurs: they seemed happy. I remember the photographs of Annette and Gassman taken in via Veneto; they were young! Annette had breathtaking feet; she was always wearing shoes with very low heels; she could see the uncle of the first two toes of the foot; it looked like the attachment of the breasts exposed by a generous neckline. I was fascinated by this erotic symbol. When I was talking to her, I never took my eyes off her feet.

My first true love was for a guy in Camargue, starring in the movie Crin blanc. He was a savage, always riding a horse, working in a bull and horse farm. He was elusive and beautiful: a blond gypsy, the first kiss, his, disappointed me. Even today, I don't like kissing on my mouth. I am not convinced of the approach to that intimacy, the way, perhaps too easy, too fast of its achievement. It would be necessary for the mouth to be stripped, to offer half undesired... It would be necessary for the mouth to be always hidden so that the kiss and its nakedness would become a pure gift; an act of secret love, like that other one... Even when I made love the first time I was disappointed. I thought, "It was wrong. This is not the way to do it. "There is a Vanoni song called "It's all here": it tells of a woman who is always surprised, incredulous in the face of the things of life that inevitably disappoint her. There is a mistake, I thought, it cannot be so, in the sense so insignificant, compared to what I had imagined.

Once my sister, in the country, saw me in the kitchen kneading the flour with the yeast to make the bread; she looked at me stunned and with a certain irony she said to me: "What are you doing? You know that in Milan pasta is already ready! I don't think it's a limited case; today, many people think like you; they have completely lost contact with matter, with the origin of things, the dark part of certain existences. There are so many ways to perceive ourselves. Breathe wood, for example, touch it, feel its vibrations or waves living in a room, feel the mood of a place. I often get into some people's homes, smell their lives, have sympathy for an armchair or chair, or don't want to sit on others, without rational explanations. Sensations, distant emotions reappearing. Entering a bathroom and feeling immediately a deep discomfort, as if imprinted in my memory of memories you don't remember an event submerged by other images. Or try a wave of great pleasure, of "rediscovered tana".

I remember when I had my first menstruation; I was thirteen years old, it was summer, at the sea. I wasn't prepared. I knew it this way, but only vaguely. It happened at Villa Santiago on the French Riviera, and there were guests at home, collaborators of my father who worked with him at a script. [...] Suddenly my father said aloud, addressing the group, that it was a feast day because his daughter... had become a woman.... He did not say so kindly, his words were very crude. It seemed to me like an infamous betrayal, like someone who steals your diary or who says, like my sister did to our peers, who put my ovate in my bra... things that make you sink, that burn you forever. In his official announcement at the summer table there was cynicism, contempt for the woman I had since become, with the duty to open my legs... Since then I had officially entered the pack of females to assemble. Something like that isn't said to friends at the table. I understood the curse of being a woman, a woman with her recurring symbol: blood, open sex, wounded, prey. By ridiculing the situation, demanding the blasphemous complicity of strangers, he blamed me, involving me in something dark, which I could not detect at the time. For a long time there was a feeling of filth, of anxiety mixed with danger: something that resurrected the sleeping animal in me.

I made years ago a film in the Canary Islands, a mediocre film with impossible actors. One of them, black, huge, monstrous, very bad, at all costs wanted to get my skin, since I did not deign to accept his attentions; done absolutely inconceivable for him, as for many white actors. He swore to make me pay for it. He rode a horse, he was an all-Italian western. He was always trying to push me with his horse on the steep edges of the Canyons to make me fall. I've been saved more than once by the stuntmen in the movie. I resisted heroically. Luckily, I was doing very well on horseback and I wasn't afraid. That actor had ended up in the United States, on the front page of the black news, for throwing the girlfriend off the fourth floor of a hotel, smashing her. Luckily, the girl didn't die, and he got off easy. It was really terrifying, gigantic, a closet, hateful, arrogant, devoid of any charm. My room was on the 30th floor of the hotel. I was really very small [...] and I don't know why I once slept in the middle of them, in the big bed. [...] In short, I was among them and I must have listened and understood something that scared me very much, pretended to sleep. I remember my mother's cinnamon smell and my father's hard smell. Maybe they said some heinous things. [...] My mother cried all night. [...] My mother's despair was so complete, that her words were not uttered to the end. They were eaten by tears. [...] It seemed so desperate to me that bed, so alone my mother, that perhaps I thought there that I would not get married.

At the age of fifteen, I was overwhelmed by the passion for acting [...] helped me Elvira. She lived upstairs, was educated, smart, and was a stewardess on planes going to America. From me she was considered a bird woman, free, happy and independent. I was waiting for her on the stairs to see her wearing blue with gold buttons and a hat. [...] Inventing an excuse full of fantasy and security, she convinced my mother to let me go out with her. He took me to an elegant living room, where boys, all older than me, read a comedy: The enemy of Niccodemi. Leaving with a sailboat around the world, flying with real wings, wouldn't give me the thrill of that living room, where everyone pretended to be another.

Smells like cinnamon. She had pink, thin, transparent skin. You could see the pale celestial veins. When she was young, she had red hair and green eyes. She was in love with my father, who loved her in a way I never understood. He betrayed, offended, neglected, humiliated. And yet they were always together. [...] She spoke of him as she would, forgetting all the offenses. I could hear her chatting with her friends telling her life with him, ours with her, full of details, unknown details. Nothing had to do with reality, not even the color of my brothers' eyes or hair or mine. He told a life that we hadn't lived, especially in small things.

But, if they left, those objects certainly had a reason. I never had constancy looking for them, after a little look back, stealthy, polite not to offend them, I continued. Sometimes I think about them, their temperature, their color, and I'm sure they're fine where they chose to be. But I also have affectionate, stubborn objects that I'd like to unpack, but they're stuck all my life for no reason. And then I end up loving them. The amount of those I lose, compared to those who remain, is great. [...] It is the things that turn away from me. [...] I fall madly in love with certain things. They understand it, they don't want to be prisoners and they leave. [...] I need to touch them and perhaps they cannot bear to be loved. I feel they have a slight rebellion against me. A need for freedom. They get stolen by the first one that happens, they hide in mysterious places. [...] They told me that I am the one who let them fall [...] I may not have a good grip, that is, I have no strength in my hands. I've never been possessive. I prefer objects to choose me, more than I choose them.

Even my eyes see little and reinvent everything, so I no longer know what is true and what is not. When I close them, together with the facts, I see the colors of Sardinia. The tenacity and smell of the wind that stuns. And on the pink sand, the fragments of coral brought by the waves, which leave light and temporary drawings. The wind there is proud and bizarre. He's always right about everything.
